atomic-structure/atoms-elements-compound.summary: Define atoms, elements and compounds and explain the differences between them.

quiz.q: What is the key difference between a compound and a mixture?
opts: In a compound, elements are chemically bonded in fixed proportions — a mixture is not bonded and can be separated physically.
A compound contains only one type of atom. A mixture contains two or more types.
A mixture is always a liquid. A compound is always a solid.
They are the same — both describe combinations of different substances.

wrong_explanations.1: One type of atom = ELEMENT. Compounds contain two or more elements chemically bonded.
wrong_explanations.2: Mixtures and compounds can be solids, liquids or gases — state does not define them.
wrong_explanations.3: Compounds and mixtures are fundamentally different — chemical bonding is the key distinction.

quiz.q: A student heats a mixture of iron filings and sulfur. A grey solid forms that cannot be separated using a magnet. What has formed?
opts: A compound — iron sulfide — formed by chemical bonding between iron and sulfur
A mixture — the iron and sulfur have blended more thoroughly
An element — the sulfur has been destroyed leaving only iron
A solution — the sulfur has dissolved into the iron

wrong_explanations.1: If it were still a mixture, the iron could be separated magnetically. Since it cannot, a chemical reaction has occurred.
wrong_explanations.2: Elements cannot be destroyed by chemical reactions — only rearranged into compounds.
wrong_explanations.3: Sulfur is a solid at these temperatures — it does not dissolve into iron.

quiz.q: Which equation is correctly balanced?
opts: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O
H₂ + O₂ → H₂O
H₂ + O₂ → H₂O₂
2H₂ + 2O₂ → 2H₂O

wrong_explanations.1: H₂ + O₂ → H₂O: left has 2 O atoms, right has only 1 O atom — not balanced.
wrong_explanations.2: H₂ + O₂ → H₂O₂: this equation is balanced (2H and 2O each side) but produces hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂), not water. The question asks about water formation.
wrong_explanations.3: 2H₂ + 2O₂ → 2H₂O: left has 4 O atoms, right has only 2 O atoms — not balanced.

quiz.q: Which of these is a mixture?
opts: Bronze — copper and tin metals mixed together, not chemically bonded
Water — hydrogen and oxygen in a fixed 2:1 ratio
Sodium chloride — sodium and chlorine in ionic bonds
Carbon dioxide — carbon and oxygen in covalent bonds

wrong_explanations.1: Water is a COMPOUND — hydrogen and oxygen are chemically bonded. Electrolysis is needed to separate them.
wrong_explanations.2: Sodium chloride is a COMPOUND — ionic bonds between Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions.
wrong_explanations.3: Carbon dioxide is a COMPOUND — covalent bonds between C and O atoms.

foundation.common_mistake: A COMPOUND is NOT a mixture. In a compound, elements are CHEMICALLY BONDED — you need a chemical reaction to separate them (e.g. electrolysis to split water into hydrogen and oxygen). In a MIXTURE, substances are just mixed — physical methods separate them. Water is a compound. Saltwater is a mixture.

atomic-structure/mixtures.summary: Describe physical methods for separating mixtures and the principles behind each.

quiz.q: A student wants to obtain pure water from seawater. Which technique should they use?
opts: Simple distillation — heat the seawater and condense the steam
Filtration — filter through paper to remove the salt
Chromatography — water travels faster than dissolved salts
Crystallisation — cool the seawater to crystallise the water

wrong_explanations.1: Filtration only removes INSOLUBLE solids — salt is dissolved in seawater so passes straight through filter paper.
wrong_explanations.2: Chromatography separates dissolved substances by their travel speed — it does not separate water from dissolved salts.
wrong_explanations.3: Crystallisation removes water to leave the SALT — the opposite of what is needed here.

quiz.q: In a chromatography experiment, a spot travels 4.5 cm and the solvent front travels 9.0 cm. What is the Rf value?
opts: 13.5 — adding the two distances
4.5 — using just the distance the spot moved

wrong_explanations.1: Rf = 9.0 ÷ 4.5 = 2.0 — this is dividing the WRONG WAY. Rf is always substance ÷ solvent, and must be between 0 and 1.
wrong_explanations.2: Adding distances has no meaning. Rf = substance distance ÷ solvent distance = 4.5 ÷ 9.0 = 0.50.
wrong_explanations.3: Just the distance alone gives no useful information — Rf is a ratio, which is why it has no units.

quiz.q: Why is fractional distillation used to separate crude oil rather than simple distillation?
opts: Crude oil contains many liquids with different boiling points — fractional distillation separates them into different fractions
Crude oil is a solid — simple distillation cannot be used on solids
The fractions in crude oil are insoluble in each other — filtration would work just as well
Simple distillation uses too much energy for industrial use

wrong_explanations.1: Crude oil is a LIQUID mixture — simple distillation collects one fraction at a time but cannot separate all components simultaneously.
wrong_explanations.2: The fractions IN crude oil mix readily — filtration only works for insoluble particles, not dissolved or miscible liquids.
wrong_explanations.3: Energy cost is relevant industrially, but the primary reason is that crude oil has many components with DIFFERENT boiling points that need to be separated simultaneously.

foundation.common_mistake: Filtration only removes INSOLUBLE solids. You CANNOT filter salt from water — salt is dissolved (soluble). Use evaporation or distillation instead. Also: in distillation you collect the VAPOUR that condenses — the liquid. Crystallisation collects the SOLID that forms as the solution cools.

fifas.label: Rf Value Calculation
fifas.question: In a chromatography experiment, a dye spot moves 6.0 cm. The solvent front moves 9.0 cm. Calculate the Rf value.
steps: Rf = distance moved by substance ÷ distance moved by solvent
No unit conversion needed — Rf has no units

atomic-structure/model-of-the-atom.summary: Describe how the model of the atom developed as new evidence was discovered.

quiz.q: In Rutherford's gold foil experiment, most alpha particles passed straight through the foil. What does this tell us?
opts: Atoms are mostly empty space — most particles found no matter to deflect them
Gold atoms are very large — the particles passed through the gaps between atoms
Alpha particles are too fast to be deflected
The plum pudding model was correct — the positive charge is spread out

wrong_explanations.1: Atomic SIZE explains why particles might miss atoms, not why they pass THROUGH them. The key conclusion is that the atom is mostly empty space internally.
wrong_explanations.2: Alpha particles can be deflected — some WERE deflected at large angles, showing the nucleus exists.
wrong_explanations.3: The plum pudding model predicted only SMALL deflections. The large angle deflections and back-scattering DISPROVED the plum pudding model.

quiz.q: Why did the scientific community accept Rutherford's nuclear model over Thomson's plum pudding model?
opts: The gold foil experiment produced results that could not be explained by the plum pudding model — the new model fitted the evidence
Rutherford was more famous than Thomson, so his model was believed
The plum pudding model was proposed before electrons were discovered
Both models are still equally accepted today

wrong_explanations.1: Scientific acceptance is based on EVIDENCE, not reputation — the gold foil results clearly contradicted the plum pudding model.
wrong_explanations.2: Thomson's plum pudding model was specifically based on and designed to explain the discovery of electrons.
wrong_explanations.3: Rutherford's nuclear model replaced the plum pudding model — it is not still equally accepted, though both are discussed historically.

foundation.common_mistake: In Rutherford's experiment, MOST particles passed STRAIGHT THROUGH — this is what shows atoms are mostly empty space. Only a FEW bounced back. Students often get this backwards and say most particles bounced back. The few that did bounce back were what proved the nucleus exists.

atomic-structure/subatomic-particles.summary: Describe the relative charges and masses of protons, neutrons and electrons and the scale of atoms.

quiz.q: A magnesium atom (Mg, atomic number 12) forms a Mg²⁺ ion. How many electrons does the ion have?
opts: 10 — magnesium loses 2 electrons from its original 12
14 — magnesium gains 2 electrons to form a +2 ion
12 — the number of electrons stays the same
2 — only the outermost electrons remain

wrong_explanations.1: Gaining electrons gives a NEGATIVE charge. Mg²⁺ is POSITIVE, so electrons were LOST.
wrong_explanations.2: Mg²⁺ is formed by losing 2 electrons. Original: 12 electrons. After loss: 12 − 2 = 10 electrons.
wrong_explanations.3: Only the 2 outermost electrons are lost — but the ion still has all 10 remaining electrons, not just 2.

quiz.q: Why does an atom have no overall electrical charge?
opts: The number of protons (+1 each) equals the number of electrons (−1 each) — the charges cancel
Protons and neutrons have equal and opposite charges that cancel
Electrons are on the outside and shield the positive nucleus
The atom vibrates too fast for any charge to accumulate

wrong_explanations.1: Neutrons have ZERO charge — they cannot cancel proton charges. Charge neutrality comes from protons balancing electrons.
wrong_explanations.2: Shielding describes how inner electrons reduce the attractive force on outer electrons — it does not explain why atoms are electrically neutral.
wrong_explanations.3: Vibration does not affect electrical charge — the neutrality is a consequence of proton and electron numbers being equal.

foundation.common_mistake: The number of PROTONS never changes in a chemical reaction — only electrons are gained or lost to form ions. Also: electrons have negligible mass — almost all atomic mass is in the nucleus (protons + neutrons both have relative mass 1). The atom is MOSTLY EMPTY SPACE — the nucleus is 10,000 times smaller than the atom.

atomic-structure/relative-atomic-mass.summary: Define atomic number, mass number, isotopes and relative atomic mass — and calculate Ar from isotope abundances.

quiz.q: Chlorine-35 and chlorine-37 are isotopes. What is the same and what differs between them?
opts: Same: atomic number (17 protons and 17 electrons). Different: mass number — Cl-35 has 18 neutrons, Cl-37 has 20 neutrons.
Same: mass number. Different: atomic number and proton count.
Same: everything — isotopes are identical atoms.
Same: neutron number. Different: proton number.

wrong_explanations.1: If mass numbers were the same they would be identical — isotopes are DEFINED by having different mass numbers.
wrong_explanations.2: Isotopes DO have the same chemical properties — but they are NOT identical. They differ in neutron count and mass.
wrong_explanations.3: If neutron numbers were the same but proton numbers differed, they would be different ELEMENTS — not isotopes.

quiz.q: An element has two isotopes: 60% at mass 63 and 40% at mass 65. What is its Ar?
opts: 64.0 — simple average of 63 and 65
128 — total of both mass numbers added together
63 — just the most abundant isotope mass

wrong_explanations.1: Simple average gives 64 only if BOTH isotopes are 50/50 abundant. Since there is more of the lighter isotope (60% at mass 63), the weighted average is pulled below 64.
wrong_explanations.2: Adding mass numbers has no physical meaning — Ar is a weighted average, not a sum.
wrong_explanations.3: Using only the most abundant isotope ignores the 40% contribution from mass 65 — the true Ar is pulled above 63 by the heavier isotope.

foundation.common_mistake: Ar is a WEIGHTED average — not a simple average. Chlorine has Ar 35.5 because ~75% is Cl-35 and only ~25% is Cl-37. If both were 50/50, Ar would be 36. The heavier isotope has LESS influence because it's less abundant. Also: isotopes have SAME chemical properties (same electrons) but DIFFERENT physical properties (different mass).

atomic-structure/electronic-structure.summary: Describe how electrons fill shells and write electronic configurations for the first 20 elements.

quiz.q: An element has the electronic configuration 2.8.6. Which group of the periodic table is it in?
opts: Group 6 — it has 6 electrons in its outermost shell
Group 2 — there are 2 complete shells below the outer shell
Group 16 — add all the electrons together
Period 3 — it has 3 shells

wrong_explanations.1: The number of complete inner shells tells you the PERIOD — 2 inner shells means Period 3. Groups are determined by OUTER electrons.
wrong_explanations.2: 16 is the total number of electrons (2+8+6) — this tells you the atomic number, not the group.
wrong_explanations.3: The NUMBER OF SHELLS tells you the PERIOD (3 shells = Period 3). The GROUP is determined by the number of OUTER electrons (6).

quiz.q: Why do lithium (2.1) and sodium (2.8.1) have similar chemical properties?
opts: Both have 1 electron in their outer shell — they react in similar ways to achieve a full outer shell
Both have the same total number of electrons
Both are in the same period of the periodic table
Both have the same mass number

wrong_explanations.1: Li has 3 electrons total, Na has 11 — very different totals, but the same outer shell count (1) is what matters for reactivity.
wrong_explanations.2: Li is Period 2, Na is Period 3 — they are in different periods. Same GROUP, different periods.
wrong_explanations.3: Li has mass number 7, Na has mass number 23 — different mass numbers. Chemical similarity comes from electron configuration.

foundation.common_mistake: Always fill shells in ORDER — 2 in the first, then 8 in the second, then continue. The 3rd shell fills to 8 before the 4th shell starts. Students sometimes try to put more than 8 in the 3rd shell for early elements — for the first 20, the 3rd shell only goes up to 8.

atomic-structure/periodic-table.summary: Describe the structure of the periodic table and explain how it is organised.

quiz.q: An element is in Period 3 and Group 2. What is its electronic configuration?
opts: 2.8.2 — 3 shells (Period 3) and 2 outer electrons (Group 2)
3.2 — period number first, then group number
2.2 — two shells and two outer electrons
2.8.3 — Period 3 means 3 electrons in the outer shell

wrong_explanations.1: The periodic table doesn't give configurations directly — the group number is the outer electrons, but you must fill the inner shells first.
wrong_explanations.2: Period 3 means 3 SHELLS, not 3 electrons in the outer shell. The shells fill as 2.8.2 — inner shells full, outer shell has 2.
wrong_explanations.3: Period 3 means 3 SHELLS — not 3 electrons in the outermost shell. The Group number (2) gives the outer electron count.

quiz.q: Why do all Group 7 elements have similar chemical properties?
opts: They all have 7 electrons in their outer shell — they react similarly by gaining one electron to achieve a full shell
They are all gases at room temperature
They all have the same number of electrons
They are all in the same period of the periodic table

wrong_explanations.1: Fluorine is a gas but iodine is a solid at room temperature — physical state varies within Group 7.
wrong_explanations.2: Different Group 7 elements have different numbers of electrons (F:9, Cl:17, Br:35) — the OUTER electron count (7) is what's the same.
wrong_explanations.3: Elements in the same group are in DIFFERENT periods — they share the same outer electron count, not the same period.

foundation.common_mistake: The GROUP NUMBER tells you the number of OUTER ELECTRONS — not the total electrons or the period. The PERIOD NUMBER tells you how many shells. So an element in Period 3, Group 2 has 3 shells and 2 outer electrons — electronic configuration 2.8.2 (magnesium). Don't confuse groups and periods.

So an element in Period 3, Group 2 has 3 shells and 2 outer electrons — electronic configuration 2.8.2 (magnesium). Don't confuse groups and periods.

Group 7 reactivity decreases down (outer shell further, weaker pull on incoming electron). Predict properties of unknown elements from position.

atomic-structure/development-periodic-ta.summary: Describe how the periodic table developed, including Newlands and Mendeleev's contributions.

quiz.q: Why did the scientific community initially reject Newlands' Law of Octaves?
opts: It only worked for the first 16 elements and forced elements into groups regardless of whether properties matched
Newlands arranged elements by atomic number rather than relative atomic mass
The law of octaves was never published so other scientists couldn't evaluate it
Newlands did not leave gaps for undiscovered elements — but neither did he need to

wrong_explanations.1: Newlands arranged by RELATIVE ATOMIC MASS — atomic numbers were not yet known.
wrong_explanations.2: Newlands did publish his work — it was presented to the Chemical Society in 1866.
wrong_explanations.3: Not leaving gaps was a problem for Mendeleev too initially — but the bigger issue for Newlands was that his pattern broke down after the first 16 elements.

quiz.q: What was the key reason Mendeleev's periodic table was eventually accepted by scientists?
opts: He predicted properties of undiscovered elements — and when they were found, the predictions were accurate
He was the first to arrange elements in order of atomic mass
He discovered all the noble gases and added them to the table
His table was the simplest and easiest to remember

wrong_explanations.1: Newlands had also arranged by atomic mass — Mendeleev's key advance was leaving gaps and making testable predictions.
wrong_explanations.2: Noble gases were discovered by other scientists (Ramsay and others) after Mendeleev's original table — they were later added as Group 0.
wrong_explanations.3: Science accepts models based on EVIDENCE and PREDICTIVE POWER, not simplicity alone.

foundation.common_mistake: Mendeleev arranged elements by RELATIVE ATOMIC MASS — the MODERN table is arranged by ATOMIC NUMBER. Mendeleev's arrangement had a few inconsistencies because Ar and atomic number don't always match perfectly (e.g. argon and potassium). The modern arrangement by atomic number resolves all these problems.

atomic-structure/metals-non-metals.summary: Compare the properties of metals and non-metals and explain them in terms of structure and bonding.

quiz.q: Why are metals good conductors of electricity?
opts: Metals have delocalised (free) electrons that can move through the structure and carry charge
Metal atoms are larger, so electrons move more easily
Metals have more protons than non-metals, creating stronger positive charge
Metal compounds dissolve in water to release ions that carry charge

wrong_explanations.1: Atomic size does not determine conductivity — it is the presence of FREE electrons, not large atoms, that allows charge to flow.
wrong_explanations.2: Proton count determines the element, not conductivity. Non-metals also have many protons but are poor conductors.
wrong_explanations.3: Solid metals conduct electricity — not dissolved ions. Ionic conductivity in solution is different from metallic conductivity in a solid.

quiz.q: Sulfur dioxide (SO₂) dissolves in water to form an acidic solution. Is sulfur a metal or non-metal?
opts: Non-metal — non-metal oxides form acidic solutions when dissolved in water
Metal — metal oxides are always basic
Metalloid — SO₂ is both acidic and basic
It could be either — the acidity of its oxide doesn't indicate the type of element

wrong_explanations.1: Metal oxides are BASIC — they neutralise acids. SO₂ forms an acidic solution, which is the behaviour of NON-METAL oxides.
wrong_explanations.2: SO₂ is not amphoteric (both acidic and basic) — it is distinctly acidic. Sulfur is clearly a non-metal.
wrong_explanations.3: The type of oxide (acidic or basic) is a reliable indicator of whether the element is a metal or non-metal.

foundation.common_mistake: Graphite and graphene are non-metals that conduct electricity — they are exceptions. Most non-metals do not conduct, but graphite has delocalised electrons between its layers. Don't say 'all non-metals don't conduct' — say 'non-metals generally don't conduct, except graphite and graphene'.

atomic-structure/group-0.summary: Describe the properties of noble gases and explain why they are so unreactive.

quiz.q: Why are noble gases so unreactive?
opts: They have full outer electron shells — no tendency to gain, lose or share electrons to become more stable
They are gases — gases cannot react with solid or liquid substances
They have no electrons in their outer shell — so they cannot form bonds
They are too small to collide effectively with other atoms

wrong_explanations.1: Many gases are highly reactive — chlorine gas (Cl₂), oxygen gas (O₂) etc. Being a gas doesn't make something unreactive.
wrong_explanations.2: Noble gases have FULL outer shells, not empty ones. Helium has 2 electrons (full 1st shell), others have 8 outer electrons.
wrong_explanations.3: Atomic size affects reactivity in some cases, but noble gases are unreactive because of electron configuration, not size.

quiz.q: Argon has a higher boiling point than neon. Why?
opts: Argon is a larger atom with more electrons — stronger London dispersion forces between atoms need more energy to overcome
Argon has a higher atomic number, so its electrons are more strongly attracted to the nucleus
Argon forms molecules (Ar₂) while neon is monatomic — more bonds to break
Argon is radioactive, releasing energy that raises its boiling point

wrong_explanations.1: Stronger nuclear attraction would make electrons harder to remove but doesn't directly raise boiling point — the intermolecular forces between atoms are what matter.
wrong_explanations.2: Noble gases are ALL monatomic — they never form Ar₂ or Ne₂ molecules under normal conditions.
wrong_explanations.3: Argon is stable and not radioactive. Radon is the radioactive noble gas.

foundation.common_mistake: Noble gases are MONATOMIC — they exist as single atoms (He, Ne, Ar) — NOT as molecules (not He₂ or Ne₂). This is because they are already stable with full outer shells and have no reason to form bonds. Other non-metals like oxygen (O₂) and nitrogen (N₂) exist as diatomic molecules because they share electrons to achieve full outer shells.

atomic-structure/group-1.summary: Describe the properties of Group 1 metals and explain trends in reactivity down the group.

quiz.q: What are the products when sodium reacts with water?
opts: Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and hydrogen gas (H₂)
Sodium oxide (Na₂O) and water
Sodium chloride (NaCl) and hydrogen
Sodium hydroxide and oxygen gas

wrong_explanations.1: Sodium reacts with oxygen to form sodium OXIDE — but when it reacts with water specifically, the products are hydroxide and hydrogen.
wrong_explanations.2: NaCl is the product of sodium reacting with CHLORINE or hydrochloric acid — not water.
wrong_explanations.3: Oxygen is not released when metals react with water. The equation is: 2Na + 2H₂O → 2NaOH + H₂.

quiz.q: Why is potassium more reactive than lithium?
opts: Potassium has more electron shells — its outer electron is further from the nucleus and more easily lost
Potassium has more protons — so it has a stronger positive charge making it more reactive
Potassium is a larger atom so it reacts with a larger surface area
Potassium's outer electron has a higher charge than lithium's outer electron

wrong_explanations.1: More protons means stronger nuclear charge — this would actually make it HARDER to lose the electron, not easier. But the extra shells and increased distance outweigh the effect of more protons.
wrong_explanations.2: Surface area affects reaction rate but not inherent reactivity — the question is about why K is more reactive than Li in terms of properties.
wrong_explanations.3: All electrons have the same charge (−1 each) — the 'ease of losing' them depends on how strongly they are attracted to the nucleus.

foundation.common_mistake: Reactivity INCREASES down Group 1 (opposite to Group 7). This is because the outer electron gets further from the nucleus and is more easily lost. Going down Group 1: more shells → outer electron further away → weaker attraction → MORE reactive. Going down Group 7: non-metals get LESS reactive (harder to attract an extra electron).

atomic-structure/group-7.summary: Describe the properties of halogens and explain trends in reactivity down Group 7.

quiz.q: Chlorine water is added to a solution of potassium bromide. What is observed?
opts: The solution turns orange/brown — chlorine displaces bromide ions, releasing bromine
No change — chlorine cannot react with bromide ions
The solution turns pale green — chlorine is absorbed by the solution
A white precipitate forms — chlorine reacts with potassium ions

wrong_explanations.1: Chlorine IS more reactive than bromine and CAN displace bromide — so a reaction does occur.
wrong_explanations.2: Chlorine water has a yellow-green colour but when it reacts with bromide, the BROWN colour of bromine is what appears in the solution.
wrong_explanations.3: Chlorine does not react with potassium ions — it displaces bromide ions. No precipitate forms.

quiz.q: Why is fluorine more reactive than chlorine?
opts: Fluorine is a smaller atom — its outer shell is closer to the nucleus, so it attracts an incoming electron more strongly
Fluorine has fewer electrons, making it less stable and more reactive
Fluorine has more protons, making its positive charge stronger
Fluorine is a gas, making it react more easily than other states of matter

wrong_explanations.1: Fewer electrons in an outer shell means the atom needs to GAIN electrons — but that doesn't directly explain why fluorine attracts electrons more strongly. The distance from the nucleus is the key factor.
wrong_explanations.2: Fluorine does have fewer protons (9) than chlorine (17) — but its outer shell is MUCH closer to those protons. The closeness wins over the fewer-proton effect.
wrong_explanations.3: Chlorine is also a gas — being a gas is not the reason fluorine is more reactive.

quiz.q: Iodine solution is added to sodium chloride solution. What happens?
opts: No reaction — iodine is less reactive than chlorine and cannot displace it
Chlorine gas is released — iodine displaces chloride ions
A brown precipitate forms — iodine reacts with sodium ions
The solution turns pale yellow — iodine bleaches the solution

wrong_explanations.1: A more reactive halogen displaces a less reactive one. Iodine is LESS reactive than chlorine — so iodine cannot displace chloride ions.
wrong_explanations.2: Iodine does not react with sodium ions — and even if it tried to displace chloride, it couldn't because iodine is less reactive than chlorine.
wrong_explanations.3: Iodine actually turns starch blue-black — it doesn't bleach. Chlorine is the halogen with bleaching properties.

foundation.common_mistake: Reactivity DECREASES down Group 7 — the OPPOSITE of Group 1. In Group 1, going down makes metals MORE reactive (outer electron easier to LOSE). In Group 7, going down makes non-metals LESS reactive (harder to GAIN an extra electron when the outer shell is further away).

wrong_explanations.3: Iodine actually turns starch blue-black — it doesn't bleach. Chlorine is the halogen with bleaching properties.

Astatine predicted to be solid, dark coloured and least reactive halogen. Displacement reactions as reactivity evidence.

bonding/chemical-bonds.summary: Describe why atoms form chemical bonds and the three main types of bonding.

quiz.q: Sodium chloride is made from a metal and a non-metal. Predict the type of bonding it contains and describe how it forms.
opts: Ionic — the metal atom transfers electrons to the non-metal, forming oppositely charged ions that attract
Covalent — the metal and non-metal share a pair of electrons
Metallic — the atoms sit in a sea of delocalised electrons
Hydrogen bonding — a weak attraction forms between the two atoms

wrong_explanations.1: Sharing electrons is covalent bonding, and it occurs between two NON-metals — not a metal with a non-metal.
wrong_explanations.2: Metallic bonding needs metal atoms only (a pure metal or alloy), not a metal combined with a non-metal.
wrong_explanations.3: Hydrogen bonding is a weak intermolecular force, not the main bond between a metal and a non-metal.

quiz.q: Magnesium (Group 2) reacts with oxygen (Group 6) to form magnesium oxide. Identify the type of bonding in MgO.
opts: Ionic — magnesium transfers 2 electrons to oxygen, forming Mg²⁺ and O²⁻ ions
Covalent — they share 2 electrons to fill their outer shells
Metallic — magnesium releases electrons into a sea
No bonding — both are stable so they do not react

wrong_explanations.1: Sharing electrons is covalent and only happens between two non-metals; magnesium is a metal.
wrong_explanations.2: Metallic bonding occurs in metals only, not in a compound of a metal with a non-metal.
wrong_explanations.3: Magnesium burns vigorously in oxygen — MgO is a stable ionic compound.

quiz.q: Water (H₂O) is made from two non-metals. Predict the type of bonding it contains.
opts: Covalent — the non-metal atoms share pairs of electrons
Ionic — one atom transfers electrons to the other
Metallic — the atoms share a sea of delocalised electrons
Ionic — because water can conduct electricity

wrong_explanations.1: Electron transfer (ionic) happens between a metal and a non-metal; hydrogen and oxygen are both non-metals.
wrong_explanations.2: Metallic bonding needs metal atoms; water contains none.
wrong_explanations.3: Pure water barely conducts; where it does, it is due to tiny amounts of ions, not because it is ionic.

quiz.q: Explain why copper is described as having metallic bonding.
opts: Its atoms release outer electrons into a delocalised sea, and the positive ions are attracted to that sea
Its atoms share pairs of electrons in covalent bonds
It transfers electrons to form positive and negative ions
Its atoms are simply packed closely together with no forces between them

wrong_explanations.1: Sharing pairs of electrons is covalent bonding, which occurs between non-metals — copper is a metal.
wrong_explanations.2: Transfer forming positive and negative ions is ionic bonding, which needs a non-metal as well.
wrong_explanations.3: There ARE strong forces — the electrostatic attraction between the positive ions and the electron sea.

quiz.q: Describe the difference in how electrons behave in ionic bonding compared with covalent bonding.
opts: In ionic bonding electrons are transferred from one atom to another; in covalent bonding electrons are shared between atoms
In ionic bonding electrons are shared; in covalent bonding they are transferred
In both, electrons are transferred, but ionic bonds are stronger
In both, electrons are shared, but covalent bonds involve metals

wrong_explanations.1: This is the wrong way round: ionic = transfer, covalent = sharing.
wrong_explanations.2: Covalent bonding is sharing, not transfer; only ionic bonding transfers electrons.
wrong_explanations.3: Covalent bonding is sharing (not transfer) and occurs between non-metals, not metals.

quiz.q: State why atoms form chemical bonds.
opts: To gain a full, stable outer shell of electrons, like the noble gases
To increase their mass
To become radioactive
To lose all of their electrons

wrong_explanations.1: Bonding rearranges electrons, not mass — the number of protons and neutrons is unchanged.
wrong_explanations.2: Bonding has nothing to do with radioactivity, which involves the nucleus.
wrong_explanations.3: Atoms aim for a FULL outer shell, not an empty one — many gain or share electrons rather than lose them.

quiz.q: Name the type of bonding that occurs in a pure metal such as iron.
opts: Metallic
Ionic
Covalent
Intermolecular

wrong_explanations.1: Ionic bonding needs a metal AND a non-metal; pure iron is a metal only.
wrong_explanations.2: Covalent bonding is sharing between non-metals; iron is a metal.
wrong_explanations.3: Intermolecular forces act between molecules; a pure metal has no molecules.

quiz.q: Name the type of bonding that forms between two non-metals.
opts: Covalent
Ionic
Metallic
No bond forms

wrong_explanations.1: Ionic bonding forms between a metal and a non-metal, not two non-metals.
wrong_explanations.2: Metallic bonding needs metal atoms only.
wrong_explanations.3: Two non-metals do bond — by sharing electrons (covalent).

quiz.q: State whether a metal atom loses or gains electrons when it forms an ion.
opts: Loses electrons, forming a positive ion
Gains electrons, forming a positive ion
Loses electrons, forming a negative ion
Gains electrons, forming a negative ion

wrong_explanations.1: Gaining electrons would make it negative; losing them is what makes a metal ion positive.
wrong_explanations.2: Losing electrons gives a POSITIVE ion (fewer electrons than protons), not a negative one.
wrong_explanations.3: Gaining electrons is what non-metals do to form negative ions; metals lose electrons.

quiz.q: Identify which substance contains ionic bonding.
opts: Sodium chloride
Oxygen gas
Copper metal
Diamond

wrong_explanations.1: Oxygen (O₂) is two non-metals joined by a covalent bond, not ionic.
wrong_explanations.2: Copper is a pure metal — its bonding is metallic.
wrong_explanations.3: Diamond is carbon only, held by covalent bonds throughout a giant structure — not ionic.

foundation.common_mistake: Students often think that when a simple molecular substance melts, its covalent bonds break. They do not: melting only separates whole molecules from each other by overcoming the weak intermolecular forces between them. The strong covalent bonds inside each molecule stay intact — which is why a low melting point tells you nothing about how strong the covalent bonds themselves are.

wrong_explanations.2: Covalent bonding is sharing, not transfer; only ionic bonding transfers electrons.
wrong_explanations.3: Covalent bonding is sharing (not transfer) and occurs between non-metals, not metals.

quiz.q: A substance has a high melting point and conducts electricity only when molten or dissolved, never when solid. Deduce its type of bonding and justify your answer.
opts: Ionic — the ions are fixed in the solid but become free to move (and carry charge) when molten or dissolved
Metallic — delocalised electrons carry charge in all states
Simple covalent — the molecules move when the substance melts
Giant covalent — the electrons are released only on melting

wrong_explanations.1: A metal would conduct as a SOLID too (delocalised electrons move in the solid) — this substance does not.
wrong_explanations.2: Simple covalent substances have LOW melting points and never conduct (no charged particles).
wrong_explanations.3: Giant covalent substances (e.g. diamond) do not conduct even when molten — no free charges (graphite excepted).

quiz.q: Compare ionic bonding and metallic bonding in terms of the particles present and the forces holding them together.
opts: Ionic: oppositely charged ions attracted by electrostatic forces. Metallic: positive ions attracted to a sea of delocalised electrons
Both contain only neutral atoms held together by shared electron pairs
Ionic contains delocalised electrons; metallic contains positive and negative ions
Both contain positive and negative ions attracting each other

wrong_explanations.1: Neither is neutral atoms sharing pairs — that describes covalent bonding.
wrong_explanations.2: This swaps the two over: delocalised electrons are in METALS; positive and negative ions are in IONIC compounds.
wrong_explanations.3: Metals do not contain negative ions — they have positive ions and a sea of (negative) delocalised electrons.

quiz.q: Explain why the type of bonding in a substance can often be predicted from the positions of its elements in the periodic table.
opts: Metals (left/centre) tend to lose electrons and non-metals (right) tend to gain or share them, so the combination of positions predicts transfer, sharing or a metallic sea
Elements in the same period always bond ionically
The bond type depends only on the atomic mass, which increases across the table
All elements on the right of the table form metallic bonds

wrong_explanations.1: It is not the period that decides bonding, but whether each element is a metal or a non-metal.
wrong_explanations.2: Atomic mass does not determine bond type; whether atoms lose, gain or share electrons does.
wrong_explanations.3: The right-hand side of the table is the NON-metals, which form covalent or ionic bonds, not metallic.

quiz.q: A shiny solid conducts electricity and can be bent into shape without breaking. Deduce its type of bonding and explain both properties.
opts: Metallic — delocalised electrons carry the current, and layers of ions can slide so it bends rather than shatters
Ionic — the ions carry current and slide over each other
Simple covalent — the molecules conduct and flex
Giant covalent — free electrons conduct and the rigid lattice bends

wrong_explanations.1: Ionic solids do NOT conduct (ions fixed) and are brittle — they shatter, not bend.
wrong_explanations.2: Simple covalent substances do not conduct at all (no charged particles).
wrong_explanations.3: Giant covalent substances are hard and rigid, not bendable, and mostly do not conduct.

quiz.q: Explain why solid magnesium chloride does not conduct electricity, but molten magnesium chloride does.
opts: In the solid the ions are locked in fixed positions; when molten they are free to move and carry charge
Solid magnesium chloride has no ions until it melts
Melting adds electrons that carry the current
The solid is too cold for any current to flow

wrong_explanations.1: The Mg²⁺ and Cl⁻ ions exist in the solid lattice already — they simply cannot move when solid.
wrong_explanations.2: Conduction in molten ionic compounds is by moving IONS, not by added electrons.
wrong_explanations.3: Temperature alone is not the point — it is whether the charged ions are free to move.

higher.common_mistake: Students often think that when a simple molecular substance melts, its covalent bonds break. They do not: melting only separates whole molecules from each other by overcoming the weak intermolecular forces between them. The strong covalent bonds inside each molecule stay intact — which is why a low melting point tells you nothing about how strong the covalent bonds themselves are.

bonding/ionic-bonding.summary: Describe how ionic bonds form by electron transfer between metals and non-metals.

quiz.q: State the type of bonding that forms when a metal reacts with a non-metal.
opts: Ionic bonding — electrons are transferred from the metal to the non-metal
Covalent bonding — the metal and non-metal share electrons
Metallic bonding — the atoms share a sea of delocalised electrons
No bonding — metals and non-metals do not react together

wrong_explanations.1: Covalent bonding is sharing, and it happens between two NON-metals — not a metal with a non-metal.
wrong_explanations.2: Metallic bonding occurs in metals and alloys (metal atoms only), not between a metal and a non-metal.
wrong_explanations.3: Metals and non-metals react readily — e.g. sodium burns vigorously in chlorine to make salt.

quiz.q: Identify the pair of elements that will form an ionic bond.
opts: Magnesium and oxygen
Hydrogen and chlorine
Oxygen and nitrogen
Copper and zinc

wrong_explanations.1: Hydrogen and chlorine are both non-metals, so they share electrons — that is a covalent bond, not ionic.
wrong_explanations.2: Oxygen and nitrogen are both non-metals, so they bond covalently (by sharing), not ionically.
wrong_explanations.3: Copper and zinc are both metals — together they form an alloy held by metallic bonding, not an ionic bond.

quiz.q: Chlorine is in Group 7. State the charge on a chloride ion.

wrong_explanations.1: Non-metals GAIN electrons to fill their outer shell — gaining makes them negative, not positive.
wrong_explanations.2: The charge is not the group number. A Group 7 atom needs just 1 more electron, so it gains 1 and becomes 1−.
wrong_explanations.3: Chlorine gains electrons (becoming negative); it does not lose 7 to become 7+.

quiz.q: Magnesium forms a Mg²⁺ ion. State how many electrons a magnesium atom loses.
opts: 0 — it gains 2 electrons

wrong_explanations.1: Losing only 1 electron would give a 1+ ion, not the 2+ ion stated.
wrong_explanations.2: Magnesium is in Group 2, so it has 2 outer electrons to lose — not 6.
wrong_explanations.3: Magnesium is a metal, so it LOSES electrons to form a positive ion; it does not gain them.

quiz.q: Describe how you can use an element's group number to predict the charge on its ion.
opts: Group 1, 2 and 3 metals lose that many electrons (1+, 2+, 3+); Group 5, 6 and 7 non-metals gain electrons to reach a full shell (3−, 2−, 1−)
The charge is always equal to the group number, whether the element is a metal or a non-metal
Metals always form 1+ ions and non-metals always form 1− ions, whatever their group
The charge equals the number of the period (row) the element is in

wrong_explanations.1: For non-metals the charge is NOT the group number — Group 6 gains 2 (not 6) to become 2−.
wrong_explanations.2: The charge depends on the group: a Group 2 metal forms 2+, a Group 3 metal forms 3+.
wrong_explanations.3: The charge comes from the group (electrons gained or lost), not the period.

quiz.q: Sodium (2.8.1) reacts with chlorine (2.8.7) to form sodium chloride. Describe what happens to the electrons.
opts: Sodium loses its 1 outer electron to form Na⁺ (2.8); chlorine gains it to form Cl⁻ (2.8.8); the oppositely charged ions then attract
Sodium and chlorine share one pair of electrons in a single covalent bond
Both atoms lose their outer electrons into a shared pool
Chlorine loses one electron to sodium, so chlorine becomes positive

wrong_explanations.1: Sharing is covalent bonding. NaCl is a metal + non-metal, so electrons are TRANSFERRED, not shared.
wrong_explanations.2: Only the metal (sodium) loses electrons; the non-metal (chlorine) gains them.
wrong_explanations.3: Chlorine GAINS the electron (it needs 1 more for a full shell), so it becomes the NEGATIVE ion.

quiz.q: Explain why an ionic compound such as sodium chloride has no overall electrical charge.
opts: The total positive charge on the metal ions exactly balances the total negative charge on the non-metal ions
The electrons transferred are destroyed, leaving neutral atoms
The ions are held so tightly that their charges disappear
Only the outer shells carry charge, and these cancel when the shells are full

wrong_explanations.1: Electrons are transferred, not destroyed — charge is conserved. The compound is neutral because the ion charges balance.
wrong_explanations.2: Charge does not 'disappear' — the ions keep their charges; the compound is neutral because they sum to zero.
wrong_explanations.3: A full shell is not uncharged. Neutrality comes from equal amounts of positive and negative charge.

quiz.q: Determine the formula of calcium chloride. (Calcium is in Group 2, chlorine is in Group 7.)
opts: CaCl₂ — Ca²⁺ needs two Cl⁻ ions to balance its charge
CaCl — one calcium ion bonds with one chloride ion
Ca₂Cl — two calcium ions bond with one chloride ion
CaCl₃ — calcium needs three chloride ions to become stable

wrong_explanations.1: CaCl would give +2 + (−1) = +1 overall — not neutral. You need two Cl⁻ to balance one Ca²⁺.
wrong_explanations.2: Ca₂Cl would give 2(+2) + (−1) = +3 — not neutral.
wrong_explanations.3: CaCl₃ would give +2 + 3(−1) = −1 — not neutral. Exactly two Cl⁻ balance one Ca²⁺.

quiz.q: Explain, in terms of electron arrangement, why metals form positive ions but non-metals form negative ions.
opts: Metals have few outer electrons and lose them to empty their outer shell, leaving a positive ion; non-metals have nearly full shells and gain electrons, becoming negative
Metals are heavier, so their nuclei pull electrons in and become positive
Non-metals contain more protons, which makes them negative
Metals gain electrons and non-metals lose them, to reach a full shell

wrong_explanations.1: Mass/heaviness does not set the charge — it is how many electrons are lost or gained to reach a full shell.
wrong_explanations.2: Proton number sets the element's identity, not the sign of the ion; gaining electrons is what makes non-metals negative.
wrong_explanations.3: This is the wrong way round: metals LOSE electrons (become +), non-metals GAIN electrons (become −).

quiz.q: Describe how a dot-and-cross diagram represents the formation of the ions in sodium chloride.
opts: Electrons from one atom are drawn as dots and from the other as crosses, showing the single outer electron moving from sodium to chlorine so each ion has a full outer shell
It shows the sodium and chlorine sharing a pair of electrons drawn between them
It shows a sea of electrons moving freely around fixed positive ions
It shows the chlorine atom giving one electron to the sodium atom

wrong_explanations.1: That describes a COVALENT dot-and-cross (shared pair). In NaCl the electron is transferred, not shared.
wrong_explanations.2: That is the metallic bonding model, not a dot-and-cross diagram of ionic NaCl.
wrong_explanations.3: The transfer is the other way: sodium (the metal) gives its electron to chlorine (the non-metal).

foundation.common_mistake: Students often say the ionic bond IS the transfer of electrons from the metal to the non-metal. That is not the bond: the transfer is a one-off event that only creates the ions. The ionic bond itself is the strong electrostatic attraction between the oppositely charged ions produced. Remember too that these charges must balance, so every ionic compound has an overall charge of zero.

opts: Sodium loses its 1 outer electron to form Na⁺ (2.8); chlorine gains it to form Cl⁻ (2.8.8); the oppositely charged ions then attract
Sodium and chlorine share one pair of electrons in a single covalent bond
Both atoms lose their outer electrons into a shared pool
Chlorine loses one electron to sodium, so chlorine becomes positive

wrong_explanations.1: Sharing is covalent bonding. NaCl is a metal + non-metal, so electrons are TRANSFERRED, not shared.
wrong_explanations.2: Only the metal (sodium) loses electrons; the non-metal (chlorine) gains them.
wrong_explanations.3: Chlorine GAINS the electron (it needs 1 more for a full shell), so it becomes the NEGATIVE ion.

wrong_explanations.1: Electrons are transferred, not destroyed — charge is conserved. The compound is neutral because the ion charges balance.
wrong_explanations.2: Charge does not 'disappear' — the ions keep their charges; the compound is neutral because they sum to zero.
wrong_explanations.3: A full shell is not uncharged. Neutrality comes from equal amounts of positive and negative charge.

opts: CaCl₂ — Ca²⁺ needs two Cl⁻ ions to balance its charge
CaCl — one calcium ion bonds with one chloride ion
Ca₂Cl — two calcium ions bond with one chloride ion
CaCl₃ — calcium needs three chloride ions to become stable

wrong_explanations.2: Ca₂Cl would give 2(+2) + (−1) = +3 — not neutral.
wrong_explanations.3: CaCl₃ would give +2 + 3(−1) = −1 — not neutral. Exactly two Cl⁻ balance one Ca²⁺.

opts: Metals have few outer electrons and lose them to empty their outer shell, leaving a positive ion; non-metals have nearly full shells and gain electrons, becoming negative
Metals are heavier, so their nuclei pull electrons in and become positive
Non-metals contain more protons, which makes them negative
Metals gain electrons and non-metals lose them, to reach a full shell

wrong_explanations.1: Mass/heaviness does not set the charge — it is how many electrons are lost or gained to reach a full shell.
wrong_explanations.2: Proton number sets the element's identity, not the sign of the ion; gaining electrons is what makes non-metals negative.
wrong_explanations.3: This is the wrong way round: metals LOSE electrons (become +), non-metals GAIN electrons (become −).

quiz.q: Describe how a dot-and-cross diagram represents the formation of the ions in sodium chloride.
opts: Electrons from one atom are drawn as dots and from the other as crosses, showing the single outer electron moving from sodium to chlorine so each ion has a full outer shell
It shows the sodium and chlorine sharing a pair of electrons drawn between them
It shows a sea of electrons moving freely around fixed positive ions
It shows the chlorine atom giving one electron to the sodium atom

wrong_explanations.1: That describes a COVALENT dot-and-cross (shared pair). In NaCl the electron is transferred, not shared.
wrong_explanations.2: That is the metallic bonding model, not a dot-and-cross diagram of ionic NaCl.
wrong_explanations.3: The transfer is the other way: sodium (the metal) gives its electron to chlorine (the non-metal).

quiz.q: Magnesium reacts with chlorine. Deduce the formula of the compound formed and justify it using the ion charges.
opts: MgCl₂ — Mg²⁺ has a 2+ charge, so it needs two Cl⁻ ions to balance it
MgCl — one magnesium ion pairs with one chloride ion
Mg₂Cl — two magnesium ions pair with one chloride ion
MgCl₃ — magnesium needs three chloride ions

wrong_explanations.1: MgCl gives +2 + (−1) = +1 — not neutral. Two Cl⁻ are needed to balance Mg²⁺.
wrong_explanations.2: Mg₂Cl gives 2(+2) + (−1) = +3 — not neutral.
wrong_explanations.3: MgCl₃ gives +2 + 3(−1) = −1 — not neutral; only two Cl⁻ are required.

quiz.q: A student writes: 'The ionic bond is the transfer of electrons from sodium to chlorine.' Explain why this statement is not correct.
opts: The transfer only creates the ions; the ionic bond is the electrostatic attraction between the oppositely charged ions that results
It is correct — the ionic bond is the movement of the electron
The electrons are shared, not transferred, so the description is wrong
Sodium gains the electron, so the direction of transfer is wrong

wrong_explanations.1: The transfer is a one-off event, not the ongoing bond — so the statement is not correct.
wrong_explanations.2: Sodium chloride is ionic (metal + non-metal), so electrons are transferred, not shared.
wrong_explanations.3: Sodium is the metal and LOSES the electron; chlorine gains it. The stated direction is right — the error is calling the transfer 'the bond'.

quiz.q: Potassium reacts with oxygen. Predict the formula of potassium oxide and justify your answer using ion charges. (K is Group 1, O is Group 6.)
opts: K₂O — each K⁺ carries 1+, so two are needed to balance one O²⁻
KO — one potassium ion balances one oxygen ion
KO₂ — one potassium ion needs two oxide ions
K₂O₃ — by analogy with aluminium oxide

wrong_explanations.1: KO gives +1 + (−2) = −1 — not neutral. Two K⁺ are needed to balance one O²⁻.
wrong_explanations.2: KO₂ gives +1 + 2(−2) = −3 — not neutral.
wrong_explanations.3: K forms 1+ (not 3+), so the aluminium-oxide pattern does not apply; K₂O is correct.

quiz.q: Explain why the noble gases in Group 0 do not normally form ions.
opts: Their outer shells are already full, so they have no tendency to lose or gain electrons
They are gases, and gases cannot form ions
Their atoms are too small to hold a charge
They already exist as negative ions in nature

wrong_explanations.1: State of matter does not decide this — many gases form ions when they react. Noble gases don't because their shells are full.
wrong_explanations.2: Atom size does not prevent ion formation; a full outer shell removes the tendency to react.
wrong_explanations.3: Noble gases are neutral atoms with full shells, not naturally occurring ions.

quiz.q: Explain, in terms of electron transfer, why the formula of aluminium oxide is Al₂O₃.
opts: Al loses 3 electrons to form Al³⁺ and O gains 2 to form O²⁻; balancing the charges needs two Al³⁺ (6+) and three O²⁻ (6−)
Aluminium and oxygen share three pairs of electrons
Aluminium forms Al²⁺ and oxygen forms O³⁻, giving Al₃O₂
The subscripts 2 and 3 come from the group numbers 2 and 3

wrong_explanations.1: Aluminium oxide is ionic (metal + non-metal) — electrons are transferred, not shared.
wrong_explanations.2: Aluminium is Group 3 (forms Al³⁺) and oxygen is Group 6 (forms O²⁻); the charges are swapped here, which would give the wrong formula.
wrong_explanations.3: The subscripts come from balancing the CHARGES (3+ and 2− → lowest common multiple 6), not from the group numbers directly.

higher.common_mistake: Students often say the ionic bond IS the transfer of electrons from the metal to the non-metal. That is not the bond: the transfer is a one-off event that only creates the ions. The ionic bond itself is the strong electrostatic attraction between the oppositely charged ions produced. Remember too that these charges must balance, so every ionic compound has an overall charge of zero.

bonding/ionic-compounds.summary: Describe the giant ionic lattice structure of ionic compounds and link it to their properties.

quiz.q: Explain why solid sodium chloride does not conduct electricity, but molten sodium chloride does.
opts: In the solid the ions are held in fixed positions and cannot move; when molten the ions are free to move and carry charge
Solid sodium chloride contains no ions — they only form when it melts
Solid sodium chloride is white, and colour prevents conduction
Molten sodium chloride conducts using free electrons, not ions

wrong_explanations.1: NaCl is ionic in all states — the Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions are present in the solid, they just cannot move.
wrong_explanations.2: Colour has no effect on electrical conductivity.
wrong_explanations.3: Ionic conduction is carried by IONS, not electrons; electron conduction happens in metals.

quiz.q: Explain why ionic compounds have high melting points.
opts: Many strong electrostatic forces act between the oppositely charged ions throughout the giant lattice, so a lot of energy is needed to separate them
The large size of the crystal means more energy is needed to melt it
Ionic compounds contain strong covalent bonds that must be broken
The electrons in the compound are tightly held and resist heating

wrong_explanations.1: The physical size of a crystal does not set the melting point — the strength of the bonding does.
wrong_explanations.2: Ionic compounds contain ionic bonds (electrostatic attraction between ions), not covalent bonds.
wrong_explanations.3: Electrons are transferred to form the ions; they are not the reason for the high melting point.

quiz.q: Describe the structure of a giant ionic lattice.
opts: A regular 3D arrangement of many oppositely charged ions, held by strong electrostatic forces acting in all directions
A small group of a few ions joined into a single molecule
A sea of delocalised electrons around fixed positive ions
Long chains of atoms joined by weak forces

wrong_explanations.1: An ionic compound is NOT made of small molecules — it is a giant lattice of billions of ions.
wrong_explanations.2: That is the metallic bonding model; ionic lattices have positive AND negative ions, no electron sea.
wrong_explanations.3: That describes polymers; ionic lattices are rigid 3D arrangements of ions, not chains.

quiz.q: Ionic compounds are hard but brittle. Explain why they shatter when hit.
opts: A blow shifts the layers of ions so that like-charged ions line up next to each other; they repel strongly and the crystal splits
The covalent bonds inside the crystal snap
The delocalised electrons stop holding the ions together
The ions melt at the point of impact

wrong_explanations.1: Ionic compounds have no covalent bonds — they are held by electrostatic attraction between ions.
wrong_explanations.2: Ionic compounds contain no delocalised electrons — that is a metallic feature.
wrong_explanations.3: Shattering is a mechanical effect of ion repulsion, not melting.

quiz.q: Explain why many ionic compounds dissolve in water and the solution then conducts electricity.
opts: Water molecules pull the ions away from the lattice; the freed ions can move through the solution and carry charge
The water breaks the ions down into neutral atoms that carry current
The water adds electrons that carry the current
Dissolving turns the compound into a metal

wrong_explanations.1: The ions keep their charges when they dissolve — they are not turned into neutral atoms.
wrong_explanations.2: Conduction in the solution is by the moving IONS, not by added electrons.
wrong_explanations.3: Dissolving separates the existing ions; it does not create metallic bonding.

quiz.q: State the type of structure that ionic compounds form.
opts: A giant ionic lattice
Small separate molecules
A metallic lattice
Long polymer chains

wrong_explanations.1: Ionic compounds are not made of discrete molecules; they form a giant repeating lattice.
wrong_explanations.2: A metallic lattice has positive ions and delocalised electrons, not a mix of positive and negative ions.
wrong_explanations.3: Polymer chains are a covalent, molecular arrangement, not ionic.

quiz.q: State the two conditions under which an ionic compound will conduct electricity.
opts: When it is molten or when it is dissolved in water
When it is solid or when it is molten
When it is solid or when it is dissolved
Only when it is solid

wrong_explanations.1: A solid ionic compound does NOT conduct — its ions are fixed.
wrong_explanations.2: The solid does not conduct; only the dissolved (and molten) states do.
wrong_explanations.3: The solid never conducts, because the ions cannot move.

quiz.q: State whether ionic compounds have high or low melting points.
opts: High
Low
Always exactly 0°C
They do not melt at all

wrong_explanations.1: Ionic compounds have HIGH melting points because of the strong forces throughout the lattice.
wrong_explanations.2: 0°C is the melting point of ice, not a general rule for ionic compounds.
wrong_explanations.3: Ionic compounds do melt — but only at high temperatures.

quiz.q: Describe how the ions are arranged in solid sodium chloride.
opts: Positive and negative ions alternate in a regular, repeating 3D pattern
The ions are randomly scattered with large gaps between them
All the positive ions are on one side and all the negative ions on the other
The ions are joined in pairs that float freely

wrong_explanations.1: The lattice is regular and ordered, not random.
wrong_explanations.2: Ions alternate so that each is surrounded by oppositely charged ions — they are not separated by charge.
wrong_explanations.3: The ions are fixed in a giant lattice, not floating pairs.

quiz.q: Predict whether solid potassium bromide will conduct electricity, and give a reason.
opts: No — its ions are fixed in the lattice and cannot move
Yes — it contains ions, which always conduct
Yes — its delocalised electrons carry the current
No — potassium bromide contains no charged particles

wrong_explanations.1: Containing ions is not enough — they must be FREE TO MOVE, and in a solid they are fixed.
wrong_explanations.2: Ionic compounds have no delocalised electrons; that is a metallic feature.
wrong_explanations.3: It does contain charged particles (K⁺ and Br⁻ ions); the reason it does not conduct is that they cannot move.

foundation.common_mistake: Students often say that because ionic compounds contain charged ions they should conduct electricity even when solid. They do not: in a solid the ions are locked in fixed positions in the lattice and cannot move. Only when the compound is melted or dissolved are the ions free to move — and it is moving charged particles that carry a current.

quiz.q: Explain why solid sodium chloride does not conduct electricity, but molten sodium chloride does.
opts: In the solid the ions are held in fixed positions and cannot move; when molten the ions are free to move and carry charge
Solid sodium chloride contains no ions — they only form when it melts
Solid sodium chloride is white, and colour prevents conduction
Molten sodium chloride conducts using free electrons, not ions

wrong_explanations.1: NaCl is ionic in all states — the Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions are present in the solid, they just cannot move.
wrong_explanations.2: Colour has no effect on electrical conductivity.
wrong_explanations.3: Ionic conduction is carried by IONS, not electrons; electron conduction happens in metals.

opts: Many strong electrostatic forces act between the oppositely charged ions throughout the giant lattice, so a lot of energy is needed to separate them

The electrons in the compound are tightly held and resist heating

wrong_explanations.1: The physical size of a crystal does not set the melting point — the strength of the bonding does.
wrong_explanations.2: Ionic compounds contain ionic bonds (electrostatic attraction between ions), not covalent bonds.
wrong_explanations.3: Electrons are transferred to form the ions; they are not the reason for the high melting point.

opts: A regular 3D arrangement of many oppositely charged ions, held by strong electrostatic forces acting in all directions
A small group of a few ions joined into a single molecule
A sea of delocalised electrons around fixed positive ions
Long chains of atoms joined by weak forces

wrong_explanations.1: An ionic compound is NOT made of small molecules — it is a giant lattice of billions of ions.
wrong_explanations.2: That is the metallic bonding model; ionic lattices have positive AND negative ions, no electron sea.
wrong_explanations.3: That describes polymers; ionic lattices are rigid 3D arrangements of ions, not chains.

quiz.q: Ionic compounds are hard but brittle. Explain why they shatter when hit.
opts: A blow shifts the layers of ions so that like-charged ions line up next to each other; they repel strongly and the crystal splits
The covalent bonds inside the crystal snap
The delocalised electrons stop holding the ions together
The ions melt at the point of impact

wrong_explanations.1: Ionic compounds have no covalent bonds — they are held by electrostatic attraction between ions.
wrong_explanations.2: Ionic compounds contain no delocalised electrons — that is a metallic feature.
wrong_explanations.3: Shattering is a mechanical effect of ion repulsion, not melting.

quiz.q: Explain why many ionic compounds dissolve in water and the solution then conducts electricity.
opts: Water molecules pull the ions away from the lattice; the freed ions can move through the solution and carry charge
The water breaks the ions down into neutral atoms that carry current
The water adds electrons that carry the current
Dissolving turns the compound into a metal

wrong_explanations.1: The ions keep their charges when they dissolve — they are not turned into neutral atoms.
wrong_explanations.2: Conduction in the solution is by the moving IONS, not by added electrons.
wrong_explanations.3: Dissolving separates the existing ions; it does not create metallic bonding.

quiz.q: Magnesium oxide has a much higher melting point than sodium chloride. Explain why.
opts: Mg²⁺ and O²⁻ carry 2+/2− charges, larger than the 1+/1− of Na⁺ and Cl⁻, so the electrostatic forces between the ions are stronger and need more energy to break
Magnesium oxide has a bigger crystal, so more bonds must be broken
Magnesium oxide contains more ions per formula unit than sodium chloride
Magnesium is a heavier element than sodium, which raises the melting point

wrong_explanations.1: Crystal size is not the factor — both are giant lattices; the strength of each ion-ion attraction is what matters.
wrong_explanations.2: Both MgO and NaCl have a 1:1 ratio of ions — neither has more ions per formula unit.
wrong_explanations.3: Atomic mass does not set the melting point; the ionic CHARGE does.

quiz.q: Explain, in terms of structure and bonding, why ionic compounds are hard and brittle whereas metals are malleable.
opts: In an ionic solid, shifting the layers brings like charges together and they repel, so it shatters; in a metal the layers of ions slide and the electron sea keeps them bonded, so it bends
Ionic compounds contain delocalised electrons that snap; metals do not
Ionic bonds are weak and metallic bonds are strong
Metals are molecular and ionic compounds are giant

wrong_explanations.1: Ionic compounds contain no delocalised electrons; that is a metallic feature.
wrong_explanations.2: Both bonds are strong — the difference is what happens to the structure when layers move.
wrong_explanations.3: Both are giant structures; the difference is the electron sea in metals versus fixed ions in ionic solids.

quiz.q: A white solid melts at about 800°C and conducts electricity when molten but not when solid. Deduce its structure and bonding, and justify your answer using its properties.
opts: A giant ionic lattice with ionic bonding — the high melting point shows strong forces, and it conducts only when the ions become free to move on melting
A simple molecular substance — the low forces let it melt and conduct
A metallic structure — it conducts because of delocalised electrons
A giant covalent structure — it conducts when the covalent bonds melt

wrong_explanations.1: Simple molecular substances have LOW melting points and never conduct — this melts at 800°C and does conduct when molten.
wrong_explanations.2: A metal would conduct as a SOLID too; this one does not.
wrong_explanations.3: Giant covalent substances do not conduct when molten (graphite excepted), and their bonds do not 'melt' into charge carriers.

quiz.q: Explain why ionic compounds do not conduct electricity in the solid state, even though they are made of charged ions.
opts: The ions are held in fixed positions in the lattice, so although they are charged they cannot move to carry a current
The charges on the ions cancel out completely in the solid
Solids can never conduct electricity
The ions turn into neutral atoms when the compound is solid

wrong_explanations.1: The charges do not cancel — the ions keep them; conduction fails only because the ions cannot move.
wrong_explanations.2: Many solids conduct (metals, graphite); the issue here is that the ions are fixed.
wrong_explanations.3: The ions stay as ions in the solid; they do not become neutral atoms.

quiz.q: Suggest why not all ionic compounds are soluble in water.
opts: In some compounds the electrostatic forces between the ions are too strong for the water molecules to pull them out of the lattice
Insoluble ionic compounds contain no ions
Water can only dissolve metals
Insoluble compounds have covalent bonds instead of ionic bonds

wrong_explanations.1: All ionic compounds contain ions; solubility depends on how the ion-ion forces compare with the ion-water attractions.
wrong_explanations.2: Water dissolves many ionic compounds (non-metal-containing), not just metals.
wrong_explanations.3: Insoluble ionic compounds are still ionic — the difference is the strength of their lattice forces.

higher.common_mistake: Students often say that because ionic compounds contain charged ions they should conduct electricity even when solid. They do not: in a solid the ions are locked in fixed positions in the lattice and cannot move. Only when the compound is melted or dissolved are the ions free to move — and it is moving charged particles that carry a current.

bonding/covalent-bonding.summary: Describe how covalent bonds form by electron sharing between non-metal atoms.

quiz.q: Carbon (Group 4) bonds with hydrogen to form methane, CH₄. Determine how many covalent bonds a carbon atom forms.
opts: 4 — carbon has 4 outer electrons and needs 4 more, so it forms 4 shared pairs
1 — carbon forms only one bond at a time
2 — carbon forms 2 bonds like oxygen
8 — carbon needs 8 electrons in total

wrong_explanations.1: Carbon forms one bond per shared pair, and it needs 4 shared pairs — not a single bond.
wrong_explanations.2: Oxygen (Group 6) needs 2 more electrons so forms 2 bonds; carbon (Group 4) needs 4.
wrong_explanations.3: Carbon needs 8 electrons in its outer shell, but each BOND provides 2 shared electrons, so 4 bonds give the 8.

quiz.q: Explain why oxygen exists as O₂ molecules rather than as single O atoms.
opts: Each oxygen atom has 6 outer electrons and needs 2 more; by sharing two pairs with another O atom, both reach a full outer shell of 8
Two oxygen atoms join to make a more stable element in the periodic table
Oxygen atoms repel each other and pair up to cancel the repulsion
Oxygen forms O₂ so that it can be a gas at room temperature

wrong_explanations.1: Bonding does not change an element's position in the periodic table; O₂ forms because of electron sharing.
wrong_explanations.2: Atoms of the same element do repel slightly, but covalent bonding (sharing) creates a stronger overall attraction.
wrong_explanations.3: Whether a substance is a gas depends on intermolecular forces, not on why the molecule forms.

quiz.q: Describe what is meant by a covalent bond.
opts: A shared pair of electrons between two non-metal atoms, attracted to both nuclei
The transfer of electrons from a metal to a non-metal
A sea of delocalised electrons shared between metal ions
A weak attraction between two separate molecules

wrong_explanations.1: Transferring electrons is ionic bonding, which occurs between a metal and a non-metal.
wrong_explanations.2: A delocalised electron sea is the metallic bonding model.
wrong_explanations.3: That describes an intermolecular force, which acts BETWEEN molecules, not the bond within one.

quiz.q: State the number of single covalent bonds present in a water molecule, H₂O.

wrong_explanations.1: Oxygen forms 1 bond with each of the 2 hydrogen atoms, giving 2 bonds — not 1.
wrong_explanations.2: Water has only 2 hydrogen atoms, so only 2 O–H bonds form.
wrong_explanations.3: That would need 4 hydrogen atoms; water has 2.

quiz.q: Explain why a chlorine molecule (Cl₂) contains a single covalent bond.
opts: Each chlorine atom has 7 outer electrons and needs 1 more, so the two atoms share one pair, giving both a full shell of 8
Each chlorine atom shares three pairs of electrons
One chlorine atom transfers an electron to the other
The two atoms are held together by a sea of delocalised electrons

wrong_explanations.1: Sharing three pairs would be a triple bond; chlorine needs only 1 more electron, so it shares one pair.
wrong_explanations.2: Transferring an electron is ionic bonding; two identical non-metal atoms share instead.
wrong_explanations.3: A delocalised electron sea is metallic bonding; Cl₂ is a simple covalent molecule.

quiz.q: State the type of elements that form covalent bonds.
opts: Non-metals only
Metals only
A metal and a non-metal
Noble gases only

wrong_explanations.1: A metal only would give metallic bonding.
wrong_explanations.2: A metal with a non-metal gives ionic bonding (transfer), not covalent.
wrong_explanations.3: Noble gases rarely bond at all, as their outer shells are already full.

quiz.q: State what is shared between atoms in a covalent bond.
opts: A pair of electrons
A proton
A neutron
A whole atom

wrong_explanations.1: Protons stay in the nucleus and are not shared in bonding.
wrong_explanations.2: Neutrons stay in the nucleus and are not involved in bonding.
wrong_explanations.3: Atoms are not shared — only a pair of outer electrons is.

quiz.q: Name the type of covalent bond formed when two pairs of electrons are shared between two atoms.
opts: A double bond
A single bond
A triple bond
An ionic bond

wrong_explanations.1: A single bond shares only one pair of electrons.
wrong_explanations.2: A triple bond shares three pairs, not two.
wrong_explanations.3: An ionic bond involves transfer, not shared pairs.

quiz.q: Identify which substance contains covalent bonds.
opts: Carbon dioxide
Sodium chloride
Magnesium oxide
Copper

wrong_explanations.1: Sodium chloride is a metal + non-metal — ionic bonding.
wrong_explanations.2: Magnesium oxide is a metal + non-metal — ionic bonding.
wrong_explanations.3: Copper is a pure metal — metallic bonding.

quiz.q: State how many shared pairs of electrons there are in a triple bond.

wrong_explanations.1: One shared pair is a single bond.
wrong_explanations.2: Two shared pairs is a double bond.
wrong_explanations.3: A triple bond has 3 shared pairs (which is 6 electrons, but only 3 pairs).

foundation.common_mistake: Students often say that covalent bonds must be weak because substances like water have low boiling points. The covalent bonds themselves are actually very strong. The low melting and boiling points come from the weak intermolecular forces BETWEEN whole molecules — when the substance melts, those weak forces are overcome, not the strong covalent bonds inside each molecule.

opts: 4 — carbon has 4 outer electrons and needs 4 more, so it forms 4 shared pairs
1 — carbon forms only one bond at a time
2 — carbon forms 2 bonds like oxygen
8 — carbon needs 8 electrons in total

wrong_explanations.1: Carbon forms one bond per shared pair, and it needs 4 shared pairs — not a single bond.
wrong_explanations.2: Oxygen (Group 6) needs 2 more electrons so forms 2 bonds; carbon (Group 4) needs 4.
wrong_explanations.3: Carbon needs 8 electrons in its outer shell, but each BOND provides 2 shared electrons, so 4 bonds give the 8.

Oxygen atoms repel each other and pair up to cancel the repulsion
Oxygen forms O₂ so that it can be a gas at room temperature

wrong_explanations.1: Bonding does not change an element's position in the periodic table; O₂ forms because of electron sharing.
wrong_explanations.2: Atoms of the same element do repel slightly, but covalent bonding (sharing) creates a stronger overall attraction.
wrong_explanations.3: Whether a substance is a gas depends on intermolecular forces, not on why the molecule forms.
