opts: A shared pair of electrons between two non-metal atoms, attracted to both nuclei
The transfer of electrons from a metal to a non-metal
A sea of delocalised electrons shared between metal ions
A weak attraction between two separate molecules

wrong_explanations.1: Transferring electrons is ionic bonding, which occurs between a metal and a non-metal.
wrong_explanations.2: A delocalised electron sea is the metallic bonding model.
wrong_explanations.3: That describes an intermolecular force, which acts BETWEEN molecules, not the bond within one.

quiz.q: State the number of single covalent bonds present in a water molecule, H₂O.

wrong_explanations.1: Oxygen forms 1 bond with each of the 2 hydrogen atoms, giving 2 bonds — not 1.
wrong_explanations.2: Water has only 2 hydrogen atoms, so only 2 O–H bonds form.
wrong_explanations.3: That would need 4 hydrogen atoms; water has 2.

quiz.q: Explain why a chlorine molecule (Cl₂) contains a single covalent bond.
opts: Each chlorine atom has 7 outer electrons and needs 1 more, so the two atoms share one pair, giving both a full shell of 8
Each chlorine atom shares three pairs of electrons
One chlorine atom transfers an electron to the other
The two atoms are held together by a sea of delocalised electrons

wrong_explanations.1: Sharing three pairs would be a triple bond; chlorine needs only 1 more electron, so it shares one pair.
wrong_explanations.2: Transferring an electron is ionic bonding; two identical non-metal atoms share instead.
wrong_explanations.3: A delocalised electron sea is metallic bonding; Cl₂ is a simple covalent molecule.

quiz.q: Explain, in terms of electrons, why a nitrogen molecule (N₂) contains a triple bond.
opts: Each nitrogen atom has 5 outer electrons and needs 3 more, so the two atoms share three pairs, giving both a full shell of 8
Nitrogen atoms share one pair of electrons
Each nitrogen atom transfers 3 electrons to the other
Nitrogen forms a triple bond because it is a gas

wrong_explanations.1: Sharing only one pair (a single bond) would leave each nitrogen short of a full shell.
wrong_explanations.2: Transfer is ionic bonding; two identical non-metal atoms share electrons instead.
wrong_explanations.3: Being a gas is a consequence of weak intermolecular forces, not the reason for the triple bond.

quiz.q: A dot-and-cross diagram of carbon dioxide shows O=C=O. Explain how this arrangement gives every atom a full outer shell.
opts: Carbon shares two pairs with each oxygen (two double bonds), reaching 8 electrons; each oxygen shares two pairs with carbon, also reaching 8
Carbon transfers 4 electrons to the two oxygen atoms
Each atom keeps its own electrons and none are shared
Carbon shares one pair with each oxygen, so all reach 8

wrong_explanations.1: CO₂ is covalent (all non-metals), so electrons are shared, not transferred.
wrong_explanations.2: If no electrons were shared, none of the atoms would reach a full shell.
wrong_explanations.3: Sharing only one pair with each oxygen would leave carbon with 6 electrons, not 8 — CO₂ needs double bonds.

quiz.q: Explain why simple molecular substances have low melting and boiling points, despite containing strong covalent bonds.
opts: Melting and boiling only overcome the weak intermolecular forces between whole molecules; the strong covalent bonds inside the molecules are not broken
The covalent bonds are actually very weak and break easily
The molecules lose their electrons when heated
Simple molecules have no forces between them at all

wrong_explanations.1: The covalent bonds are strong — that is why they stay intact when the substance melts.
wrong_explanations.2: No electrons are lost on melting; only the weak intermolecular forces are overcome.
wrong_explanations.3: There ARE forces between molecules (intermolecular forces) — they are just weak.

quiz.q: Compare a single, a double and a triple covalent bond in terms of the number of shared electrons.
opts: A single bond shares 2 electrons (1 pair), a double bond 4 electrons (2 pairs) and a triple bond 6 electrons (3 pairs)
A single bond shares 1 electron, a double 2 and a triple 3
All three share the same number of electrons but differ in strength only
A triple bond shares fewer electrons than a single bond

wrong_explanations.1: Each bond is a shared PAIR (2 electrons), so a single bond shares 2 electrons, not 1.
wrong_explanations.2: The number of shared electrons increases: 2, 4 then 6 — they are not equal.
wrong_explanations.3: A triple bond shares MORE electrons (6) than a single bond (2).

quiz.q: Ammonia is NH₃. Deduce the number of covalent bonds nitrogen forms, and explain why.
opts: 3 — nitrogen has 5 outer electrons and needs 3 more, so it forms 3 single bonds with 3 hydrogen atoms
5 — nitrogen forms one bond for each outer electron
1 — nitrogen forms a single bond only
8 — nitrogen needs 8 electrons so forms 8 bonds

wrong_explanations.1: Nitrogen bonds to gain electrons up to 8, not one bond per existing outer electron.
wrong_explanations.2: One bond would leave nitrogen well short of a full shell.
wrong_explanations.3: Nitrogen needs 8 electrons in its shell, but each bond provides 2 shared electrons, so it needs 3 bonds, not 8.

higher.common_mistake: Students often say that covalent bonds must be weak because substances like water have low boiling points. The covalent bonds themselves are actually very strong. The low melting and boiling points come from the weak intermolecular forces BETWEEN whole molecules — when the substance melts, those weak forces are overcome, not the strong covalent bonds inside each molecule.
higher.higher_box: Draw dot-and-cross diagrams for: H₂, Cl₂, HCl, O₂ (double bond), N₂ (triple bond), CH₄, H₂O, NH₃, CO₂. Show lone pairs on relevant atoms. Understand bond polarity in terms of electronegativity differences. The shared electrons are attracted to both nuclei — electrostatic attraction is the covalent bond.

bonding/metallic-bonding.summary: Describe the metallic bonding model and use it to explain the properties of metals.

quiz.q: Explain why metals are good conductors of electricity.
opts: They contain delocalised electrons that are free to move through the structure and carry charge
Their positive ions move through the metal to carry the current
They have more protons than non-metals, which conduct the current
The regular lattice lets electrical signals jump between fixed ions

wrong_explanations.1: The positive ions are held in the lattice; it is the mobile delocalised electrons that carry charge.
wrong_explanations.2: Proton number sets an element's identity, not its conductivity — many non-metals have lots of protons but do not conduct.
wrong_explanations.3: Conduction comes from the free electrons moving, not from the fixed ions.

quiz.q: Explain why steel is harder than pure iron.
opts: The added carbon atoms are a different size to the iron atoms, so they distort the regular lattice and stop the layers of ions sliding
Steel contains more electrons, which makes stronger metallic bonds
The carbon atoms fill the gaps, which increases the density and hardness
Steel is purer than iron, and purer metals are always harder

wrong_explanations.1: There may be more atoms overall, but the KEY reason is the disruption of the lattice by different-sized atoms.
wrong_explanations.2: Carbon does occupy spaces, but the hardening comes specifically from preventing the layers from sliding.
wrong_explanations.3: Steel is iron made LESS pure (carbon added); it is the added, differently-sized atoms that harden it.

quiz.q: Describe the structure of a metal according to the metallic bonding model.
opts: A regular lattice of positive metal ions surrounded by a sea of delocalised electrons
A giant lattice of alternating positive and negative ions
Separate metal molecules held by weak intermolecular forces
Neutral metal atoms sharing pairs of electrons in covalent bonds

wrong_explanations.1: Alternating positive and negative ions describes an IONIC lattice, not a metal.
wrong_explanations.2: Metals are giant structures, not separate molecules.
wrong_explanations.3: Metals do not share pairs in covalent bonds; their outer electrons are delocalised.

quiz.q: Explain why metals are malleable (can be hammered into shape).
opts: The layers of positive ions can slide over one another, and the electron sea re-surrounds them in their new positions without the bonding breaking
The metallic bonds break and then instantly reform as new bonds
The delocalised electrons push the ions apart to let them move
The ions are so small that they simply flow like a liquid

wrong_explanations.1: The bonding does not break — the electron sea keeps holding the ions together as the layers slide.
wrong_explanations.2: The electrons hold the ions together; they do not push them apart.
wrong_explanations.3: The ions stay in a solid lattice; they slide as layers rather than flowing freely.

quiz.q: Explain why metals have high melting points.
opts: There is strong electrostatic attraction between the many positive ions and the sea of delocalised electrons, so a lot of energy is needed to overcome it
Metals contain strong covalent bonds throughout
The delocalised electrons are very heavy
Metal atoms are simply packed together with no forces to overcome

wrong_explanations.1: Metals do not contain covalent bonds; the metallic bond is the ion-to-electron-sea attraction.
wrong_explanations.2: Electrons have almost no mass; the high melting point is due to the strength of the attraction, not electron mass.
wrong_explanations.3: There ARE strong forces — that is exactly why the melting point is high.

quiz.q: Name the particles that are free to move in a metal.
opts: Delocalised electrons
Positive ions
Neutrons
Whole atoms

wrong_explanations.1: The positive ions are fixed in the lattice; only the electrons are free to move.
wrong_explanations.2: Neutrons are locked in the nuclei and never move freely.
wrong_explanations.3: The atoms have become fixed positive ions; it is their outer electrons that are free.

quiz.q: State what is meant by an alloy.
opts: A mixture of a metal with one or more other elements
A very pure sample of a single metal
A compound of a metal and a non-metal
A metal that contains no electrons

wrong_explanations.1: A pure metal is the opposite of an alloy; an alloy is deliberately mixed.
wrong_explanations.2: An alloy is a MIXTURE, not a chemically bonded compound.
wrong_explanations.3: All metals contain delocalised electrons; that is not what defines an alloy.

quiz.q: Identify a pair of properties of metals that both result from metallic bonding.
opts: Good electrical conductivity and malleability
Being brittle and being an electrical insulator
Low melting point and low density in every case
Dissolving in water and conducting only when molten

wrong_explanations.1: Metals are malleable, not brittle, and they conduct rather than insulate.
wrong_explanations.2: Most metals have HIGH melting points and are dense.
wrong_explanations.3: Conducting only when molten describes ionic compounds, not metals (metals conduct as solids).

quiz.q: State the charge on the metal ions in the metallic bonding model.
opts: Positive
Negative
Neutral (no charge)
It changes constantly

wrong_explanations.1: The ions are positive because the atoms have LOST electrons into the sea.
wrong_explanations.2: If the ions were neutral there would be nothing for the electron sea to attract.
wrong_explanations.3: The ion charge is fixed at positive; it is the electrons that move.

quiz.q: Identify why alloys are generally harder than the pure metals they are made from.
opts: The different-sized atoms disrupt the regular lattice so the layers cannot slide as easily
Alloys contain many more delocalised electrons
Alloys are purer than the original metal
Alloys have no metallic bonding, so they are more rigid

wrong_explanations.1: The hardening is about lattice disruption, not the number of electrons.
wrong_explanations.2: Alloys are LESS pure (they are mixtures), which is exactly why they are harder.
wrong_explanations.3: Alloys still have metallic bonding — that is why they remain conductive and are still metals.

foundation.common_mistake: Students often say the metallic bond is an attraction between neighbouring metal atoms. It is not between atoms: the metal atoms lose their outer electrons to become positive ions, and the bond is the strong electrostatic attraction between those positive ions and the surrounding sea of delocalised electrons. That electron sea is also why metals bend rather than shatter — the layers of ions can slide while the sea holds everything together.

opts: They contain delocalised electrons that are free to move through the structure and carry charge
Their positive ions move through the metal to carry the current
They have more protons than non-metals, which conduct the current
The regular lattice lets electrical signals jump between fixed ions

wrong_explanations.1: The positive ions are held in the lattice; it is the mobile delocalised electrons that carry charge.
wrong_explanations.2: Proton number sets an element's identity, not its conductivity — many non-metals have lots of protons but do not conduct.
wrong_explanations.3: Conduction comes from the free electrons moving, not from the fixed ions.

quiz.q: Explain why steel is harder than pure iron.
opts: The added carbon atoms are a different size to the iron atoms, so they distort the regular lattice and stop the layers of ions sliding
Steel contains more electrons, which makes stronger metallic bonds
The carbon atoms fill the gaps, which increases the density and hardness
Steel is purer than iron, and purer metals are always harder

wrong_explanations.1: There may be more atoms overall, but the KEY reason is the disruption of the lattice by different-sized atoms.
wrong_explanations.2: Carbon does occupy spaces, but the hardening comes specifically from preventing the layers from sliding.
wrong_explanations.3: Steel is iron made LESS pure (carbon added); it is the added, differently-sized atoms that harden it.

quiz.q: Describe the structure of a metal according to the metallic bonding model.
opts: A regular lattice of positive metal ions surrounded by a sea of delocalised electrons
A giant lattice of alternating positive and negative ions
Separate metal molecules held by weak intermolecular forces
Neutral metal atoms sharing pairs of electrons in covalent bonds

wrong_explanations.1: Alternating positive and negative ions describes an IONIC lattice, not a metal.
wrong_explanations.2: Metals are giant structures, not separate molecules.
wrong_explanations.3: Metals do not share pairs in covalent bonds; their outer electrons are delocalised.

quiz.q: Explain why metals are malleable (can be hammered into shape).
opts: The layers of positive ions can slide over one another, and the electron sea re-surrounds them in their new positions without the bonding breaking
The metallic bonds break and then instantly reform as new bonds
The delocalised electrons push the ions apart to let them move
The ions are so small that they simply flow like a liquid

wrong_explanations.1: The bonding does not break — the electron sea keeps holding the ions together as the layers slide.
wrong_explanations.2: The electrons hold the ions together; they do not push them apart.
wrong_explanations.3: The ions stay in a solid lattice; they slide as layers rather than flowing freely.

quiz.q: Explain why metals have high melting points.
opts: There is strong electrostatic attraction between the many positive ions and the sea of delocalised electrons, so a lot of energy is needed to overcome it
Metals contain strong covalent bonds throughout
The delocalised electrons are very heavy
Metal atoms are simply packed together with no forces to overcome

wrong_explanations.1: Metals do not contain covalent bonds; the metallic bond is the ion-to-electron-sea attraction.
wrong_explanations.2: Electrons have almost no mass; the high melting point is due to the strength of the attraction, not electron mass.
wrong_explanations.3: There ARE strong forces — that is exactly why the melting point is high.

quiz.q: Explain why metals are good conductors of both electricity and heat.
opts: The delocalised electrons are free to move: they carry electric charge, and they also transfer kinetic (thermal) energy quickly through the structure
The positive ions vibrate and pass on both current and heat
The covalent bonds carry the current and the heat
Metals absorb heat and electricity because they are shiny

wrong_explanations.1: Ion vibration transfers some heat, but electrical current and most heat transfer are carried by the mobile electrons.
wrong_explanations.2: Metals have no covalent bonds; conduction is due to delocalised electrons.
wrong_explanations.3: Shininess is unrelated to conductivity.

quiz.q: Explain why an alloy such as bronze is harder than the pure copper it is mostly made from.
opts: The tin atoms are a different size to the copper atoms, distorting the regular layers so they cannot slide over each other as easily
Bronze contains stronger covalent bonds than copper
Tin is a gas that fills the gaps in the copper
Bronze has fewer delocalised electrons than copper

wrong_explanations.1: Metals and alloys have no covalent bonds; the hardening is due to lattice distortion.
wrong_explanations.2: Tin is a solid metal; it sits within the lattice as differently-sized atoms.
wrong_explanations.3: The number of electrons is not the reason — disrupting the layers is.

quiz.q: Compare metallic bonding with ionic bonding in terms of the particles present and whether the solid conducts electricity.
opts: A metal has positive ions in a sea of delocalised electrons and conducts as a solid; an ionic solid has fixed positive and negative ions and does not conduct until molten or dissolved
Both have delocalised electrons and both conduct as solids
Both have fixed ions and neither conducts as a solid
A metal has positive and negative ions; an ionic solid has an electron sea

wrong_explanations.1: Ionic solids have no delocalised electrons and do not conduct as solids, so they are not the same.
wrong_explanations.2: Metals DO conduct as solids (mobile electrons), so 'neither conducts' is wrong.
wrong_explanations.3: This swaps them over: the electron sea is in the METAL; positive and negative ions are in the IONIC solid.

quiz.q: Explain why metals can be drawn into wires (are ductile) without breaking.
opts: The layers of positive ions slide into new positions while the delocalised electron sea keeps them bonded, so the structure does not break
The metallic bonds are weak, so the metal stretches easily
The ions turn into a liquid as the wire is drawn
The electrons leave the metal, letting the ions move

wrong_explanations.1: The bonds are strong; ductility comes from the layers sliding while the electron sea maintains the bonding.
wrong_explanations.2: The metal stays solid while being drawn into a wire.
wrong_explanations.3: The electrons stay in the metal (that is what holds it together as it deforms).

quiz.q: Copper is used for electrical wiring. Suggest two properties of copper that make it suitable, and link each to metallic bonding.
opts: It conducts electricity well (delocalised electrons carry charge) and it is ductile (layers of ions slide, so it can be drawn into wires)
It is brittle (so it snaps to length) and it is an insulator (so it is safe)
It has a low melting point (so it melts in use) and it is magnetic
It is radioactive (so it carries current) and it is transparent

wrong_explanations.1: Brittleness and insulation would make copper useless for wiring — it is ductile and conductive.
wrong_explanations.2: A low melting point would be a disadvantage in a wire, and copper's suitability is not due to magnetism.
wrong_explanations.3: Copper is neither radioactive nor transparent, and neither would help it carry current.

higher.common_mistake: Students often say the metallic bond is an attraction between neighbouring metal atoms. It is not between atoms: the metal atoms lose their outer electrons to become positive ions, and the bond is the strong electrostatic attraction between those positive ions and the surrounding sea of delocalised electrons. That electron sea is also why metals bend rather than shatter — the layers of ions can slide while the sea holds everything together.

bonding/states-of-matter.summary: Describe the three states of matter, the changes between them and how to use state symbols.

quiz.q: Ice melts to form water. State whether this is a physical or a chemical change, and explain your answer.
opts: A physical change — the substance is still H₂O; only the arrangement of the particles changes
A chemical change — a new substance, water, is made from ice
A chemical change — heating must have broken chemical bonds
Neither — melting is not classed as physical or chemical

wrong_explanations.1: Ice and water are the same substance (H₂O) — water is not a new substance, just a different state.
wrong_explanations.2: Melting only overcomes the forces between the water molecules; the covalent O–H bonds are not broken.
wrong_explanations.3: A change of state IS a physical change, because the composition of the substance does not change.

quiz.q: Describe the arrangement and movement of the particles in a solid.
opts: Closely packed in a regular pattern, vibrating about fixed positions
Closely packed but able to move around and flow past each other
Far apart and moving quickly in all directions
Arranged in long chains that slide over one another

wrong_explanations.1: Particles that can move past each other and flow describes a LIQUID, not a solid.
wrong_explanations.2: Particles far apart moving quickly describes a GAS.
wrong_explanations.3: Sliding chains describe a polymer's structure, not the general particle model of a solid.

quiz.q: Name the change of state that occurs when a liquid turns into a gas.
opts: Boiling (or evaporation)
Condensation
Freezing
Melting

wrong_explanations.1: Condensation is the reverse — gas turning into liquid.
wrong_explanations.2: Freezing is liquid turning into solid.
wrong_explanations.3: Melting is solid turning into liquid.

quiz.q: In the equation NaCl(s) → Na⁺(?) + Cl⁻(?), state the symbol that shows the ions are dissolved in water.
opts: (aq)

wrong_explanations.1: (l) means a pure liquid, e.g. molten NaCl(l) or water H₂O(l) — not a substance dissolved in water.
wrong_explanations.2: (s) means solid; here the dissolved ions are free in solution, not a solid.
wrong_explanations.3: (g) means a gas; dissolved ions in water are not gaseous.

quiz.q: Explain, in terms of particles, why a gas can be compressed but a solid cannot.
opts: A gas has large spaces between its particles that can be pushed closer together; a solid's particles are already touching
A gas has heavier particles that squash more easily
A solid has no particles, so there is nothing to compress
A gas is warmer, and warm things always compress

wrong_explanations.1: Particle mass does not control compressibility — the spacing between particles does.
wrong_explanations.2: A solid is made of particles too; they are just packed closely with no space to compress into.
wrong_explanations.3: Temperature is not the reason — it is the spacing of the particles.

quiz.q: Name the three states of matter.
opts: Solid, liquid and gas
Solid, powder and gas
Ice, water and steam
Element, compound and mixture

wrong_explanations.1: A powder is just a finely divided solid, not a separate state of matter.
wrong_explanations.2: Ice, water and steam are the three states OF WATER specifically, not the general names.
wrong_explanations.3: Element, compound and mixture describe types of substance, not states of matter.

quiz.q: State what happens to the particles of a solid when it melts.
opts: They gain energy and break free from their fixed positions so they can move past each other
They lose energy and pack more tightly together
They are destroyed and new particles form
They stop moving completely

wrong_explanations.1: Melting adds energy, so the particles move more, not less; they do not pack tighter.
wrong_explanations.2: Melting is a physical change — no particles are destroyed or created.
wrong_explanations.3: Particles move MORE when a solid melts, not less.

quiz.q: Name the change of state from a gas to a liquid.
opts: Condensation
Boiling
Sublimation
Melting

wrong_explanations.1: Boiling is the reverse — liquid to gas.
wrong_explanations.2: Sublimation is solid changing directly to gas.
wrong_explanations.3: Melting is solid to liquid.

quiz.q: State the state symbol used for a pure liquid.
opts: (aq)

wrong_explanations.1: (aq) means dissolved in water, not a pure liquid.
wrong_explanations.2: (s) means solid.
wrong_explanations.3: (g) means gas.

quiz.q: Identify the state of matter in which the particles are far apart and move quickly in all directions.
opts: Gas
Solid
Liquid
A dissolved solid

wrong_explanations.1: In a solid the particles are packed closely and only vibrate in fixed positions.
wrong_explanations.2: In a liquid the particles are still close together, just able to flow.
wrong_explanations.3: A dissolved solid is spread through a liquid, but its particles are not far apart in a gas-like way.

foundation.common_mistake: Students often think that melting or boiling a substance must be a chemical change because something clearly happens. It is a physical change: the particles gain or lose energy and rearrange, but the substance itself is unchanged — ice, water and steam are all H₂O. (Remember too that (aq) means dissolved in water specifically, not just any liquid.)

quiz.q: Ice melts to form water. State whether this is a physical or a chemical change, and explain your answer.
opts: A physical change — the substance is still H₂O; only the arrangement of the particles changes
A chemical change — a new substance, water, is made from ice
A chemical change — heating must have broken chemical bonds
Neither — melting is not classed as physical or chemical

wrong_explanations.1: Ice and water are the same substance (H₂O) — water is not a new substance, just a different state.
wrong_explanations.2: Melting only overcomes the forces between the water molecules; the covalent O–H bonds are not broken.
wrong_explanations.3: A change of state IS a physical change, because the composition of the substance does not change.

quiz.q: Describe the arrangement and movement of the particles in a solid.
opts: Closely packed in a regular pattern, vibrating about fixed positions
Closely packed but able to move around and flow past each other
Far apart and moving quickly in all directions
Arranged in long chains that slide over one another

wrong_explanations.1: Particles that can move past each other and flow describes a LIQUID, not a solid.
wrong_explanations.2: Particles far apart moving quickly describes a GAS.
wrong_explanations.3: Sliding chains describe a polymer's structure, not the general particle model of a solid.

quiz.q: Name the change of state that occurs when a liquid turns into a gas.
opts: Boiling (or evaporation)
Condensation
Freezing
Melting

wrong_explanations.1: Condensation is the reverse — gas turning into liquid.
wrong_explanations.2: Freezing is liquid turning into solid.
wrong_explanations.3: Melting is solid turning into liquid.

quiz.q: In the equation NaCl(s) → Na⁺(?) + Cl⁻(?), state the symbol that shows the ions are dissolved in water.
opts: (aq)

wrong_explanations.1: (l) means a pure liquid, e.g. molten NaCl(l) or water H₂O(l) — not a substance dissolved in water.
wrong_explanations.2: (s) means solid; here the dissolved ions are free in solution, not a solid.
wrong_explanations.3: (g) means a gas; dissolved ions in water are not gaseous.

quiz.q: Explain, in terms of particles, why a gas can be compressed but a solid cannot.
opts: A gas has large spaces between its particles that can be pushed closer together; a solid's particles are already touching
A gas has heavier particles that squash more easily
A solid has no particles, so there is nothing to compress
A gas is warmer, and warm things always compress

wrong_explanations.1: Particle mass does not control compressibility — the spacing between particles does.
wrong_explanations.2: A solid is made of particles too; they are just packed closely with no space to compress into.
wrong_explanations.3: Temperature is not the reason — it is the spacing of the particles.

quiz.q: Explain, in terms of energy and forces, what happens to the particles when a liquid boils.
opts: The particles gain enough energy to overcome the forces holding them together and escape into the gas state
The particles lose energy and move closer together
The chemical bonds inside the particles break to release a gas
The particles shrink so they can float away

wrong_explanations.1: Boiling adds energy — the particles move more and separate, they do not lose energy or move closer.
wrong_explanations.2: Boiling overcomes the forces BETWEEN particles, not the bonds inside them (that would be a chemical change).
wrong_explanations.3: Particles do not shrink; they gain energy and move apart.

quiz.q: As a pure solid is heated until it melts, its temperature stays constant during melting even though heating continues. Explain why.
opts: The energy supplied is used to overcome the forces between the particles (to change the state), rather than to raise the temperature
The thermometer stops working at the melting point
No energy is being supplied during melting
The particles stop absorbing energy once melting begins

wrong_explanations.1: The thermometer works fine — the energy is going into the state change, not into heating.
wrong_explanations.2: Energy is still being supplied; it is being used to separate the particles.
wrong_explanations.3: The particles keep absorbing energy — it is used to break the structure apart, not to warm it.

quiz.q: The particle model represents particles as small solid spheres. Suggest one limitation of this model.
opts: It treats particles as solid, identical spheres and ignores the forces between them (and their real shapes and sizes)
It shows that particles move, which is incorrect
It proves that all substances are the same
It cannot be used to explain changes of state

wrong_explanations.1: Particles really do move — that part of the model is correct, so it is not a limitation.
wrong_explanations.2: The model actually shows substances differ (different arrangements); it does not claim they are the same.
wrong_explanations.3: The model IS used to explain changes of state; that is one of its strengths.

quiz.q: Explain why a change of state is classed as a physical change and not a chemical change.
opts: No new substance is made — the same particles are simply rearranged, and the change can be reversed
A new substance is always produced, but it looks the same
Chemical bonds within the particles are broken and remade
Energy is released, which defines a physical change

wrong_explanations.1: If a new substance were made it would be a chemical change; in a change of state the substance is unchanged.
wrong_explanations.2: The bonds inside the particles are not broken — only the forces between particles change.
wrong_explanations.3: Energy transfer alone does not define the type of change; whether a new substance forms does.

quiz.q: Predict what happens to the water particles when steam condenses on a cold window, in terms of their energy and movement.
opts: They lose energy, slow down, and the forces between them pull them together into a liquid
They gain energy and spread further apart
They turn into a new chemical substance
They break apart into hydrogen and oxygen

wrong_explanations.1: Condensation removes energy — the particles slow down and come together, they do not gain energy or spread out.
wrong_explanations.2: Condensation is a physical change; steam and liquid water are the same substance.
wrong_explanations.3: The molecules stay as H₂O; the covalent bonds do not break.

higher.common_mistake: Students often think that melting or boiling a substance must be a chemical change because something clearly happens. It is a physical change: the particles gain or lose energy and rearrange, but the substance itself is unchanged — ice, water and steam are all H₂O. (Remember too that (aq) means dissolved in water specifically, not just any liquid.)

bonding/properties-ionic-compounds.summary: Explain the properties of ionic compounds in terms of their giant lattice structure.

quiz.q: A student tests whether sodium chloride conducts electricity in four forms: solid, dissolved in water, molten, and as a vapour. Predict in which forms it conducts, and explain why.
opts: Dissolved in water and molten — in both the ions are free to move and carry charge
Only as a solid — the lattice provides a path for the electrons
In all four forms — sodium chloride always contains ions
Only when dissolved — the water is what carries the current

wrong_explanations.1: Solid NaCl has ions but they are FIXED — they cannot move to carry charge, so it does not conduct as a solid.
wrong_explanations.2: Having ions is not enough; they must be free to move. In the solid (and vapour) they cannot carry a current.
wrong_explanations.3: Molten NaCl also conducts — water is not required. Free ions from melting OR dissolving both allow conduction.

quiz.q: Explain why ionic compounds conduct electricity when molten or dissolved, but not when solid.
opts: When molten or dissolved the ions are free to move and carry charge; in the solid they are held in fixed positions
Melting or dissolving creates ions that were not there in the solid
Molten ionic compounds conduct using delocalised electrons
Solid ionic compounds have no charged particles

wrong_explanations.1: The ions already exist in the solid — melting or dissolving frees them to move, it does not create them.
wrong_explanations.2: Ionic conduction is by moving IONS, not delocalised electrons (that is metals).
wrong_explanations.3: Solid ionic compounds do contain charged ions; they simply cannot move.

quiz.q: Explain why ionic compounds have high melting and boiling points.
opts: There are many strong electrostatic attractions between the oppositely charged ions throughout the giant lattice, needing a lot of energy to overcome
The crystals are large, so more heat is needed
Strong covalent bonds throughout the lattice must be broken
The ions are very heavy and resist heating

wrong_explanations.1: Crystal size does not set the melting point; the strength of the ionic bonding does.
wrong_explanations.2: Ionic compounds have ionic bonds (ion-to-ion attraction), not covalent bonds.
wrong_explanations.3: Ion mass is not the reason; the strength of the electrostatic attraction is.

quiz.q: Ionic compounds are hard but brittle. Explain why a crystal shatters when it is struck.
opts: The blow shifts a layer of ions so like charges line up next to each other; they repel and the crystal splits apart
The covalent bonds in the crystal snap
The ions melt at the point of impact
The delocalised electrons are knocked out of place

wrong_explanations.1: Ionic compounds contain no covalent bonds — they are held by ionic (electrostatic) attraction.
wrong_explanations.2: Shattering is caused by ion repulsion when layers shift, not by melting.
wrong_explanations.3: Ionic compounds have no delocalised electrons; that is a metallic feature.

quiz.q: Describe the type of structure that is responsible for the properties of ionic compounds.
opts: A giant ionic lattice — a regular 3D arrangement of oppositely charged ions with strong electrostatic forces in all directions
A small molecule of a few ions held weakly together
A lattice of positive ions in a sea of delocalised electrons
Long chains of ions joined by weak intermolecular forces

wrong_explanations.1: Ionic compounds are giant lattices, not small molecules.
wrong_explanations.2: That is the metallic model; ionic compounds have positive AND negative ions, no electron sea.
wrong_explanations.3: That describes a polymer-like arrangement, not an ionic lattice.

quiz.q: State whether ionic compounds are usually solids, liquids or gases at room temperature.
opts: Solids
Liquids
Gases
They are usually a mixture of all three

wrong_explanations.1: Their high melting points (strong lattice forces) mean they are solid at room temperature.
wrong_explanations.2: Ionic compounds have high melting points, so they are not liquids at room temperature.
wrong_explanations.3: They are solid at room temperature, not a mix of states.

quiz.q: State two ways of making an ionic compound conduct electricity.
opts: Melt it, or dissolve it in water
Cool it, or crush it into powder
Keep it solid, or dissolve it
Melt it, or keep it solid

wrong_explanations.1: Cooling keeps the ions fixed; crushing a solid still leaves the ions unable to move.
wrong_explanations.2: A solid ionic compound does not conduct — only the dissolved (and molten) states do.
wrong_explanations.3: The solid state does not conduct; melting is one valid way, dissolving is the other.

quiz.q: State whether ionic compounds are malleable or brittle.
opts: Brittle
Malleable
Both equally
Neither — they are always liquid

wrong_explanations.1: Malleability is a property of metals; ionic compounds shatter when struck.
wrong_explanations.2: Ionic compounds are clearly brittle, not malleable.
wrong_explanations.3: Ionic compounds are solid at room temperature, not liquid.

quiz.q: Identify the reason solid ionic compounds do not conduct electricity.
opts: The ions are fixed in position in the lattice and cannot move
There are no ions present in the solid
The ions have lost their charge
The solid contains only neutral molecules

wrong_explanations.1: The ions are present in the solid lattice; they simply cannot move.
wrong_explanations.2: The ions keep their charges in the solid.
wrong_explanations.3: Ionic solids are made of charged ions, not neutral molecules.

quiz.q: State what happens to the melting point of an ionic compound as the charges on its ions increase.
opts: It increases, because the electrostatic forces between the ions become stronger
It decreases, because higher charges repel
It stays the same, because charge does not affect melting point
It becomes zero

wrong_explanations.1: Higher charges give stronger attraction between the ions, not repulsion — melting point rises.
wrong_explanations.2: Charge strongly affects melting point; higher charge → stronger forces → higher melting point.
wrong_explanations.3: The melting point does not fall to zero; it rises with higher ionic charge.

foundation.common_mistake: Students often say that ionic solutions conduct electricity because electrons flow through them, just like in a metal. They do not: ionic compounds have no free electrons. They conduct only when molten or dissolved, and it is the ions themselves that move and carry the charge. (This is also why ionic compounds are brittle rather than bendy — shifting the lattice brings like charges together, and they repel.)

quiz.q: A student tests whether sodium chloride conducts electricity in four forms: solid, dissolved in water, molten, and as a vapour. Predict in which forms it conducts, and explain why.
opts: Dissolved in water and molten — in both the ions are free to move and carry charge
Only as a solid — the lattice provides a path for the electrons
In all four forms — sodium chloride always contains ions
Only when dissolved — the water is what carries the current

wrong_explanations.1: Solid NaCl has ions but they are FIXED — they cannot move to carry charge, so it does not conduct as a solid.
wrong_explanations.2: Having ions is not enough; they must be free to move. In the solid (and vapour) they cannot carry a current.
wrong_explanations.3: Molten NaCl also conducts — water is not required. Free ions from melting OR dissolving both allow conduction.

quiz.q: Explain why ionic compounds conduct electricity when molten or dissolved, but not when solid.
opts: When molten or dissolved the ions are free to move and carry charge; in the solid they are held in fixed positions
Melting or dissolving creates ions that were not there in the solid
Molten ionic compounds conduct using delocalised electrons
Solid ionic compounds have no charged particles

wrong_explanations.1: The ions already exist in the solid — melting or dissolving frees them to move, it does not create them.
wrong_explanations.2: Ionic conduction is by moving IONS, not delocalised electrons (that is metals).
wrong_explanations.3: Solid ionic compounds do contain charged ions; they simply cannot move.

quiz.q: Explain why ionic compounds have high melting and boiling points.
opts: There are many strong electrostatic attractions between the oppositely charged ions throughout the giant lattice, needing a lot of energy to overcome
The crystals are large, so more heat is needed
Strong covalent bonds throughout the lattice must be broken
The ions are very heavy and resist heating

wrong_explanations.1: Crystal size does not set the melting point; the strength of the ionic bonding does.
wrong_explanations.2: Ionic compounds have ionic bonds (ion-to-ion attraction), not covalent bonds.
wrong_explanations.3: Ion mass is not the reason; the strength of the electrostatic attraction is.

quiz.q: Ionic compounds are hard but brittle. Explain why a crystal shatters when it is struck.
opts: The blow shifts a layer of ions so like charges line up next to each other; they repel and the crystal splits apart
The covalent bonds in the crystal snap
The ions melt at the point of impact
The delocalised electrons are knocked out of place

wrong_explanations.1: Ionic compounds contain no covalent bonds — they are held by ionic (electrostatic) attraction.
wrong_explanations.2: Shattering is caused by ion repulsion when layers shift, not by melting.
wrong_explanations.3: Ionic compounds have no delocalised electrons; that is a metallic feature.

quiz.q: Describe the type of structure that is responsible for the properties of ionic compounds.
opts: A giant ionic lattice — a regular 3D arrangement of oppositely charged ions with strong electrostatic forces in all directions
A small molecule of a few ions held weakly together
A lattice of positive ions in a sea of delocalised electrons
Long chains of ions joined by weak intermolecular forces

wrong_explanations.1: Ionic compounds are giant lattices, not small molecules.
wrong_explanations.2: That is the metallic model; ionic compounds have positive AND negative ions, no electron sea.
wrong_explanations.3: That describes a polymer-like arrangement, not an ionic lattice.

quiz.q: Magnesium oxide (MgO) has a higher melting point than sodium chloride (NaCl). Explain why.
opts: Mg²⁺ and O²⁻ carry 2+/2− charges, larger than the 1+/1− of Na⁺ and Cl⁻, so the electrostatic forces between the ions are stronger and need more energy to overcome
Magnesium oxide has a larger crystal lattice, so more energy is needed
Magnesium oxide has more ions per formula unit than sodium chloride
Magnesium is heavier than sodium, which raises the melting point

wrong_explanations.1: Crystal size is not the determining factor — both are giant lattices; the strength of each ion-ion attraction matters.
wrong_explanations.2: Both MgO and NaCl are 1:1 — neither has more ions per formula unit.
wrong_explanations.3: Atomic mass does not set the melting point; the ionic charge does.

quiz.q: Explain the difference between how a metal conducts electricity and how molten sodium chloride conducts electricity.
opts: A metal conducts using delocalised electrons that move; molten sodium chloride conducts using its ions that move
Both conduct using delocalised electrons
Both conduct using moving ions
A metal conducts using ions; molten sodium chloride uses electrons

wrong_explanations.1: Only the metal uses delocalised electrons; molten NaCl uses moving ions, not electrons.
wrong_explanations.2: The metal does not conduct by moving ions — its ions are fixed; the electrons move.
wrong_explanations.3: This swaps them over: metals use electrons, molten ionic compounds use ions.

quiz.q: Predict which has the higher melting point, sodium chloride (NaCl) or sodium oxide (Na₂O), and justify your answer using ionic charge.
opts: Sodium oxide — its O²⁻ ions carry a 2− charge compared with Cl⁻ at 1−, giving stronger forces and a higher melting point
Sodium chloride — because it contains chlorine, which is heavier than oxygen
They are equal, because both contain sodium ions
Sodium oxide — because it has more atoms in its formula

wrong_explanations.1: Atomic mass is not the key factor; the O²⁻ charge (2−) creates stronger attraction than Cl⁻ (1−).
wrong_explanations.2: Having the same positive ion does not make the melting points equal — the negative ion's charge differs.
wrong_explanations.3: The higher melting point comes from the higher ionic charge (O²⁻), not simply from more atoms.

quiz.q: Explain why the melting point of an ionic compound gives information about the strength of the forces between its ions.
opts: A higher melting point means more energy is needed to separate the ions, which shows the electrostatic forces holding them are stronger
A higher melting point means the ions are lighter
The melting point depends only on the size of the crystal
The melting point is unrelated to the forces between ions

wrong_explanations.1: Ion mass is not what the melting point reveals; it reflects the strength of the attraction.
wrong_explanations.2: Crystal size does not set the melting point; the strength of the ionic forces does.
wrong_explanations.3: The melting point is directly linked to the strength of the forces between the ions.

quiz.q: Suggest why magnesium oxide is used to line the inside of furnaces.
opts: Its 2+/2− ions give very strong ionic bonding and a very high melting point, so it can withstand the high temperatures without melting
It is a good electrical conductor when solid
It has a low melting point, so it melts to seal the furnace
It is a soft, flexible material that absorbs heat

wrong_explanations.1: Solid MgO does not conduct electricity (fixed ions); that is not why it is used to line furnaces.
wrong_explanations.2: A low melting point would be useless in a furnace; MgO is chosen for its VERY HIGH melting point.
wrong_explanations.3: MgO is hard and brittle, not soft and flexible; it is chosen for heat resistance.

higher.common_mistake: Students often say that ionic solutions conduct electricity because electrons flow through them, just like in a metal. They do not: ionic compounds have no free electrons. They conduct only when molten or dissolved, and it is the ions themselves that move and carry the charge. (This is also why ionic compounds are brittle rather than bendy — shifting the lattice brings like charges together, and they repel.)

bonding/properties-small-molecules.summary: Explain why simple molecular substances have low melting points and do not conduct electricity.

quiz.q: Water (H₂O) boils at 100°C but methane (CH₄) boils at −161°C, although both are simple molecular. Explain why the difference is so large.
opts: Water molecules have stronger intermolecular forces than methane molecules, so more energy is needed to separate them
Water has more covalent bonds, so it takes more energy to break them
Methane is lighter, so it simply evaporates faster
Water contains oxygen, which makes the molecules too heavy to boil easily

wrong_explanations.1: Boiling does not break covalent bonds — water has 2 and methane has 4, so bond count is not the reason.
wrong_explanations.2: Lighter molecules can evaporate faster, but boiling point is set by the strength of the intermolecular forces.
wrong_explanations.3: The key reason is the stronger intermolecular forces in water, not simply the mass of the molecules.

quiz.q: Explain why simple molecular substances do not conduct electricity.
opts: They have no free electrons or ions — there are no charged particles that can move to carry a current
Their covalent bonds are too strong to let electrons move
Their molecules are too small to hold a charge
Simple molecules are always gases, and gases never conduct

wrong_explanations.1: Conduction needs free charged particles, not weaker bonds — even weak bonds would not help without free charges.
wrong_explanations.2: Molecular size is not the point; metals are large structures and conduct because of free electrons.
wrong_explanations.3: Some gases can conduct (e.g. when ionised), and simple molecular substances can be solids or liquids too.

quiz.q: Explain why simple molecular substances have low melting and boiling points.
opts: The forces between the molecules (intermolecular forces) are weak, so only a little energy is needed to separate them
The covalent bonds within the molecules are very weak
The molecules lose electrons easily when warmed
There are no forces at all between the molecules

wrong_explanations.1: The covalent bonds within molecules are strong; it is the forces between molecules that are weak.
wrong_explanations.2: No electrons are lost on melting; the weak intermolecular forces are simply overcome.
wrong_explanations.3: There ARE forces between molecules (intermolecular forces) — they are just weak.

quiz.q: State what is overcome when a simple molecular substance boils.
opts: The weak intermolecular forces between the molecules
The strong covalent bonds within the molecules
The ionic bonds between the molecules
The metallic bonds holding the molecules together

wrong_explanations.1: The strong covalent bonds inside the molecules are NOT broken on boiling.
wrong_explanations.2: Simple molecular substances contain no ionic bonds.
wrong_explanations.3: There are no metallic bonds in a simple molecular substance.

quiz.q: Describe what a simple molecular substance is.
opts: A substance made of small molecules, each with a fixed number of atoms joined by strong covalent bonds
A giant lattice of ions held by electrostatic forces
A giant structure of atoms all joined by covalent bonds
A metal made of positive ions in a sea of electrons

wrong_explanations.1: A giant ionic lattice describes an ionic compound, not a simple molecular substance.
wrong_explanations.2: A giant covalent structure (like diamond) is different — its covalent bonds run throughout, with no small molecules.
wrong_explanations.3: That describes a metal, not a molecular substance.

quiz.q: State whether simple molecular substances have high or low melting points.
opts: Low
High
Always exactly 100°C
They never melt

wrong_explanations.1: Simple molecular substances have LOW melting points because of weak intermolecular forces.
wrong_explanations.2: 100°C is the boiling point of water specifically, not a general rule.
wrong_explanations.3: They do melt — easily, in fact, at low temperatures.

quiz.q: State whether simple molecular substances conduct electricity.
opts: No
Yes, always
Only when solid
Only as a metal

wrong_explanations.1: They have no free charged particles, so they do not conduct.
wrong_explanations.2: They do not conduct in any state (no free electrons or ions).
wrong_explanations.3: Simple molecular substances are not metals and do not conduct.

quiz.q: Name the type of force that must be overcome to melt a simple molecular substance.
opts: Intermolecular forces
Covalent bonds
Ionic bonds
Metallic bonds

wrong_explanations.1: Covalent bonds are within molecules and are not broken on melting.
wrong_explanations.2: There are no ionic bonds in a simple molecular substance.
wrong_explanations.3: There are no metallic bonds in a simple molecular substance.

quiz.q: Identify which of these is a simple molecular substance.
opts: Carbon dioxide
Sodium chloride
Copper
Diamond

wrong_explanations.1: Sodium chloride is a giant ionic lattice, not a simple molecule.
wrong_explanations.2: Copper is a metal with a giant metallic structure.
wrong_explanations.3: Diamond is a giant covalent structure, not a simple molecule.

quiz.q: State whether the covalent bonds inside the molecules break when a simple molecular substance melts.
opts: No — only the forces between the molecules are overcome
Yes — the covalent bonds break to let it melt
Yes — half of the covalent bonds break
Only if the substance is heated very slowly

wrong_explanations.1: The strong covalent bonds stay intact; melting overcomes the weak forces between molecules.
wrong_explanations.2: No covalent bonds break on melting, not even some of them.
wrong_explanations.3: The heating rate does not change which forces are overcome; the covalent bonds stay intact.

foundation.common_mistake: Students often say that simple molecular substances melt easily because their covalent bonds are weak. The covalent bonds inside each molecule are actually strong. What is weak are the intermolecular forces BETWEEN the separate molecules — melting or boiling overcomes those and leaves the covalent bonds untouched, which is why the melting point is low.

quiz.q: Water (H₂O) boils at 100°C but methane (CH₄) boils at −161°C, although both are simple molecular. Explain why the difference is so large.
opts: Water molecules have stronger intermolecular forces than methane molecules, so more energy is needed to separate them
Water has more covalent bonds, so it takes more energy to break them
Methane is lighter, so it simply evaporates faster
Water contains oxygen, which makes the molecules too heavy to boil easily

wrong_explanations.1: Boiling does not break covalent bonds — water has 2 and methane has 4, so bond count is not the reason.
wrong_explanations.2: Lighter molecules can evaporate faster, but boiling point is set by the strength of the intermolecular forces.
wrong_explanations.3: The key reason is the stronger intermolecular forces in water, not simply the mass of the molecules.

quiz.q: Explain why simple molecular substances do not conduct electricity.
opts: They have no free electrons or ions — there are no charged particles that can move to carry a current
Their covalent bonds are too strong to let electrons move
Their molecules are too small to hold a charge
Simple molecules are always gases, and gases never conduct

wrong_explanations.1: Conduction needs free charged particles, not weaker bonds — even weak bonds would not help without free charges.
wrong_explanations.2: Molecular size is not the point; metals are large structures and conduct because of free electrons.
wrong_explanations.3: Some gases can conduct (e.g. when ionised), and simple molecular substances can be solids or liquids too.

quiz.q: Explain why simple molecular substances have low melting and boiling points.
opts: The forces between the molecules (intermolecular forces) are weak, so only a little energy is needed to separate them
The covalent bonds within the molecules are very weak
The molecules lose electrons easily when warmed
There are no forces at all between the molecules

wrong_explanations.1: The covalent bonds within molecules are strong; it is the forces between molecules that are weak.
wrong_explanations.2: No electrons are lost on melting; the weak intermolecular forces are simply overcome.
wrong_explanations.3: There ARE forces between molecules (intermolecular forces) — they are just weak.

quiz.q: State what is overcome when a simple molecular substance boils.
opts: The weak intermolecular forces between the molecules
The strong covalent bonds within the molecules
The ionic bonds between the molecules
The metallic bonds holding the molecules together

wrong_explanations.1: The strong covalent bonds inside the molecules are NOT broken on boiling.
wrong_explanations.2: Simple molecular substances contain no ionic bonds.
wrong_explanations.3: There are no metallic bonds in a simple molecular substance.

quiz.q: Describe what a simple molecular substance is.
opts: A substance made of small molecules, each with a fixed number of atoms joined by strong covalent bonds
A giant lattice of ions held by electrostatic forces
A giant structure of atoms all joined by covalent bonds
A metal made of positive ions in a sea of electrons

wrong_explanations.1: A giant ionic lattice describes an ionic compound, not a simple molecular substance.
wrong_explanations.2: A giant covalent structure (like diamond) is different — its covalent bonds run throughout, with no small molecules.
wrong_explanations.3: That describes a metal, not a molecular substance.

quiz.q: Explain why larger molecules generally have higher melting and boiling points than smaller molecules.
opts: Larger molecules have more electrons, giving stronger intermolecular forces, so more energy is needed to separate them
Larger molecules have stronger covalent bonds inside them
Larger molecules are always ionic
Larger molecules contain free electrons that raise the melting point

wrong_explanations.1: The covalent bonds inside are not what melts; it is the intermolecular forces, which grow with molecule size.
wrong_explanations.2: Larger molecular substances are still covalent/molecular, not ionic.
wrong_explanations.3: Simple molecular substances have no free electrons; the higher melting point is due to stronger intermolecular forces.

quiz.q: Explain why melting a simple molecular substance does not break its covalent bonds.
opts: Melting only needs enough energy to overcome the weak intermolecular forces between molecules, which is far less than the energy needed to break the strong covalent bonds
The covalent bonds are weaker than the intermolecular forces
The molecules gain electrons that protect the covalent bonds
The covalent bonds are shared out among all the molecules

wrong_explanations.1: Covalent bonds are much STRONGER than intermolecular forces, so melting overcomes the weak forces only.
wrong_explanations.2: No electrons are gained on melting.
wrong_explanations.3: Covalent bonds stay within each molecule; they are not shared between molecules.

quiz.q: Many simple molecular substances are gases at room temperature, but iodine is a solid. Suggest why iodine is a solid.
opts: Iodine molecules are relatively large, so the intermolecular forces between them are strong enough to hold them as a solid at room temperature
Iodine has stronger covalent bonds than other molecules
Iodine is an ionic compound
Iodine molecules contain free electrons

wrong_explanations.1: The covalent bond strength inside I₂ is not the reason; it is the stronger intermolecular forces between larger molecules.
wrong_explanations.2: Iodine (I₂) is a simple molecular substance, not ionic.
wrong_explanations.3: Iodine has no free electrons; its solid state is due to intermolecular forces.

quiz.q: A substance has a low melting point and does not conduct electricity in any state. Deduce its structure and bonding.
opts: Simple molecular, with covalent bonding — low melting point (weak intermolecular forces) and no charged particles to conduct
Ionic — it has a low melting point and free ions
Metallic — it does not conduct because the electrons are fixed
Giant covalent — low melting point from weak covalent bonds

wrong_explanations.1: Ionic compounds have HIGH melting points and conduct when molten — this does neither.
wrong_explanations.2: Metals conduct (delocalised electrons) and usually have high melting points.
wrong_explanations.3: Giant covalent structures have very HIGH melting points, not low ones.

quiz.q: Explain why the melting point of a simple molecular substance tells you nothing about the strength of its covalent bonds.
opts: Melting overcomes only the weak intermolecular forces between molecules; the strong covalent bonds inside the molecules are not involved
Because the covalent bonds break at exactly the melting point
Because covalent bonds have no strength to measure
Because the melting point measures the ionic bonds instead

wrong_explanations.1: The covalent bonds are not broken on melting, so the melting point cannot tell you their strength.
wrong_explanations.2: Covalent bonds are strong and definitely have a measurable strength — just not one shown by the melting point.
wrong_explanations.3: There are no ionic bonds in a simple molecular substance.

higher.common_mistake: Students often say that simple molecular substances melt easily because their covalent bonds are weak. The covalent bonds inside each molecule are actually strong. What is weak are the intermolecular forces BETWEEN the separate molecules — melting or boiling overcomes those and leaves the covalent bonds untouched, which is why the melting point is low.

bonding/polymers.summary: Describe the structure of polymers and explain their properties.

quiz.q: Explain why polymers have much lower melting points than giant covalent structures such as diamond.
opts: A polymer is made of separate large molecules held together by weak intermolecular forces, while diamond has strong covalent bonds throughout the whole structure
Polymers contain fewer atoms than diamond, so there is less to heat
Diamond has ionic bonds, which are stronger than the covalent bonds in polymers
Polymers are man-made, and natural structures are always stronger

wrong_explanations.1: A single polymer chain can contain millions of atoms — the difference is the TYPE of forces, not the number of atoms.
wrong_explanations.2: Diamond is entirely covalent, with no ionic bonds; its strong covalent network gives the high melting point.
wrong_explanations.3: Whether a material is natural or synthetic does not set its melting point; structure and bonding do.

quiz.q: Ethene (CH₂=CH₂) can be used to make poly(ethene), but ethane (C₂H₆) cannot. Explain why.
opts: Ethene has a C=C double bond that can open up so the monomers join together; ethane is saturated (only single bonds), so it cannot join in this way
Ethane is too heavy a molecule to react
Ethane contains no carbon, so it cannot form a carbon chain
Ethene is an ionic compound, which is needed for polymerisation

wrong_explanations.1: Molecular mass is not the barrier; ethane simply has no C=C double bond to open up.
wrong_explanations.2: Ethane (C₂H₆) does contain carbon — it just has no double bond to enable addition polymerisation.
wrong_explanations.3: Ethene is a covalent molecule, not ionic; the double bond is what matters.

quiz.q: Describe how a polymer forms from monomers.
opts: Many small monomer molecules join together to form one long chain molecule
A single large molecule breaks apart into many small ones
Two atoms share electrons to form one molecule
Positive and negative ions attract to form a lattice

wrong_explanations.1: Polymerisation joins monomers together; it does not break a molecule apart.
wrong_explanations.2: A single shared pair between two atoms is one covalent bond, not polymerisation.
wrong_explanations.3: That describes ionic bonding, not the formation of a polymer.

quiz.q: Explain why poly(ethene) is a solid at room temperature.
opts: Its molecules are very long chains, so there is enough intermolecular force between them to hold it as a solid
Its covalent bonds are ionic and therefore strong
It contains a giant lattice of ions
It has delocalised electrons that lock it in place

wrong_explanations.1: Poly(ethene) has covalent, not ionic, bonds; its solid state is due to intermolecular forces between long chains.
wrong_explanations.2: Poly(ethene) is molecular, not a giant ionic lattice.
wrong_explanations.3: Poly(ethene) has no delocalised electrons; it is an insulator.

quiz.q: State what is meant by a monomer.
opts: A small molecule that can join with many others to form a polymer
The long chain molecule made when many small molecules join
A single atom that makes up a polymer
A mixture of two different plastics

wrong_explanations.1: The long chain is the POLYMER; the monomer is the small starting molecule.
wrong_explanations.2: A monomer is a molecule, not a single atom.
wrong_explanations.3: A monomer is a pure substance (one type of molecule), not a mixture.

quiz.q: State what a polymer is.
opts: A very large molecule made of many repeating units
A small molecule with only a few atoms
A giant lattice of ions
A pure metal

wrong_explanations.1: Polymers are large, not small, molecules.
wrong_explanations.2: Polymers are molecular, not ionic lattices.
wrong_explanations.3: Polymers are non-metals, not metals.

quiz.q: Name the small molecules that join together to make a polymer.
opts: Monomers
Polymers
Isotopes
Alloys

wrong_explanations.1: The polymer is the final long chain; the small starting molecules are monomers.
wrong_explanations.2: Isotopes are atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons — unrelated.
wrong_explanations.3: An alloy is a mixture of metals, not a building block of a polymer.

quiz.q: State whether polymers conduct electricity.
opts: No — they are insulators
Yes — like metals
Only when solid
Only when molten

wrong_explanations.1: Polymers have no free electrons or ions, so they are insulators.
wrong_explanations.2: Polymers do not conduct in any state — they have no free charged particles.
wrong_explanations.3: Melting a polymer does not give it free charged particles, so it still does not conduct.

quiz.q: Identify the monomer used to make poly(ethene).
opts: Ethene
Ethane
Ethanol
Methane

wrong_explanations.1: Ethane is saturated (no C=C), so it cannot undergo addition polymerisation.
wrong_explanations.2: Ethanol is an alcohol and does not form poly(ethene).
wrong_explanations.3: Methane has only one carbon and no double bond, so it cannot form poly(ethene).

quiz.q: State the type of bond in a monomer that allows addition polymerisation to happen.
opts: A carbon–carbon double bond (C=C)
A single carbon–carbon bond
An ionic bond
A metallic bond

wrong_explanations.1: A single C–C bond cannot open up to join monomers; a C=C double bond is needed.
wrong_explanations.2: Monomers are covalent molecules, not ionic.
wrong_explanations.3: Monomers contain no metallic bonding.

foundation.common_mistake: Students often call polymers giant covalent structures because the chains are so large. They are not: a polymer is a very large molecule, and the chains are held to each other only by weak intermolecular forces. In a giant covalent structure such as diamond, strong covalent bonds run all the way through — which is why diamond's melting point is far higher than any polymer's.

quiz.q: Explain why polymers have much lower melting points than giant covalent structures such as diamond.
opts: A polymer is made of separate large molecules held together by weak intermolecular forces, while diamond has strong covalent bonds throughout the whole structure
Polymers contain fewer atoms than diamond, so there is less to heat
Diamond has ionic bonds, which are stronger than the covalent bonds in polymers
Polymers are man-made, and natural structures are always stronger

wrong_explanations.1: A single polymer chain can contain millions of atoms — the difference is the TYPE of forces, not the number of atoms.
wrong_explanations.2: Diamond is entirely covalent, with no ionic bonds; its strong covalent network gives the high melting point.
wrong_explanations.3: Whether a material is natural or synthetic does not set its melting point; structure and bonding do.

quiz.q: Ethene (CH₂=CH₂) can be used to make poly(ethene), but ethane (C₂H₆) cannot. Explain why.
opts: Ethene has a C=C double bond that can open up so the monomers join together; ethane is saturated (only single bonds), so it cannot join in this way
Ethane is too heavy a molecule to react
Ethane contains no carbon, so it cannot form a carbon chain
Ethene is an ionic compound, which is needed for polymerisation

wrong_explanations.1: Molecular mass is not the barrier; ethane simply has no C=C double bond to open up.
wrong_explanations.2: Ethane (C₂H₆) does contain carbon — it just has no double bond to enable addition polymerisation.
wrong_explanations.3: Ethene is a covalent molecule, not ionic; the double bond is what matters.

quiz.q: Describe how a polymer forms from monomers.
opts: Many small monomer molecules join together to form one long chain molecule
A single large molecule breaks apart into many small ones
Two atoms share electrons to form one molecule
Positive and negative ions attract to form a lattice

wrong_explanations.1: Polymerisation joins monomers together; it does not break a molecule apart.
wrong_explanations.2: A single shared pair between two atoms is one covalent bond, not polymerisation.
wrong_explanations.3: That describes ionic bonding, not the formation of a polymer.

quiz.q: Explain why poly(ethene) is a solid at room temperature.
opts: Its molecules are very long chains, so there is enough intermolecular force between them to hold it as a solid
Its covalent bonds are ionic and therefore strong
It contains a giant lattice of ions
It has delocalised electrons that lock it in place

wrong_explanations.1: Poly(ethene) has covalent, not ionic, bonds; its solid state is due to intermolecular forces between long chains.
wrong_explanations.2: Poly(ethene) is molecular, not a giant ionic lattice.
wrong_explanations.3: Poly(ethene) has no delocalised electrons; it is an insulator.

quiz.q: State what is meant by a monomer.
opts: A small molecule that can join with many others to form a polymer
The long chain molecule made when many small molecules join
A single atom that makes up a polymer
A mixture of two different plastics

wrong_explanations.1: The long chain is the POLYMER; the monomer is the small starting molecule.
wrong_explanations.2: A monomer is a molecule, not a single atom.
wrong_explanations.3: A monomer is a pure substance (one type of molecule), not a mixture.

quiz.q: Explain, in terms of the double bond, how addition polymerisation forms poly(ethene) from ethene.
opts: The C=C double bond in each ethene opens up; the freed electrons form new bonds to neighbouring monomers, linking them into a long saturated chain
The ethene molecules lose atoms which join to form the chain
The ethene molecules transfer electrons to form ions that attract
Water is removed between each pair of monomers to join them

wrong_explanations.1: In addition polymerisation no atoms are lost — the whole monomer becomes part of the chain.
wrong_explanations.2: Polymerisation of ethene is by opening the double bond (covalent), not by forming ions.
wrong_explanations.3: Removing water describes condensation polymerisation, not the addition polymerisation of ethene.

quiz.q: Explain why a polymer with longer chains tends to have a higher melting point than one with shorter chains.
opts: Longer chains have more points of contact, so the total intermolecular forces between chains are stronger and need more energy to overcome
Longer chains have stronger covalent bonds along them
Longer chains contain ions that attract strongly
Longer chains are always cross-linked

wrong_explanations.1: The covalent bonds along the chain are not what melts; the intermolecular forces between chains are, and these grow with chain length.
wrong_explanations.2: Polymers are molecular, not ionic — there are no ions between the chains.
wrong_explanations.3: Chain length and cross-linking are different things; a longer chain is not automatically cross-linked.

quiz.q: PVC is stiffer and higher-melting than poly(ethene). Suggest why, in terms of intermolecular forces.
opts: PVC contains polar C–Cl bonds, giving stronger intermolecular forces between the chains than in poly(ethene)
PVC has ionic bonds between its chains
PVC has weaker covalent bonds, so it sets harder
PVC chains are metallic and conduct heat away

wrong_explanations.1: PVC is still a molecular polymer, not ionic; the stronger forces come from the polar C–Cl bonds.
wrong_explanations.2: Weaker covalent bonds would make it less stable, not stiffer; the effect is due to stronger intermolecular forces.
wrong_explanations.3: PVC is an insulator, not metallic.

quiz.q: Explain why melting a polymer does not break the covalent bonds along its chains.
opts: Melting only supplies enough energy to overcome the weak intermolecular forces between the chains, which is far less than needed to break the strong covalent bonds
The covalent bonds are weaker than the forces between chains
The chains gain electrons that protect the bonds
The covalent bonds are shared between the chains

wrong_explanations.1: Covalent bonds are much stronger than the intermolecular forces, so only the weak forces are overcome.
wrong_explanations.2: No electrons are gained on melting.
wrong_explanations.3: Covalent bonds run along each chain; they are not shared between separate chains.

quiz.q: In addition polymerisation, explain why no small molecule is lost when the monomers join together.
opts: Every atom of each monomer ends up in the polymer chain, because the monomers simply add together at the opened double bond
A water molecule is released, but it is too small to notice
The monomers lose hydrogen atoms as they join
Half of each monomer is left behind as waste

wrong_explanations.1: Losing water is condensation polymerisation; addition polymerisation loses nothing.
wrong_explanations.2: No hydrogen atoms are lost in addition polymerisation.
wrong_explanations.3: No part of the monomer is left behind — all atoms are kept in the polymer.

higher.common_mistake: Students often call polymers giant covalent structures because the chains are so large. They are not: a polymer is a very large molecule, and the chains are held to each other only by weak intermolecular forces. In a giant covalent structure such as diamond, strong covalent bonds run all the way through — which is why diamond's melting point is far higher than any polymer's.

bonding/giant-covalent-structures.summary: Describe the structures of diamond and graphite and explain their contrasting properties.

quiz.q: Explain why graphite conducts electricity but diamond does not.
opts: Graphite has a delocalised electron per carbon free to move, while diamond uses all four outer electrons in bonds
Graphite packs more carbon atoms into each layer, and more atoms always means better electrical conductivity
Diamond is transparent, so charge passes through it as light rather than as an electric current
Graphite conducts because it has metallic bonding, and diamond has ionic bonding that traps its electrons

wrong_explanations.1: Conductivity depends on having charge carriers free to move, not on how many atoms the structure contains.
wrong_explanations.2: Whether a material is transparent to light is unrelated to whether it conducts electricity.
wrong_explanations.3: Both are giant covalent, not metallic or ionic; the free electrons in graphite come from each carbon bonding to only three others.

quiz.q: Explain why graphite is used as a lubricant.
opts: Its atoms form layers held by weak forces between them, so the layers slide over each other easily
Its surface is so perfectly smooth that there are no atoms on it for other surfaces to catch on
It dissolves in water between the surfaces to form a thin, slippery lubricating solution
It is as hard as diamond, and very hard materials naturally make surfaces slippery

wrong_explanations.1: All solids have atoms at their surface; graphite is slippery because its layers slide, not because atoms are absent.
wrong_explanations.2: Graphite does not dissolve in water — it works as a dry lubricant, so no solution is formed.
wrong_explanations.3: Graphite is soft, not hard like diamond; hardness would make it abrasive, not slippery.

quiz.q: Explain why diamond is very hard and has a very high melting point.
opts: Every carbon is joined to four others by strong covalent bonds throughout a giant rigid lattice
Its separate molecules are held to each other by unusually strong intermolecular forces that need lots of energy
It is held together throughout by strong ionic bonds between oppositely charged carbon ions
Its delocalised electrons form a strong sea that holds all the carbon atoms tightly in place

wrong_explanations.1: Diamond is not made of separate molecules — it is one giant covalent lattice, so there are no intermolecular forces.
wrong_explanations.2: Diamond is entirely covalent and contains no ions; there are no ionic bonds to break.
wrong_explanations.3: Diamond has no delocalised electrons — all four outer electrons of each carbon are held in covalent bonds.

quiz.q: Describe what a giant covalent structure is.
opts: A structure in which many atoms are all joined by covalent bonds running throughout
A structure built from many small, separate molecules held close together in a regular pattern
A regular lattice of positive and negative ions held together by strong electrostatic attraction
A lattice of positive metal ions surrounded by a sea of delocalised electrons

wrong_explanations.1: That describes a simple molecular substance; a giant covalent structure has no separate molecules — the bonding is continuous.
wrong_explanations.2: That is a giant ionic lattice; giant covalent structures contain no ions, only atoms joined by covalent bonds.
wrong_explanations.3: That is a giant metallic structure; giant covalent structures have no delocalised electrons or metal ions.

quiz.q: State why diamond and graphite can both be made of carbon yet have very different properties.
opts: They are different forms (allotropes) of carbon, with the atoms joined in different arrangements
They actually contain different elements, which is what gives them their different physical properties
One is a metal and the other a non-metal, so their bonding and properties differ
Diamond is built from ions while graphite is built from small carbon molecules

wrong_explanations.1: Both are pure carbon — the same single element; only the arrangement of the atoms differs.
wrong_explanations.2: Carbon is a non-metal in both forms; neither diamond nor graphite is a metal.
wrong_explanations.3: Both are giant covalent structures of carbon atoms — neither contains ions or separate small molecules.

quiz.q: Describe the structure of graphene and give one of its uses.
opts: A single layer of graphite, one atom thick, that conducts electricity; used in electronics
A hollow ball of carbon atoms that is soft and slippery; used as a dry lubricant
A giant 3D lattice with four bonds per carbon that is very hard; used in cutting tools
A long chain of many repeating carbon units, joined end to end; used to make plastic bags

wrong_explanations.1: A hollow ball of carbon that acts as a lubricant describes a fullerene, not graphene.
wrong_explanations.2: A hard giant 3D lattice with four bonds per carbon describes diamond, not graphene.
wrong_explanations.3: A long chain of repeating units used for plastic bags describes a polymer, not graphene.

quiz.q: Describe the structure of buckminsterfullerene (C₆₀) and give one of its uses.
opts: A hollow cage of 60 carbon atoms; used to deliver drugs into the body
A single flat sheet of carbon, one atom thick, that conducts; used in electronics
A giant 3D lattice of carbon that is extremely hard; used in cutting tools
A lattice of positive carbon ions in a sea of electrons; used in electrical wiring

wrong_explanations.1: A single flat sheet one atom thick describes graphene, not a fullerene.
wrong_explanations.2: A hard giant 3D lattice used in cutting tools describes diamond, not a hollow fullerene molecule.
wrong_explanations.3: Positive ions in a sea of electrons describes a metal; a fullerene is a covalent carbon molecule.

quiz.q: Describe carbon nanotubes and give one of their uses.
opts: Cylinders of carbon atoms, like rolled-up graphene; very strong, so used to reinforce materials
