Hollow balls of carbon atoms that release lots of heat; burned as a fuel for energy
A soft, slippery form of carbon whose layers rub off easily; used to write in pencils
A giant 3D lattice of carbon that is transparent and very hard; used as a gemstone

wrong_explanations.1: Hollow balls of carbon describe fullerenes, and carbon nanostructures are not burned as a fuel.
wrong_explanations.2: A soft, slippery carbon used to write in pencils describes graphite, not nanotubes.
wrong_explanations.3: A hard, transparent giant 3D lattice used as a gemstone describes diamond, not a nanotube.

quiz.q: Identify a use of diamond that depends on its hardness.
opts: Cutting and drilling through hard materials
Electrodes that carry an electric current
Dry lubricant for sliding machine parts
Rubbing off to write on paper

wrong_explanations.1: Carrying current needs a conductor, but diamond has no free electrons and does not conduct.
wrong_explanations.2: A lubricant must be soft and slippery; diamond is the hardest material, so it cannot lubricate.
wrong_explanations.3: Writing needs soft layers that rub off; diamond is too hard to leave a mark this way.

quiz.q: Identify which substance is a giant covalent structure.
opts: Diamond
Sodium chloride
Copper
Carbon dioxide

wrong_explanations.1: Sodium chloride is a giant ionic lattice.
wrong_explanations.2: Copper is a metal (giant metallic structure).
wrong_explanations.3: Carbon dioxide is a simple molecular substance, not giant covalent.

foundation.common_mistake: Students often assume that because diamond and graphite are both giant covalent structures made of carbon, they must have the same properties. They do not: graphite conducts electricity and is soft and slippery, while diamond does not conduct and is extremely hard. The difference is that in graphite each carbon bonds to only three others, leaving one delocalised electron per atom and weak forces between layers; in diamond every carbon bonds to four others with no free electrons.

quiz.q: Explain why graphite conducts electricity but diamond does not.
opts: Graphite has a delocalised electron per carbon free to move, while diamond uses all four outer electrons in bonds
Graphite packs more carbon atoms into each layer, and more atoms always means better electrical conductivity
Diamond is transparent, so charge passes through it as light rather than as an electric current
Graphite conducts because it has metallic bonding, and diamond has ionic bonding that traps its electrons

wrong_explanations.1: Conductivity depends on having charge carriers free to move, not on how many atoms the structure contains.
wrong_explanations.2: Whether a material is transparent to light is unrelated to whether it conducts electricity.
wrong_explanations.3: Both are giant covalent, not metallic or ionic; the free electrons in graphite come from each carbon bonding to only three others.

quiz.q: Explain why graphite is used as a lubricant.
opts: Its atoms form layers held by weak forces between them, so the layers slide over each other easily
Its surface is so perfectly smooth that there are no atoms on it for other surfaces to catch on
It dissolves in water between the surfaces to form a thin, slippery lubricating solution
It is as hard as diamond, and very hard materials naturally make surfaces slippery

wrong_explanations.1: All solids have atoms at their surface; graphite is slippery because its layers slide, not because atoms are absent.
wrong_explanations.2: Graphite does not dissolve in water — it works as a dry lubricant, so no solution is formed.
wrong_explanations.3: Graphite is soft, not hard like diamond; hardness would make it abrasive, not slippery.

quiz.q: Explain why diamond is very hard and has a very high melting point.
opts: Every carbon is joined to four others by strong covalent bonds throughout a giant rigid lattice
Its separate molecules are held to each other by unusually strong intermolecular forces that need lots of energy
It is held together throughout by strong ionic bonds between oppositely charged carbon ions
Its delocalised electrons form a strong sea that holds all the carbon atoms tightly in place

wrong_explanations.1: Diamond is not made of separate molecules — it is one giant covalent lattice, so there are no intermolecular forces.
wrong_explanations.2: Diamond is entirely covalent and contains no ions; there are no ionic bonds to break.
wrong_explanations.3: Diamond has no delocalised electrons — all four outer electrons of each carbon are held in covalent bonds.

quiz.q: Describe what a giant covalent structure is.
opts: A structure in which many atoms are all joined by covalent bonds running throughout
A structure built from many small, separate molecules held close together in a regular pattern
A regular lattice of positive and negative ions held together by strong electrostatic attraction
A lattice of positive metal ions surrounded by a sea of delocalised electrons

wrong_explanations.1: That describes a simple molecular substance; a giant covalent structure has no separate molecules — the bonding is continuous.
wrong_explanations.2: That is a giant ionic lattice; giant covalent structures contain no ions, only atoms joined by covalent bonds.
wrong_explanations.3: That is a giant metallic structure; giant covalent structures have no delocalised electrons or metal ions.

quiz.q: State why diamond and graphite can both be made of carbon yet have very different properties.
opts: They are different forms (allotropes) of carbon, with the atoms joined in different arrangements
They actually contain different elements, which is what gives them their different physical properties
One is a metal and the other a non-metal, so their bonding and properties differ
Diamond is built from ions while graphite is built from small carbon molecules

wrong_explanations.1: Both are pure carbon — the same single element; only the arrangement of the atoms differs.
wrong_explanations.2: Carbon is a non-metal in both forms; neither diamond nor graphite is a metal.
wrong_explanations.3: Both are giant covalent structures of carbon atoms — neither contains ions or separate small molecules.

quiz.q: Explain why silicon dioxide (found in sand and quartz) has a very high melting point.
opts: It is giant covalent, so many strong Si–O covalent bonds must be broken to melt it
It is ionic, so strong electrostatic forces between Si and O ions must be overcome
It is a simple molecule, so strong intermolecular forces between SiO₂ molecules must be broken
It is a metal, so a strong sea of delocalised electrons holds the ions together

wrong_explanations.1: Silicon dioxide is giant covalent, not ionic — it contains no ions, only Si and O atoms joined by covalent bonds.
wrong_explanations.2: It is not simple molecular — the Si–O covalent bonds run throughout the whole structure, with no separate molecules.
wrong_explanations.3: Silicon dioxide is a non-metal compound with no delocalised electrons; its bonding is covalent, not metallic.

quiz.q: Graphene is a single layer of graphite. Explain why it conducts electricity and is very strong.
opts: Each carbon covalently bonds to three others in a strong network, and its fourth outer electron is delocalised
It contains free-moving ions that carry charge, and the ionic bonds between them make it strong
It is a metal, so its sea of delocalised electrons lets it conduct and hold together strongly
Its molecules are held to each other by strong intermolecular forces, like a simple molecular solid

wrong_explanations.1: Graphene has no ions — its conduction comes from delocalised electrons, and its strength from covalent bonds.
wrong_explanations.2: Graphene is a covalent form of carbon, not a metal, though it does conduct via delocalised electrons.
wrong_explanations.3: Graphene is one giant covalent sheet, not separate molecules, so there are no intermolecular forces.

quiz.q: Explain why buckminsterfullerene (C₆₀) can be used to deliver drugs to specific parts of the body.
opts: Its molecules are hollow cages, so a drug can be carried inside and released where needed
It is a metal, so the drug is held on its surface by a sea of delocalised electrons
It reacts with the drug to form a new compound that then dissolves and spreads through the blood
It is giant covalent, so its bonds run throughout and hold the drug tightly inside the structure

wrong_explanations.1: C₆₀ is a covalent carbon molecule, not a metal, so it has no delocalised-electron surface to carry a drug.
wrong_explanations.2: The drug is held physically inside the hollow cage; C₆₀ does not react with it to carry it.
wrong_explanations.3: C₆₀ is a small hollow molecule, not a giant covalent structure — the drug sits inside the cage, not bonded throughout.

quiz.q: Explain why graphite has a high melting point even though its layers can slide over each other easily.
opts: Melting breaks the strong covalent bonds within layers, while sliding only overcomes the weak forces between layers
The layers are held to each other by strong ionic bonds that need a lot of energy to break
All of its delocalised electrons must first be removed before the layers can break apart and melt
Graphite does not really have a high melting point; it softens easily because the layers slide

wrong_explanations.1: There are no ionic bonds in graphite; the high melting point comes from strong covalent bonds within each layer, not forces between layers.
wrong_explanations.2: Melting breaks covalent bonds within the layers; the delocalised electrons are not removed and are not what must be overcome to melt it.
wrong_explanations.3: Graphite does have a high melting point, because of the strong covalent bonds within each layer.

quiz.q: Compare diamond and graphite in terms of the number of bonds each carbon forms and whether they conduct electricity.
opts: Diamond forms 4 bonds per carbon and does not conduct; graphite forms 3, leaving a delocalised electron, so conducts
Both form 4 bonds per carbon, and both conduct because each has a spare delocalised electron
Both form 3 bonds per carbon, and neither conducts because every electron is used in a bond
Diamond forms 3 bonds and conducts, while graphite forms 4 bonds and does not conduct

wrong_explanations.1: Only graphite conducts, and only diamond forms 4 bonds; diamond has all its electrons in bonds, so no spare delocalised electron.
wrong_explanations.2: Diamond forms 4 bonds (not 3) and graphite does conduct, because graphite has a fourth electron that is delocalised.
wrong_explanations.3: This swaps them over: diamond forms 4 bonds and does not conduct; graphite forms 3 and does conduct.

higher.common_mistake: Students often assume that because diamond and graphite are both giant covalent structures made of carbon, they must have the same properties. They do not: graphite conducts electricity and is soft and slippery, while diamond does not conduct and is extremely hard. The difference is that in graphite each carbon bonds to only three others, leaving one delocalised electron per atom and weak forces between layers; in diamond every carbon bonds to four others with no free electrons.
higher.higher_box: Graphene: a single layer of graphite — one atom thick, conducts electricity (delocalised electrons), extremely strong. Fullerenes: C₆₀ (buckminsterfullerene) — hollow spherical cage, used in drug delivery and as lubricant. Carbon nanotubes: cylindrical graphene — very strong along axis, conduct electricity. All based on hexagonal carbon networks.

bonding/metals-alloys.summary: Explain the properties of metals and alloys in terms of metallic bonding and structure.

quiz.q: Explain why metals can conduct electricity in the solid state, but ionic compounds cannot.
opts: Metals have delocalised electrons that are free to move even when solid; in an ionic solid the ions are locked in fixed positions and cannot move
Metal atoms are smaller than ions, so they move more easily through the solid
Metals have more protons, which makes them better at conducting
Ionic bonds are stronger, which stops any particle from moving

wrong_explanations.1: Atom or ion size is not the reason — it is the availability of free-moving charge carriers.
wrong_explanations.2: Proton number does not determine conductivity; many non-metals have lots of protons but do not conduct.
wrong_explanations.3: Ionic compounds CAN conduct when molten (ions freed); the issue in the solid is fixed ions, not stronger bonds.

quiz.q: Explain why brass (copper mixed with zinc) is harder than pure copper.
opts: The zinc atoms are a different size to the copper atoms, so they distort the regular lattice and stop the layers of atoms sliding over each other
Brass has more metallic bonds than copper, and more bonds means harder
Zinc is a harder metal, so mixing it in makes the product hard
Brass has been purified, and pure metals are always harder

wrong_explanations.1: More bonds does not necessarily mean harder — the hardening is due to lattice disruption preventing layers sliding.
wrong_explanations.2: The key mechanism is disrupting the regular copper lattice with differently-sized atoms, not zinc's own hardness.
wrong_explanations.3: Brass is a mixture (less pure than copper); it is the added, differently-sized atoms that harden it.

quiz.q: Explain why metals are good conductors of heat.
opts: The delocalised electrons are free to move and quickly transfer thermal energy through the structure
The positive ions carry the heat as they move around
Metals contain water that spreads the heat
The covalent bonds pass the heat along

wrong_explanations.1: The positive ions are fixed and only vibrate; the mobile delocalised electrons carry most of the heat.
wrong_explanations.2: Metals do not contain water; heat is carried by the delocalised electrons.
wrong_explanations.3: Metals have no covalent bonds; heat is transferred by the electron sea.

quiz.q: Describe the difference in structure between a pure metal and one of its alloys.
opts: A pure metal has a regular lattice of same-sized atoms; an alloy has atoms of different sizes that distort the regular arrangement
A pure metal contains ions but an alloy contains only atoms
A pure metal is molecular but an alloy is a giant lattice
A pure metal has no bonding but an alloy has metallic bonding

wrong_explanations.1: Both have positive ions and a sea of electrons; the difference is that the alloy has differently-sized atoms.
wrong_explanations.2: Both are giant metallic structures, not molecular.
wrong_explanations.3: Both are held by metallic bonding; the difference is the mix of atom sizes.

quiz.q: Explain why pure metals are often too soft for use, so alloys are made instead.
opts: In a pure metal the layers of same-sized atoms can slide over each other easily, making it soft; alloying adds differently-sized atoms that stop the layers sliding
Pure metals contain no metallic bonds, so they fall apart
Pure metals are always liquids at room temperature
Pure metals have too many delocalised electrons to be strong

wrong_explanations.1: Pure metals do have metallic bonding; they are soft because their identical-sized atoms let layers slide.
wrong_explanations.2: Most pure metals are solid at room temperature; they are simply soft.
wrong_explanations.3: The number of delocalised electrons is not the reason; sliding layers of identical atoms is.

quiz.q: Name the type of structure that a metal has.
opts: A giant metallic structure (lattice)
A simple molecular structure
A giant ionic lattice
A single small molecule

wrong_explanations.1: Metals are giant metallic structures, not made of small molecules.
wrong_explanations.2: A giant ionic lattice has positive and negative ions; a metal has positive ions and an electron sea.
wrong_explanations.3: A metal is a giant structure, not one small molecule.

quiz.q: State what an alloy is.
opts: A mixture of a metal with one or more other elements
A very pure sample of a single metal
A compound of a metal and a non-metal
A metal that has had all its electrons removed

wrong_explanations.1: A pure single metal is the opposite of an alloy.
wrong_explanations.2: An alloy is a mixture, not a chemically bonded compound.
wrong_explanations.3: Metals in an alloy keep their delocalised electrons.

quiz.q: Identify a use of copper that depends on it being a good electrical conductor.
opts: Electrical wiring
Making window glass
As a fuel for burning
As a fertiliser

wrong_explanations.1: Window glass is not a metal and is not chosen for conductivity.
wrong_explanations.2: Copper is not used as a fuel.
wrong_explanations.3: Copper is not used as a fertiliser; wiring uses its high conductivity.

quiz.q: Identify the correct description of steel.
opts: Iron mixed with a small amount of carbon
Pure iron with nothing added
Copper mixed with zinc
A non-metal element

wrong_explanations.1: Steel is an ALLOY — pure iron with nothing added is not steel.
wrong_explanations.2: Copper mixed with zinc is brass, not steel.
wrong_explanations.3: Steel is a metal alloy, not a non-metal element.

quiz.q: State whether metals are malleable or brittle.
opts: Malleable — they can be hammered into shape
Brittle — they shatter when struck
Neither — they are liquids
Both equally

wrong_explanations.1: Brittleness is a property of ionic compounds; metals bend rather than shatter.
wrong_explanations.2: Most metals are solid at room temperature, not liquid.
wrong_explanations.3: Metals are clearly malleable, not brittle.

foundation.common_mistake: Students often think that because ionic compounds only conduct when molten, metals must be the same. They are not: a metal conducts in the solid state because its delocalised electrons are already free to move. Ionic compounds cannot conduct as solids because their charge carriers are ions, which stay locked in place until the compound melts or dissolves.

wrong_explanations.2: Proton number does not determine conductivity; many non-metals have lots of protons but do not conduct.
wrong_explanations.3: Ionic compounds CAN conduct when molten (ions freed); the issue in the solid is fixed ions, not stronger bonds.

wrong_explanations.2: Most pure metals are solid at room temperature; they are simply soft.
wrong_explanations.3: The number of delocalised electrons is not the reason; sliding layers of identical atoms is.

quiz.q: Explain, in terms of particles, why metals conduct electricity and heat but simple molecular substances do not.
opts: Metals contain delocalised electrons that are free to move and carry charge and thermal energy; simple molecular substances have no free electrons or ions
Metals contain free ions but molecules do not
Metals have stronger covalent bonds than molecules
Simple molecular substances have delocalised electrons that are fixed

wrong_explanations.1: Metals conduct via delocalised electrons, not free ions (ions are fixed in a solid metal).
wrong_explanations.2: Metals have no covalent bonds; conduction is due to the electron sea.
wrong_explanations.3: Simple molecular substances have no delocalised electrons at all.

quiz.q: Stainless steel is used to make cutlery. Suggest two properties that make it suitable and relate them to its structure.
opts: It is hard and strong (differently-sized atoms disrupt the lattice so layers cannot slide) and it resists corrosion
It is soft and brittle, so it can be shaped and snapped
It has a low melting point, so it melts clean
It is a good insulator, so it is safe to hold

wrong_explanations.1: Soft, brittle cutlery would be useless; stainless steel is hard and strong because of its alloy structure.
wrong_explanations.2: A low melting point would be a disadvantage for cutlery.
wrong_explanations.3: Being a conductor or insulator is not the key reason cutlery is made from stainless steel.

quiz.q: Explain why an alloy is generally harder than the pure metal it is made from, using the idea of layers of atoms.
opts: The different-sized atoms in the alloy distort the regular layers, so the layers can no longer slide over each other easily
The alloy has weaker metallic bonds, which makes it more rigid
The alloy contains ionic bonds that hold it firmly
The alloy has fewer delocalised electrons, so it is stronger

wrong_explanations.1: Weaker bonds would make it softer, not harder; the hardening is due to lattice distortion.
wrong_explanations.2: Alloys are still held by metallic bonding, not ionic bonds.
wrong_explanations.3: The number of delocalised electrons is not the reason; disrupting the layers is.

quiz.q: Aluminium is used for aircraft bodies. Suggest why an aluminium alloy is used rather than pure aluminium.
opts: The alloy is much stronger and harder than pure aluminium while still having a low density, so the aircraft is strong but light
Pure aluminium is a better conductor, which is dangerous in flight
The alloy is cheaper because it contains no aluminium
Pure aluminium is a gas at room temperature

wrong_explanations.1: Conductivity is not the reason; the alloy is chosen because it is stronger while remaining light.
wrong_explanations.2: Aluminium alloys still contain mostly aluminium.
wrong_explanations.3: Aluminium is a solid metal at room temperature.

quiz.q: Explain why metals are malleable but ionic compounds are brittle, in terms of what happens when the layers move.
opts: In a metal the layers of ions slide and the electron sea keeps them bonded, so it bends; in an ionic solid, shifting the layers brings like charges together, which repel and split the crystal
Both bend, but metals bend more quickly
Metals contain covalent bonds that flex; ionic compounds do not
Ionic compounds contain delocalised electrons that snap

wrong_explanations.1: Ionic compounds do not bend at all — they shatter, because of ion repulsion when layers shift.
wrong_explanations.2: Metals have no covalent bonds; malleability comes from the sliding layers held by the electron sea.
wrong_explanations.3: Ionic compounds have no delocalised electrons; that is a metallic feature.

higher.common_mistake: Students often think that because ionic compounds only conduct when molten, metals must be the same. They are not: a metal conducts in the solid state because its delocalised electrons are already free to move. Ionic compounds cannot conduct as solids because their charge carriers are ions, which stay locked in place until the compound melts or dissolves.

quantitative/conservation-of-mass.summary: Explain the law of conservation of mass and balance symbol equations.

quiz.q: Why must chemical equations be balanced?
opts: Atoms cannot be created or destroyed — the same atoms must appear on both sides of the equation
Balanced equations are easier to read and understand
Unbalanced equations produce different products
Equations must be balanced to follow international chemical naming rules

wrong_explanations.1: Readability is a benefit but not the fundamental reason — conservation of mass is.
wrong_explanations.2: An unbalanced equation can still show the correct reactants and products — it just doesn't correctly represent the quantitative relationship between them.
wrong_explanations.3: Balancing is a scientific law requirement (conservation of mass), not a naming convention.

quiz.q: 24 g of magnesium reacts completely with oxygen to form magnesium oxide. 40 g of magnesium oxide is produced. How much oxygen reacted?
opts: 16 g — conservation of mass: mass of O₂ = 40 − 24 = 16 g
40 g — the oxygen has the same mass as the product
64 g — double the mass of magnesium
Cannot be determined without the balanced equation

wrong_explanations.1: Mass in = mass out. If 40 g MgO is made from 24 g Mg, the remaining 40 − 24 = 16 g must have come from oxygen.
wrong_explanations.2: The mass of oxygen is only part of the product mass — the product contains both magnesium AND oxygen.
wrong_explanations.3: Conservation of mass allows this calculation: mass O₂ = mass product − mass Mg = 40 − 24 = 16 g.

quiz.q: Which of these is a correctly balanced equation?
opts: 4Fe + 3O₂ → 2Fe₂O₃
Fe + O₂ → Fe₂O₃
2Fe + O₂ → Fe₂O₃
Fe + 3O₂ → 2Fe₂O₃

wrong_explanations.1: Fe + O₂ → Fe₂O₃: left has 1 Fe, 2 O; right has 2 Fe, 3 O — not balanced.
wrong_explanations.2: 2Fe + O₂ → Fe₂O₃: left has 2 Fe, 2 O; right has 2 Fe, 3 O — oxygen not balanced.
wrong_explanations.3: Fe + 3O₂ → 2Fe₂O₃: left has 1 Fe, 6 O; right has 4 Fe, 6 O — iron not balanced.

foundation.common_mistake: Never change the SUBSCRIPT numbers in a formula when balancing — only add or change the large numbers (coefficients) IN FRONT of formulas. Changing H₂O to H₂O₂ would create a different compound (hydrogen peroxide, not water). Always balance by adjusting coefficients only.

higher.higher_box: Calculate theoretical yield using moles. Atom economy = (Mr desired products ÷ Mr all products) × 100. Addition reactions have 100% atom economy. High atom economy = less waste, more sustainable, more economical. Evaluate different reaction routes on the basis of atom economy.

quantitative/relative-formula-mass.summary: Calculate relative formula mass (Mr) from relative atomic masses and use it in calculations.

quiz.q: What is the relative formula mass of calcium carbonate (CaCO₃)? Ar: Ca=40, C=12, O=16.
opts: 68 — (40) + (12) + (16) — counting only one oxygen
116 — (40) + (12) + (4 × 16) — counting four oxygens
52 — (40) + (12) — forgetting the oxygens

wrong_explanations.1: CaCO₃ has only ONE oxygen — but the subscript 3 after O means THREE oxygens: 3 × 16 = 48.
wrong_explanations.2: CO₃ means 3 oxygen atoms, not 4. Only CO₄ would have 4 oxygens.
wrong_explanations.3: The oxygen cannot be ignored — CaCO₃ has a carbon AND three oxygens.

quiz.q: What is the Mr of Mg(NO₃)₂? Ar: Mg=24, N=14, O=16.

wrong_explanations.1: The subscript 2 outside the bracket means the entire NO₃ group is repeated twice. So there is 1 N, NOT 2 — wait: NO₃ has 1 N and 3 O, multiplied by 2 = 2N and 6O.
wrong_explanations.2: Mg(NO₃)₂ means 2 groups of NO₃: so 2N and 6O total. Only counting 3O ignores the ×2 multiplier.
wrong_explanations.3: Each NO₃ has 3 O; multiplied by 2 = 6 O total, not 4.

foundation.common_mistake: When a formula has BRACKETS with a subscript, MULTIPLY all atoms inside the bracket by the subscript. Ca(OH)₂ has 2 oxygen atoms and 2 hydrogen atoms — not 1 of each. Write out the count carefully: Ca(OH)₂ = Ca + 2O + 2H = 40 + 32 + 2 = 74.

fifas.label: Mr Calculation — H₂SO₄
fifas.question: Calculate the relative formula mass of sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄). Ar: H=1, S=32, O=16.
steps: Mr = sum of all Ar values × number of atoms

higher.higher_box: Calculate percentage mass of an element in a compound: (Ar × number of atoms ÷ Mr) × 100. Calculate empirical formula from percentage composition. Convert empirical to molecular formula using Mr. These skills are foundations for mole calculations.

quantitative/mass-changes-reactions.summary: Explain why the measured mass sometimes appears to change in a reaction and predict mass changes.

quiz.q: A student heats calcium carbonate in an open crucible. CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂. What happens to the measured mass?
opts: The mass decreases — CO₂ gas escapes from the open crucible
The mass increases — oxygen from air is absorbed
The mass stays the same — conservation of mass
The mass first increases, then decreases

wrong_explanations.1: Oxygen is not absorbed here — CaCO₃ is decomposing, not combusting. A gas (CO₂) is PRODUCED and escapes.
wrong_explanations.2: Conservation of mass applies to the TOTAL system. In an open container, the mass of the CONTAINER contents decreases because CO₂ leaves. Conservation is still upheld if you include the escaped CO₂.
wrong_explanations.3: There is no initial increase phase — CO₂ is produced and escapes from the start of heating.

quiz.q: Why does the mass of iron increase when it rusts in air?
opts: Oxygen from the air combines with the iron — the oxygen adds to the mass of the solid product (iron oxide)
Water from humidity condenses on the rust — adding extra mass
The iron becomes denser as it rusts — same volume but more mass
Iron oxide has more atoms per formula unit than iron

wrong_explanations.1: Humidity can contribute, but the primary reason is oxygen being ABSORBED from the air. The reaction is 4Fe + 3O₂ → 2Fe₂O₃ — the oxygen becomes part of the solid.
wrong_explanations.2: Density changes do not increase total mass — mass = density × volume. If both change proportionally, mass stays the same. More mass comes from more matter (oxygen absorbed).
wrong_explanations.3: Atom count per formula unit is irrelevant — it is the MASS of oxygen absorbed from air that increases the total mass.

foundation.common_mistake: When a metal burns in air, the solid GAINS mass (oxygen is added from the air to form the oxide). Students often expect the solid to lose mass because 'burning destroys things' — but mass is always conserved. The apparent gain is real because you are adding oxygen from the atmosphere to the solid product.

fifas.label: Mass Change Prediction
fifas.question: 4.8 g of magnesium burns completely in air: 2Mg + O₂ → 2MgO. Calculate the mass of MgO produced. Ar: Mg=24, O=16.
steps: Use ratio from balanced equation: 2 × Mr(Mg) : 2 × Mr(MgO) = 48 : 80
Scale: 4.8 g of Mg. Scale factor = 4.8 ÷ 48 = 0.1
8.0 g of MgO produced (mass increases by 3.2 g — the absorbed oxygen)

The apparent gain is real because you are adding oxygen from the atmosphere to the solid product.

fifas.label: Mass Change Prediction
fifas.question: 4.8 g of magnesium burns completely in air: 2Mg + O₂ → 2MgO. Calculate the mass of MgO produced. Ar: Mg=24, O=16.
steps: Use ratio from balanced equation: 2 × Mr(Mg) : 2 × Mr(MgO) = 48 : 80
Scale: 4.8 g of Mg. Scale factor = 4.8 ÷ 48 = 0.1
8.0 g of MgO produced (mass increases by 3.2 g — the absorbed oxygen)

quantitative/chemical-measurements.summary: Describe the importance of precise measurements in chemistry and sources of uncertainty.

quiz.q: A student measures a volume using a burette and a measuring cylinder. The burette has an uncertainty of ±0.05 cm³ and the cylinder ±1 cm³. Both deliver 25 cm³. Which gives the lower percentage uncertainty?
opts: The burette — % uncertainty = (0.05 ÷ 25) × 100 = 0.2%, vs the cylinder at 4%
The measuring cylinder — larger equipment is always more accurate
They are the same — both deliver 25 cm³
The measuring cylinder — it has a smaller absolute uncertainty

wrong_explanations.1: Equipment size doesn't determine accuracy — precision depends on the uncertainty relative to the measurement.
wrong_explanations.2: Both deliver the same VOLUME but with different UNCERTAINTIES — the burette (±0.05) is far more precise than the measuring cylinder (±1).
wrong_explanations.3: The measuring cylinder has a LARGER absolute uncertainty (±1 cm³ vs ±0.05 cm³) — it is LESS precise, not more.

quiz.q: When reading a burette or measuring cylinder containing water, where should you read the volume?
opts: From the bottom of the meniscus — water curves downward, creating a concave surface
From the top of the meniscus — this gives the largest volume reading
From the middle of the meniscus — splitting the difference
It doesn't matter — the meniscus reading is always correct

wrong_explanations.1: Reading from the TOP of the meniscus gives an overestimate — the markings are calibrated for the bottom of the meniscus.
wrong_explanations.2: Reading from the middle introduces error — always read from the BOTTOM consistently to match calibration.
wrong_explanations.3: Reading from the wrong part of the meniscus introduces a systematic error — it DOES matter and affects accuracy.

foundation.common_mistake: Read the volume from the BOTTOM of the meniscus — not the top. Water curves downward in a glass tube, creating a concave meniscus. Reading from the top overestimates the volume. Also: zeroing the balance (taring) before each measurement is essential — failing to do so introduces a systematic error.

quantitative/concentration-of-solutions.summary: Define concentration and calculate it from mass and volume.

quiz.q: 25 g of glucose is dissolved in 500 cm³ of water. What is the concentration in g/dm³?
opts: 50 g/dm³ — volume = 500 ÷ 1000 = 0.5 dm³, concentration = 25 ÷ 0.5 = 50
0.05 g/dm³ — concentration = 25 ÷ 500
12500 g/dm³ — concentration = 25 × 500
2.5 g/dm³ — concentration = 25 ÷ 10

wrong_explanations.1: This uses 500 cm³ directly without converting to dm³ — gives an answer 1000× too small. Must convert: 500 cm³ = 0.5 dm³.
wrong_explanations.2: This multiplies instead of divides — and still fails to convert units.
wrong_explanations.3: This divides by 10 instead of 0.5 — an incorrect conversion. 500 cm³ = 0.5 dm³, not 10 dm³.

quiz.q: A solution has a concentration of 80 g/dm³. What mass of solute is in 250 cm³ of this solution?
opts: 20000 g — mass = 80 × 250 (forgetting to convert cm³ to dm³)

wrong_explanations.1: Using 250 directly without converting gives 80 × 250 = 20,000 — not 20. Always convert: 250 cm³ = 0.25 dm³.
wrong_explanations.2: Dividing gives volume, not mass. mass = concentration × volume.
wrong_explanations.3: 250 ÷ 10 = 25 — but the conversion is 250 ÷ 1000 = 0.25 dm³.

foundation.common_mistake: Volume MUST be in dm³ when using the concentration formula. If given in cm³, divide by 1000 first. 250 cm³ = 0.250 dm³. Students frequently forget this conversion and get answers 1000 times too large or too small.

chemical-changes/reactivity-series.summary: Describe the reactivity series of metals and use it to predict reactions.

quiz.q: A strip of zinc is placed in copper sulfate solution. What is observed?
opts: The blue solution fades and copper metal deposits on the zinc — zinc displaces copper
No change — zinc and copper have similar reactivity
The zinc dissolves and the solution turns orange
Zinc sulfate crystals form in the blue solution

wrong_explanations.1: Zinc IS more reactive than copper — a reaction occurs. The solution changes from blue (Cu²⁺) to colourless/pale (Zn²⁺).
wrong_explanations.2: The solution turns pale/colourless as Cu²⁺ ions are replaced by Zn²⁺ — orange is not the colour of any of these solutions.
wrong_explanations.3: Zinc sulfate is formed in solution (dissolved) — it doesn't crystallise out immediately under these conditions.

quiz.q: Why does copper not react with dilute hydrochloric acid?
opts: Copper is BELOW hydrogen in the reactivity series — it cannot displace hydrogen from the acid
Copper is too dense to react with liquids
Hydrochloric acid is not strong enough to react with any metals
Copper reacts with acid but the product is insoluble

wrong_explanations.1: Density doesn't determine reactivity — gold is denser than magnesium but far less reactive.
wrong_explanations.2: HCl reacts with many metals — magnesium, zinc, iron all react vigorously with HCl. Only metals BELOW hydrogen in the series don't react.
wrong_explanations.3: Copper doesn't react with dilute HCl at all — it doesn't produce any product.

foundation.common_mistake: A metal can only displace another metal that is BELOW it in the reactivity series. Copper cannot displace iron from iron sulfate — copper is less reactive than iron. The more reactive metal always displaces the less reactive one, not the other way around.

chemical-changes/extraction-of-metals.summary: Explain how metals are extracted from their ores and how the method depends on reactivity.

quiz.q: Why is aluminium extracted by electrolysis rather than by reduction with carbon?
opts: Aluminium is more reactive than carbon — carbon cannot displace it from aluminium oxide
Aluminium oxide doesn't contain enough oxygen for the carbon to react with
Carbon would contaminate the aluminium, making it too brittle to use
Electrolysis is cheaper than heating with carbon for all metals

wrong_explanations.1: Aluminium oxide has plenty of oxygen — but carbon can only REDUCE metal oxides when the metal is LESS reactive than carbon.
wrong_explanations.2: Carbon and aluminium do not form harmful alloys under these conditions — purity concerns are a separate issue.
wrong_explanations.3: Electrolysis is actually VERY EXPENSIVE — it requires huge amounts of electrical energy. The reason is purely reactivity-based.

quiz.q: Carbon monoxide reduces iron oxide in the blast furnace. What type of reaction is this?
opts: Reduction — the iron oxide loses oxygen; the carbon monoxide gains oxygen (and is oxidised)
Oxidation — the iron oxide gains electrons from the carbon monoxide
Neutralisation — the acid iron oxide is neutralised by the basic carbon
Electrolysis — electrical energy breaks apart the iron oxide

wrong_explanations.1: The IRON OXIDE loses oxygen (is REDUCED). The CO gains oxygen (is OXIDISED). These are simultaneous — the reaction is both a reduction AND an oxidation.
wrong_explanations.2: Neutralisation involves an acid and a base — iron oxide and carbon monoxide are not an acid-base pair.
wrong_explanations.3: The blast furnace uses heat (combustion), not electricity. Electrolysis is used for reactive metals like aluminium.

foundation.common_mistake: Aluminium is above carbon in the reactivity series — carbon CANNOT reduce aluminium oxide. Students often try to apply carbon reduction to all metals. Only metals BELOW carbon in the reactivity series can be extracted by carbon reduction. Metals above carbon (including aluminium) require electrolysis.

higher.higher_box: Evaluate economic and environmental factors: energy cost of electrolysis vs smelting, environmental impact of mining, transportation costs. Compare traditional vs alternative methods (phytomining, bioleaching) in terms of speed, cost, environmental impact and suitability for ore grade.

chemical-changes/oxidation-reduction.summary: Define oxidation and reduction in terms of oxygen gain/loss and use OIL RIG.

quiz.q: In the reaction CuO + H₂ → Cu + H₂O, what is the reducing agent?
opts: Hydrogen (H₂) — it reduces the copper oxide by removing its oxygen, and is itself oxidised to water
Copper oxide — it causes the hydrogen to become water
Copper — it is the product of reduction
Water — it is formed when hydrogen is reduced

wrong_explanations.1: Copper oxide is the OXIDISING AGENT — it provides the oxygen that oxidises hydrogen. It is itself reduced.
wrong_explanations.2: Copper is the PRODUCT of the reduction of CuO — it is not a reducing agent.
wrong_explanations.3: Water is a PRODUCT — reducing agents are reactants that cause reduction of another substance.

quiz.q: What does OIL RIG stand for?
opts: Oxidation Is Loss (of electrons), Reduction Is Gain (of electrons)
Oxidation Involves Losing Reactants, Reduction Involves Gaining
Oxygen In Liquid, Reactions In Gas
Only In Liquids Reactions Involve Gases

wrong_explanations.1: OIL RIG specifically refers to ELECTRONS — Oxidation Is Loss of electrons, Reduction Is Gain of electrons. The words 'reactants' and 'gaining' are incorrect.
wrong_explanations.2: OIL RIG is a memory aid specifically about electron transfer in redox reactions — not about phases or states of matter.
wrong_explanations.3: OIL RIG is entirely about electrons in redox reactions — not about the physical state of substances.

foundation.common_mistake: In a redox reaction, the REDUCING AGENT is OXIDISED (it gives away electrons/oxygen). The OXIDISING AGENT is REDUCED (it receives electrons/gains oxygen). This is counterintuitive — an oxidising agent doesn't stay oxidised, it gets reduced. Remember: agents do the opposite of what they're called.

higher.higher_box: Define precisely: oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons (OIL RIG). Write half equations for oxidation and reduction. Identify oxidising agents (gain electrons, are reduced) and reducing agents (lose electrons, are oxidised). Apply to displacement and electrolysis reactions.

chemical-changes/reactions-of-acids.summary: Describe reactions of acids with metals, metal oxides, metal hydroxides and carbonates.

quiz.q: What are the products when zinc carbonate reacts with sulfuric acid?
opts: Zinc sulfate + water + carbon dioxide
Zinc sulfate + hydrogen
Zinc oxide + water + carbon dioxide
Zinc sulfate + water only

wrong_explanations.1: Zinc + sulfuric acid → zinc sulfate + hydrogen. Carbonates produce CO₂, not H₂.
wrong_explanations.2: Zinc carbonate is a CARBONATE — the products of acid + carbonate are salt + water + CO₂, not zinc oxide.
wrong_explanations.3: Carbonates produce salt + water + CO₂ — not just salt + water. The CO₂ is essential.

quiz.q: Magnesium reacts with hydrochloric acid. What salt is produced?
opts: Magnesium chloride (MgCl₂) — HCl produces chloride salts
Magnesium sulfate — all acids produce sulfate salts
Magnesium oxide — magnesium reacts with hydrogen to form the oxide
Magnesium hydroxide — the hydrogen from HCl bonds to the magnesium

wrong_explanations.1: Sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) produces SULFATE salts. Hydrochloric acid (HCl) always produces CHLORIDE salts.
wrong_explanations.2: The acid does NOT supply oxygen — magnesium oxide would form from Mg reacting with O₂. The reaction with HCl gives a chloride salt.
wrong_explanations.3: The acid produces H⁺ ions which react to form H₂ gas — they do NOT combine with the metal to form a hydroxide.

foundation.common_mistake: Acid + metal carbonate produces THREE products: salt + water + carbon DIOXIDE. Students often forget the water or forget the CO₂. Also: the SALT name comes from the METAL and the ACID — sulfuric acid always gives sulfate salts, hydrochloric acid gives chloride salts, nitric acid gives nitrate salts.

higher.higher_box: Write ionic equations for neutralisation and precipitation. Net ionic equation: H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O. Spectator ions are omitted from ionic equations. Understand that ionic equations show only species that change — spectator ions remain unchanged in solution.

chemical-changes/salts-neutralisation.summary: Describe methods for making pure dry salts from acids and how neutralisation works.

quiz.q: Why must excess solid be added when making a salt by reacting a metal oxide with an acid?
opts: To ensure all the acid reacts — any remaining acid would contaminate the final salt
To speed up the reaction — more solid increases the rate
To increase the yield of salt — more reactant gives more product
The excess solid acts as a catalyst

wrong_explanations.1: Excess solid does increase the surface area and reaction rate — but the MAIN reason is to guarantee complete neutralisation of all the acid.
wrong_explanations.2: You cannot add more solid than the acid can react with — excess produces the same yield. The reason is to ensure complete reaction.
wrong_explanations.3: The excess solid is REMOVED by filtration — it doesn't catalyse the reaction.

quiz.q: What is the ionic equation for neutralisation?
opts: H⁺(aq) + OH⁻(aq) → H₂O(l)
Acid + alkali → salt + water
Na⁺(aq) + Cl⁻(aq) → NaCl(s)
H₂ + O₂ → H₂O

wrong_explanations.1: This is the WORD equation — an ionic equation uses symbols and charges. The ionic equation H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O shows the actual ions involved.
wrong_explanations.2: This is a precipitation ionic equation — it describes salt formation, not neutralisation.
wrong_explanations.3: This is the equation for burning hydrogen in oxygen — not neutralisation.

foundation.common_mistake: When making a salt by adding excess solid to acid — you MUST use EXCESS solid to ensure ALL the acid reacts. If you don't use excess, some acid remains and the final product will be contaminated with acid. Then filter off the excess unreacted solid — the salt is in the filtrate.

chemical-changes/ph-scale.summary: Describe the pH scale, what it measures and how indicators are used.

quiz.q: A solution has a pH of 2. Another has a pH of 4. How do their H⁺ ion concentrations compare?
opts: pH 2 has 100 times more H⁺ than pH 4 — each unit on the log scale is 10×
pH 2 has twice as many H⁺ ions as pH 4
pH 4 has more H⁺ ions — higher pH means more hydrogen
They have the same H⁺ concentration — both are acidic

wrong_explanations.1: The pH scale is LOGARITHMIC — each unit represents a 10× change, not a 2× change. pH 2 vs pH 4 = 10² = 100× difference.
wrong_explanations.2: LOWER pH means MORE H⁺ ions — pH 2 is more acidic than pH 4, not less.
wrong_explanations.3: Both are acidic (below 7) but have very different H⁺ concentrations — pH 2 is much more acidic.

quiz.q: An acid is diluted by adding water. What happens to its pH?
opts: The pH increases towards 7 — dilution reduces H⁺ concentration, making it less acidic
The pH decreases — water is neutral so it makes the acid stronger
The pH stays the same — water doesn't affect the acid
The pH jumps immediately to 7 — water always neutralises acids

wrong_explanations.1: Water does not make acid stronger — adding water DILUTES the acid, reducing H⁺ concentration and raising pH.
wrong_explanations.2: Water does affect the acid — it reduces the concentration of H⁺ ions (dilutes them). The pH INCREASES.
wrong_explanations.3: Unless you add enormous amounts of water, the pH approaches 7 gradually but never quite reaches it — you are diluting, not neutralising.

foundation.common_mistake: Lower pH = MORE ACIDIC = MORE H⁺ ions. Higher pH = MORE ALKALINE = FEWER H⁺ ions (more OH⁻). Students often confuse pH 2 and pH 12 — pH 2 is strongly acidic, pH 12 is strongly alkaline. pH 7 is neutral — pure water. NOT all solutions at pH 7 are water.

quiz.q: A solution has a pH of 2. Another has a pH of 4. How do their H⁺ ion concentrations compare?

wrong_explanations.2: Water does affect the acid — it reduces the concentration of H⁺ ions (dilutes them). The pH INCREASES.
wrong_explanations.3: Unless you add enormous amounts of water, the pH approaches 7 gradually but never quite reaches it — you are diluting, not neutralising.

higher.common_mistake: Lower pH = MORE ACIDIC = MORE H⁺ ions. Higher pH = MORE ALKALINE = FEWER H⁺ ions (more OH⁻). Students often confuse pH 2 and pH 12 — pH 2 is strongly acidic, pH 12 is strongly alkaline. pH 7 is neutral — pure water. NOT all solutions at pH 7 are water.
higher.higher_box: Strong acids fully dissociate (HCl → H⁺ + Cl⁻) — maximum H⁺ at given concentration. Weak acids partially dissociate (CH₃COOH ⇌ CH₃COO⁻ + H⁺) — less H⁺ at same concentration → higher pH. Strong acid and weak acid at same concentration: strong acid has lower pH. Strong ≠ concentrated.

chemical-changes/electrolysis-principles.summary: Describe what electrolysis is, how it works and the key terms used.

quiz.q: Why can solid sodium chloride not be electrolysed, but molten sodium chloride can?
opts: In the solid, ions are fixed in the lattice — they cannot move to carry charge. Molten NaCl has free-moving ions.
Solid NaCl doesn't contain ions — they only form when the compound melts.
Solid NaCl is too hard for electricity to pass through.
Electrolysis only works on liquids — all solids resist decomposition.

wrong_explanations.1: NaCl is always ionic — Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions exist in both solid and molten states. The issue is whether they can MOVE.
wrong_explanations.2: Hardness affects mechanical properties, not ionic conductivity — the issue is ion mobility, not physical hardness.
wrong_explanations.3: Some solids are electrolysed — e.g. electrolysis of a metal anode in electroplating. The issue specifically with NaCl solid is fixed ions.

quiz.q: During electrolysis, where does oxidation take place?
opts: At the anode — negative ions lose electrons here (OIL: Oxidation Is Loss)
At the cathode — positive ions gain electrons here
In the electrolyte — ions are oxidised as they move through the solution
At both electrodes simultaneously

wrong_explanations.1: The CATHODE is where REDUCTION occurs (positive ions GAIN electrons). Remember: RED CAT (Reduction at Cathode).
wrong_explanations.2: Ions move through the solution but are not oxidised or reduced until they reach an electrode.
wrong_explanations.3: Oxidation (at anode) and reduction (at cathode) occur at DIFFERENT electrodes — at the same time, but not at the same place.

foundation.common_mistake: CATHODE is NEGATIVE — positive ions are attracted to it and gain electrons (reduced). ANODE is POSITIVE — negative ions are attracted to it and lose electrons (oxidised). Students often confuse these. Remember: AN OX (Anode Oxidation) and RED CAT (Reduction at Cathode).

chemical-changes/electrolysis-molten.summary: Predict and explain the products of electrolysing molten ionic compounds.

quiz.q: Molten calcium chloride (CaCl₂) is electrolysed. What is produced at the cathode?
opts: Calcium metal — Ca²⁺ ions are reduced at the negative cathode
Chlorine gas — Cl⁻ ions are discharged at the cathode
Calcium chloride — no decomposition occurs at the cathode
Calcium oxide — the calcium combines with oxygen from the air

wrong_explanations.1: Cl⁻ is NEGATIVE — it moves to the POSITIVE ANODE, not the cathode. The cathode attracts positive Ca²⁺ ions.
wrong_explanations.2: Electrolysis DOES decompose the compound — that is its purpose.
wrong_explanations.3: Electrolysis is done in an enclosed container — calcium doesn't react with air at this stage. The product is pure calcium metal.

quiz.q: Why must lead bromide be melted before it can be electrolysed?
opts: In the solid, Pb²⁺ and Br⁻ ions are fixed — they cannot move to carry charge. Melting gives the ions freedom to move.
Melting changes the chemical formula of lead bromide, making electrolysis possible.
The electrodes cannot penetrate a solid — they need a liquid to make contact.
Solid lead bromide doesn't contain ions — they form when it melts.

wrong_explanations.1: Melting does NOT change the chemical formula — PbBr₂ is still PbBr₂ whether solid or molten.
wrong_explanations.2: In a proper setup, electrodes can contact a solid — but the solid still won't conduct electricity because the ions can't move.
wrong_explanations.3: Lead bromide is ionic in BOTH solid and molten states — Na⁺ and Cl⁻ (or Pb²⁺ and Br⁻) ions exist in the solid too. They just can't move.

foundation.common_mistake: In molten ionic compounds, the METAL is always produced at the CATHODE (the negative electrode). This is reduction — metal ions GAIN electrons. The NON-METAL is always produced at the ANODE (positive electrode). This is oxidation — non-metal ions LOSE electrons.

quiz.q: Molten calcium chloride (CaCl₂) is electrolysed. What is produced at the cathode?

Chlorine gas — Cl⁻ ions are discharged at the cathode

Calcium oxide — the calcium combines with oxygen from the air

wrong_explanations.3: Electrolysis is done in an enclosed container — calcium doesn't react with air at this stage. The product is pure calcium metal.

chemical-changes/electrolysis-extraction.summary: Describe how electrolysis is used to extract aluminium and purify copper.

quiz.q: Why does the graphite anode in aluminium extraction need to be replaced regularly?
opts: Oxygen produced at the anode reacts with the hot graphite: C + O₂ → CO₂ — the anode burns away
The anode dissolves into the aluminium oxide melt
The high temperature melts and destroys the graphite
Aluminium deposits on the anode, blocking it

wrong_explanations.1: Graphite does not dissolve ionically — it reacts CHEMICALLY with the oxygen produced at the anode.
wrong_explanations.2: Graphite melts at ~3600°C — well above the operating temperature of ~950°C. Physical melting is not the issue.
wrong_explanations.3: Aluminium is produced at the CATHODE (the graphite lining at the bottom) — it doesn't deposit on the anode.

quiz.q: In copper purification by electrolysis, what happens at the cathode?
opts: Pure copper deposits — Cu²⁺ ions from solution gain electrons and form solid copper
Impure copper dissolves — copper atoms lose electrons and enter solution
Oxygen gas is produced — water is oxidised at the cathode
Gold and silver from the anode deposit at the cathode

wrong_explanations.1: Impure copper DISSOLVING is what happens at the ANODE — Cu → Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻. The cathode is where Cu DEPOSITS.
wrong_explanations.2: Oxygen is produced at anodes when water is oxidised — in copper purification the anode reaction is Cu dissolving, not water oxidation.
wrong_explanations.3: Less reactive metals (Au, Ag) from the anode do NOT dissolve into solution — they fall as anode sludge. Only copper enters solution.

foundation.common_mistake: In copper purification, the IMPURE copper is the ANODE (it dissolves). The PURE copper builds up on the CATHODE. Students often get these the wrong way round. The anions in the electrolyte (SO₄²⁻) do not move to either electrode in copper purification — only copper ions are discharged.

wrong_explanations.3: Aluminium is produced at the CATHODE (the graphite lining at the bottom) — it doesn't deposit on the anode.

wrong_explanations.2: Oxygen is produced at anodes when water is oxidised — in copper purification the anode reaction is Cu dissolving, not water oxidation.

chemical-changes/electrolysis-aqueous.summary: Predict products of electrolysing aqueous solutions and explain why water adds complexity.

quiz.q: Copper sulfate solution is electrolysed with inert electrodes. What is produced at the cathode?
opts: Copper metal — Cu²⁺ ions are below hydrogen in the reactivity series and are preferentially discharged
Hydrogen gas — water provides H⁺ ions which are always discharged at the cathode
Sulfur dioxide — SO₄²⁻ ions are discharged at the cathode
Oxygen — OH⁻ ions from water are discharged at the cathode

wrong_explanations.1: H⁺ ions ARE present — but Cu²⁺ is LESS REACTIVE than H⁺ (copper is below hydrogen in the series), so Cu²⁺ is preferentially discharged.
wrong_explanations.2: SO₄²⁻ is a very stable ion — it is NOT discharged at the cathode or anode under normal conditions.
wrong_explanations.3: O₂ is produced at the ANODE (from OH⁻ oxidation), not the cathode.

quiz.q: Concentrated brine (sodium chloride solution) is electrolysed. What is produced at the anode?
opts: Chlorine gas — high concentration of Cl⁻ ions means they are preferentially discharged over OH⁻
Oxygen gas — OH⁻ ions from water are always discharged at the anode
Sodium metal — Na⁺ ions are discharged at the anode
Hydrogen gas — H⁺ ions from water are discharged at the anode

wrong_explanations.1: O₂ IS produced if the Cl⁻ concentration is LOW — but in CONCENTRATED brine, the high Cl⁻ concentration favours Cl₂ production.
wrong_explanations.2: Na⁺ is a positive ion — it moves to the CATHODE (negative electrode), not the anode.
wrong_explanations.3: H⁺ is discharged at the CATHODE (negative) — positive ions always move to the cathode.

foundation.common_mistake: In aqueous solutions, WATER provides H⁺ and OH⁻ ions that compete with the ions from the dissolved salt. At the cathode: metals above hydrogen → H₂ is produced (not the metal). At the anode: if no halide ions present → O₂ is produced (from OH⁻). Students often predict the wrong product for Na⁺ at the cathode — sodium is so reactive it is NEVER deposited from aqueous solution; hydrogen is produced instead.

energy-changes/exothermic-endothermic.summary: Define exothermic and endothermic reactions, give examples and describe their energy changes.

quiz.q: A hand warmer gets hot when activated. What type of reaction is occurring inside?
opts: Exothermic — energy is released to the surroundings, raising the temperature
Endothermic — energy is absorbed, warming the hand from within
Neither — hand warmers use electrical energy, not chemical reactions
Endothermic — the hand warmer absorbs heat from the hand

wrong_explanations.1: Endothermic reactions take IN heat from the surroundings — they make things colder, not warmer. Hand warmers get hot because they are exothermic.
wrong_explanations.2: Most disposable hand warmers use a chemical reaction (e.g. iron oxidation) — not electricity.
wrong_explanations.3: A hand warmer RELEASES heat to the hand — the reaction inside is exothermic (releases energy).

quiz.q: 100 g of solution is heated in a calorimetry experiment. Temperature rises from 22°C to 30°C. Using c = 4.18 J/g°C, how much energy was released?
opts: 3340 J — temperature change forgotten, used 8.18 instead
41800 J — forgot to use the temperature change

wrong_explanations.1: ΔT = 30 − 22 = 2 — not 8. The temperature change is the difference: 30 − 22 = 8°C.
wrong_explanations.2: Calculation partially correct but wrong ΔT value used — not a standard error in the formula.
wrong_explanations.3: Must use Q = m × c × ΔT = 100 × 4.18 × 8 = 3344 J. Cannot use just mass × specific heat capacity.

foundation.common_mistake: EXOTHERMIC reactions make the surroundings HOTTER (temperature rises). ENDOTHERMIC reactions make the surroundings COLDER (temperature falls). The reaction itself releases or absorbs energy — but the SURROUNDINGS show the opposite effect to what the reaction does. Also: ΔH is NEGATIVE for exothermic (releases energy) — students often think negative means the reaction is 'losing energy' in a bad way — it just means energy is released.

energy-changes/reaction-profiles.summary: Draw and interpret reaction profile diagrams for exothermic and endothermic reactions.

quiz.q: On a reaction profile, the reactants are at 80 kJ and the products are at 50 kJ. What is ΔH?
opts: −30 kJ — products (50) − reactants (80) = −30 kJ — exothermic reaction
+30 kJ — reactants (80) − products (50) = +30 kJ
+130 kJ — adding reactant and product energies
−80 kJ — the reactant energy is lost

wrong_explanations.1: ΔH = products − reactants = 50 − 80 = −30. Positive 30 would mean products are HIGHER than reactants — but here products (50) are lower than reactants (80).
wrong_explanations.2: Adding energies gives a meaningless value — ΔH = products MINUS reactants.
wrong_explanations.3: The reactant energy of 80 kJ is not 'lost' — ΔH shows the NET energy change from reactants to products.

quiz.q: A catalyst is added to a reaction. What changes on the reaction profile?
opts: The activation energy peak is lower — but reactant and product energy levels and ΔH stay the same
The reactant energy level increases — they become more energetic
Both the activation energy and ΔH decrease
The product energy level increases — products formed are higher energy

wrong_explanations.1: Catalysts do not give reactants more energy — they provide an ALTERNATIVE PATHWAY with a lower energy barrier.
wrong_explanations.2: ΔH (the overall energy change) is NOT changed by a catalyst — only the activation energy peak is lowered.
wrong_explanations.3: Products are the same compounds regardless of whether a catalyst is used — their energy level doesn't change.

foundation.common_mistake: The activation energy is measured from the REACTANT level to the PEAK — NOT from the reactant level to the product level. The overall energy change (ΔH) is from reactants to PRODUCTS. These are two different measurements on the same diagram. Students often confuse them.

rates-equilibrium/calculating-rates.summary: Define rate of reaction and calculate it from experimental data.

quiz.q: A reaction produces 120 cm³ of gas in 4 minutes. What is the mean rate of reaction in cm³/s?
opts: 30 cm³/s — rate = 120 ÷ 4
480 cm³/s — rate = 120 × 4
2 cm³/s — rate = 120 ÷ 60

wrong_explanations.1: 4 minutes = 4 minutes — not converted to seconds. Rate in cm³/s requires time in seconds: 4 × 60 = 240 s.
wrong_explanations.2: This multiplies instead of divides.
wrong_explanations.3: This divides by 60 (seconds in a minute) but should divide by total seconds = 4 × 60 = 240.

quiz.q: On a graph of gas volume vs time, what does a steeper gradient indicate?
opts: A faster rate of reaction — more product is being formed per unit time
A slower rate — steeper means the reaction is taking longer
The reaction is complete — no more gas is produced
More reactant has been added — the amount of gas increases

wrong_explanations.1: Steeper gradient = larger volume change per unit time = FASTER, not slower rate.
wrong_explanations.2: A FLAT (horizontal) gradient means the reaction is complete — no more gas being produced.
wrong_explanations.3: Adding more reactant might increase total gas produced — but gradient (steepness) represents RATE, not quantity.

foundation.common_mistake: The rate DECREASES over time in a closed system as REACTANTS ARE USED UP — fewer reactant particles mean fewer collisions. The graph flattens out when the reaction is complete (all reactants used). A steeper initial gradient = faster initial rate.

rates-equilibrium/factors-affecting-rate.summary: Describe and explain how temperature, concentration, surface area and pressure affect reaction rate.

quiz.q: Why does increasing temperature increase reaction rate more than just increasing the frequency of collisions?
opts: A greater proportion of particles now have energy ≥ the activation energy — so more collisions are successful
Higher temperature makes particles larger — they collide more easily
Higher temperature dissolves more of the solid reactants — increasing concentration
Temperature has no extra effect — it only increases collision frequency

wrong_explanations.1: Particles don't change size with temperature — they move faster.
wrong_explanations.2: This might apply in some specific cases but is not the general reason why temperature increases rate — the key is the energy distribution.
wrong_explanations.3: Temperature does more than increase frequency — it shifts the energy distribution so MORE particles exceed the activation energy threshold.

quiz.q: Marble chips (CaCO₃) react with HCl. The experiment is repeated with the same mass of powdered marble. How does the rate change?
opts: Rate increases — powder has much greater surface area, more particle collisions per unit time
Rate stays the same — same mass of marble is used
Rate decreases — powder dissolves faster so there are fewer particles overall
Rate increases because powder has less activation energy than chips

wrong_explanations.1: Same MASS does not mean same rate — rate depends on SURFACE AREA. Powder has enormously more surface area than chips.
wrong_explanations.2: Powder dissolving faster IS the result of faster rate — the mechanism is increased surface area for collisions.
wrong_explanations.3: Activation energy is a property of the reaction, not the physical form of the reactant. Powder and chips have the same activation energy.

foundation.common_mistake: All four factors (temperature, concentration, surface area, pressure for gases) increase rate by increasing the NUMBER OF SUCCESSFUL COLLISIONS. Temperature is special — it also increases the PROPORTION of particles with sufficient energy (≥ activation energy), making it particularly effective. Always explain in terms of collisions.

quiz.q: Why does increasing temperature increase reaction rate more than just increasing the frequency of collisions?
opts: A greater proportion of particles now have energy ≥ the activation energy — so more collisions are successful
Higher temperature makes particles larger — they collide more easily
Higher temperature dissolves more of the solid reactants — increasing concentration
Temperature has no extra effect — it only increases collision frequency

wrong_explanations.1: Particles don't change size with temperature — they move faster.
wrong_explanations.2: This might apply in some specific cases but is not the general reason why temperature increases rate — the key is the energy distribution.
wrong_explanations.3: Temperature does more than increase frequency — it shifts the energy distribution so MORE particles exceed the activation energy threshold.

rates-equilibrium/collision-theory.summary: Explain collision theory and how activation energy determines whether a reaction occurs.

quiz.q: Why do most collisions between reactant particles NOT result in a reaction?
opts: Most collisions don't have sufficient energy — the particles must have energy ≥ the activation energy
Most collisions are too fast — particles bounce off before bonds can form
Most collisions involve too many particles — only two particles can react at once
Most collisions happen between the same type of particle — same reactant colliding with itself

wrong_explanations.1: Collision speed doesn't determine success directly — it's whether the ENERGY meets the activation energy threshold.
wrong_explanations.2: Many reactions are bimolecular (two particles) but this isn't why most collisions fail — energy below Ea is the main reason.
wrong_explanations.3: Particles of the same type do collide, but in a mixture, most collisions do involve different reactant species — the issue is energy, not species.

quiz.q: A reaction has a very high activation energy. What does this predict about the reaction?
opts: The reaction will be slow — only a small proportion of collisions will have enough energy to succeed
The reaction will be fast — high activation energy means particles are very energetic
The reaction will be exothermic — high activation energy means lots of energy released
The reaction will not occur at all — nothing can overcome high activation energy

wrong_explanations.1: High activation energy doesn't mean particles are energetic — it means the THRESHOLD is high, so few particles exceed it.
wrong_explanations.2: Activation energy and ΔH are independent — a reaction can have high Ea and still be endothermic, or low Ea and highly exothermic.
wrong_explanations.3: Reactions with high Ea can still occur — just slowly. Temperature can always provide the energy. Explosives often have high Ea but react violently once started.

foundation.common_mistake: Increasing concentration or surface area increases the FREQUENCY of collisions but does NOT change the activation energy. Only a CATALYST changes the activation energy. Temperature increases both frequency AND the proportion above activation energy. These are different mechanisms.

Explain why a 10°C rise approximately doubles rate — the fraction of particles above Ea at least doubles. Distinguish between increased collision frequency and increased proportion of successful collisions.

rates-equilibrium/catalysts.summary: Describe how catalysts work, give examples and explain their industrial importance.

quiz.q: A catalyst is added to a reaction. Which of the following is true?
opts: The rate increases because the activation energy is lowered — the products and ΔH are unchanged
The products change — the catalyst reacts with some reactants to form new products
The overall energy change (ΔH) decreases — less energy is released or absorbed
The catalyst is used up proportionally to the amount of reactant that reacts

wrong_explanations.1: A catalyst provides an ALTERNATIVE PATHWAY to the same products — it does not change what products form.
wrong_explanations.2: ΔH is the difference in energy between reactants and products — the catalyst doesn't change what the reactants or products ARE, so ΔH stays the same.
wrong_explanations.3: A catalyst is NOT consumed in the reaction — by definition it is regenerated. A substance that is used up is a REACTANT, not a catalyst.

quiz.q: Why can industrial processes use lower temperatures when a catalyst is present?
opts: The catalyst lowers activation energy — more particles have sufficient energy at lower temperature — fast enough rate without needing extra heat
Catalysts generate heat themselves — reducing the need for external heating
Lower temperatures produce better quality products when catalysts are used
Catalysts compress the reactant gases — raising pressure reduces the need for temperature

wrong_explanations.1: Catalysts don't generate heat — they lower the energy barrier. If they released heat, they would be acting as reactants.
wrong_explanations.2: Product quality is not directly related to catalyst presence — the reason for lower temperature is purely kinetic (enough particles above the new lower Ea).
wrong_explanations.3: Catalysts don't affect the pressure of reactant gases.

foundation.common_mistake: A catalyst is NOT used up — it is the same at the END of the reaction as at the START. It does NOT change the products or the overall energy change (ΔH). It ONLY provides an alternative pathway with LOWER ACTIVATION ENERGY — making the reaction faster, not different.

Catalyst poisoning: impurities block active sites permanently. Enzymes as biological catalysts: highly specific, optimum temperature and pH, denature above ~40°C.

rates-equilibrium/reversible-reactions-e.summary: Describe reversible reactions and explain what is meant by dynamic equilibrium.

quiz.q: At dynamic equilibrium, which of the following is true?
opts: The forward and reverse reactions are still occurring at equal rates — concentrations of all species are constant
The reaction has stopped — no more forward or reverse reaction takes place
Concentrations of reactants and products are equal
Only the forward reaction occurs — the reverse reaction has stopped

wrong_explanations.1: 'Dynamic' means BOTH reactions are still occurring — it is NOT static. 'Equilibrium' means rates are balanced, not that the reaction stopped.
wrong_explanations.2: Concentrations are CONSTANT but NOT necessarily equal. The ratio of products to reactants can be anything — it depends on the specific reaction and conditions.
wrong_explanations.3: If only the forward reaction occurred, it would be a one-way reaction — equilibrium requires BOTH directions continuing at equal rates.

quiz.q: The forward reaction A ⇌ B releases 50 kJ/mol. How much energy does the reverse reaction B → A involve?
opts: Absorbs 50 kJ/mol — the reverse reaction is endothermic with the same energy magnitude
Releases 50 kJ/mol — the reverse reaction also releases the same energy
Absorbs 100 kJ/mol — double the forward reaction energy
Zero — the reverse reaction has no energy change

wrong_explanations.1: If the reverse also released energy, energy would be created from nothing — violating conservation of energy.
wrong_explanations.2: The energy is the SAME magnitude (50 kJ/mol) but the direction is reversed — forward exothermic means reverse endothermic.
wrong_explanations.3: Energy changes in reversible reactions are always equal and opposite — the reverse cannot have zero energy change if the forward has a non-zero change.

foundation.common_mistake: At equilibrium, the reaction has NOT stopped — it is DYNAMIC. Both forward and reverse reactions continue at equal rates. Concentrations are CONSTANT but not necessarily EQUAL. Students often say 'equilibrium means equal concentrations' — this is wrong. It means equal RATES.

wrong_explanations.3: If only the forward reaction occurred, it would be a one-way reaction — equilibrium requires BOTH directions continuing at equal rates.

wrong_explanations.3: Energy changes in reversible reactions are always equal and opposite — the reverse cannot have zero energy change if the forward has a non-zero change.

higher.higher_box: Qualitative Le Chatelier's principle: concentration increase shifts equilibrium to use up added species. Temperature increase shifts in endothermic direction. Pressure increase shifts to side with fewer moles of gas. Catalyst speeds up reaching equilibrium but doesn't change the position.

organic/crude-oil-hydrocarbons.summary: Describe crude oil as a mixture of hydrocarbons and the structure of alkanes.

quiz.q: An alkane has 5 carbon atoms. What is its molecular formula?
opts: C₅H₁₂ — using CₙH₂ₙ₊₂: n=5, 2(5)+2 = 12 hydrogens
C₅H₁₀ — using CₙH₂ₙ — the alkene formula
C₅H₈ — fewer than expected
C₅H₅ — half the expected hydrogens

wrong_explanations.1: CₙH₂ₙ is the formula for ALKENES (unsaturated) — alkanes use CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. For n=5: 2(5)+2=12 hydrogens.
wrong_explanations.2: C₅H₈ doesn't fit either alkane or alkene formula — may be an alkyne or cyclic compound.
wrong_explanations.3: C₅H₅ is not a simple alkane or alkene — alkanes always have 2n+2 hydrogens.

quiz.q: Why is incomplete combustion of fuels more dangerous than complete combustion?
opts: Carbon monoxide is produced — a toxic, colourless, odourless gas that prevents oxygen transport in blood
Incomplete combustion releases more energy — causing overheating
Water vapour produced from incomplete combustion causes corrosion
Incomplete combustion only produces CO₂ which is more harmful than CO

wrong_explanations.1: Incomplete combustion releases LESS energy per gram — not more. It is less efficient.
wrong_explanations.2: Water vapour is produced in BOTH complete and incomplete combustion — it is not specific to incomplete.
wrong_explanations.3: CO₂ contributes to climate change but is not immediately toxic like CO. Incomplete combustion produces CO (toxic) and particulates.

foundation.common_mistake: Alkanes are SATURATED — they have only SINGLE bonds and do NOT decolourise bromine water. ALKENES are UNSATURATED (have a C=C double bond) and DO decolourise bromine water. The bromine water test distinguishes saturated (no change) from unsaturated (decolourises).

higher.higher_box: Write and balance combustion equations for any alkane. Calculate molar quantities from combustion equations. Evaluate environmental impact of fossil fuels: CO₂ → climate change; CO and particulates → local health effects. Compare energy content of different fuels.

organic/fractional-distillation.summary: Describe how crude oil is separated into fractions and the properties of each fraction.

quiz.q: Where in the fractionating column does petrol collect compared to diesel?
opts: Petrol collects higher up — it has shorter chains and a lower boiling point than diesel
Petrol collects lower down — it is more dense than diesel
Both collect at the same level — they have similar chain lengths
Petrol collects at the very bottom with bitumen — it is a liquid at room temperature

wrong_explanations.1: Density does affect placement somewhat, but the key factor is BOILING POINT. Petrol has lower BP → condenses higher up.
wrong_explanations.2: Petrol (C₅–C₁₀) and diesel (C₁₅–C₂₅) have very different chain lengths — they condense at different levels.
wrong_explanations.3: Bitumen is at the BOTTOM because it doesn't vaporise — it has the longest chains and highest boiling point. Petrol is much lighter and collects high up the column.

quiz.q: Why does bitumen have a much higher boiling point than petrol?
opts: Bitumen molecules have much longer carbon chains — stronger intermolecular forces require more energy to overcome
Bitumen contains more oxygen atoms, making it heavier
Bitumen is denser so it needs more energy to change state
Petrol has double bonds that make it easier to boil

wrong_explanations.1: Crude oil fractions are HYDROCARBONS — they contain only carbon and hydrogen. Oxygen is not present.
wrong_explanations.2: Density and boiling point are related but the cause of both is CHAIN LENGTH and intermolecular forces — not density itself.
wrong_explanations.3: Alkanes (including petrol fractions) have only SINGLE bonds — no double bonds. Double bonds are found in alkenes.

foundation.common_mistake: Fractions with SHORTER chains have LOWER boiling points and collect at the TOP of the fractionating column (cooler). Fractions with LONGER chains have HIGHER boiling points and collect at the BOTTOM (hotter). Students sometimes get this backwards.

higher.higher_box: Explain boiling point trend using London dispersion forces: longer chains → larger electron clouds → greater temporary dipoles → stronger forces → higher BP. Evaluate environmental and economic issues of fossil fuel reliance. Consider development of biofuels and alternatives.

organic/properties-of-hydrocarbons.summary: Explain how chain length affects the physical and chemical properties of hydrocarbons.

quiz.q: Why is petrol (C₅–C₁₀) more flammable than diesel (C₁₅–C₂₅)?
opts: Petrol has shorter chains — more volatile, evaporates easily at room temperature, produces flammable vapour readily
Petrol contains more hydrogen — hydrogen is more flammable than carbon
Diesel is more viscous — it can't flow to the flame and doesn't ignite
Petrol has double bonds — alkenes are more reactive and flammable than alkanes

wrong_explanations.1: Both petrol and diesel are mainly hydrocarbons with similar C:H ratios — hydrogen content is not the reason for different flammability.
wrong_explanations.2: Viscosity affects flow but the primary reason for different flammability is VOLATILITY — petrol produces more vapour at room temperature.
wrong_explanations.3: Petrol fractions are alkanes (saturated, single bonds). Alkenes do have double bonds but crude oil fractions are not alkenes — they are alkanes.

quiz.q: Propane (C₃H₈) is a gas at room temperature but hexadecane (C₁₆H₃₄) is a solid. Why?
opts: Hexadecane has much longer chains — stronger intermolecular forces mean its boiling point is far above room temperature
Hexadecane contains more hydrogen — hydrogen atoms make it solid
Propane is lighter, so it exists as a gas by buoyancy
Hexadecane has ionic bonds that hold it as a solid

wrong_explanations.1: Hydrogen content doesn't determine physical state — chain length and the resulting intermolecular forces determine boiling point.
wrong_explanations.2: Buoyancy explains why lighter gases rise but doesn't explain why propane is a GAS rather than a liquid or solid at room temperature — that's about intermolecular forces.
wrong_explanations.3: Both are hydrocarbons with only covalent bonds — no ionic bonds. The difference in state is entirely due to intermolecular force strength.

foundation.common_mistake: Shorter chain hydrocarbons have LOWER boiling points and are MORE flammable/volatile. Longer chains are LESS flammable and MORE viscous. Students often get flammability and chain length confused — think of it this way: petrol (short chains) is much more flammable than diesel (longer chains).

organic/cracking-alkenes.summary: Describe cracking, explain why it is necessary and describe the structure and reactions of alkenes.

quiz.q: Why is cracking important for the petrochemical industry?
opts: It converts long-chain fractions (high supply, low demand) into short-chain fuels and alkenes (high demand) for petrol and plastics
It purifies crude oil by removing impurities like sulfur compounds
It increases the boiling point of fractions so they can be stored more safely
It converts alkenes back into alkanes for more stable storage

wrong_explanations.1: Removing sulfur (desulfurisation) is a separate process — cracking is specifically about chain length reduction.
wrong_explanations.2: Cracking produces SHORTER chains with LOWER boiling points — it decreases boiling point, not increases it.
wrong_explanations.3: Cracking produces alkenes FROM alkanes — it converts alkanes to alkenes, not the other way round.

quiz.q: A student adds bromine water to two hydrocarbons. With compound A, the bromine water decolourises. With compound B, there is no change. What does this show?
opts: Compound A is an alkene (unsaturated — C=C reacts with Br₂). Compound B is an alkane (saturated — no reaction)
Compound A is an alkane. Compound B is an alkene.
Both A and B are alkenes — both should react with bromine water
The result shows compound A is more flammable than compound B

wrong_explanations.1: It is the ALKENE (unsaturated, C=C) that decolourises bromine water — not the alkane.
wrong_explanations.2: Alkanes do NOT decolourise bromine water — so if both reacted, that would suggest both are alkenes.
wrong_explanations.3: Bromine water test shows saturation/unsaturation — not flammability.

foundation.common_mistake: Cracking always produces at least one ALKENE — alkenes are ALWAYS one of the products. You cannot crack a hydrocarbon to produce only alkanes. Also: alkenes DECOLOURISE bromine water (C=C reacts with Br₂). Alkanes do NOT decolourise bromine water.
